text: Your company has decided to make a major revision of their API in order to create better experiences for their developers. They need to keep the old version of the API available and deployable, while allowing new customers and testers to try out the new API. They want to keep the same SSL and DNS records in place to serve both APIs. What should they do?

options.text: Configure a new load balancer for the new version of the API
Reconfigure old clients to use a new endpoint for the new API
Have the old API forward traffic to the new API based on the path
Use separate backend pools for each API path behind the load balancer

optionExplanations.A: Adding a new load balancer would require new IP addresses, SSL certificates, and DNS records, which directly violates the requirement to keep the same SSL and DNS records in place for both API versions.
optionExplanations.B: Forcing old clients to migrate to a new endpoint creates disruption for existing customers and contradicts the goal of keeping the old API available and unchanged for current users.
optionExplanations.C: Routing logic should live at the load balancer/edge layer, not be embedded in the old API; having the old API forward to the new API adds unnecessary coupling, latency, and operational complexity.
optionExplanations.D: Using a single HTTP(S) load balancer with URL path-based routing to separate backend services (instance groups or NEGs) for the old and new API versions allows both versions to be served under the same SSL certificate and DNS name, while keeping them independently deployable, which satisfies all stated requirements.

text: Your company plans to migrate a multi-petabyte data set to the cloud. The data set must be available 24hrs a day. Your business analysts have experience only with using a SQL interface. How should you store the data to optimize it for ease of analysis?

options.text: Load data into Google BigQuery
Insert data into Google Cloud SQL
Put flat files into Google Cloud Storage
Stream data into Google Cloud Datastore

optionExplanations.A: BigQuery is a fully managed, serverless petabyte-scale data warehouse with a native SQL interface (standard SQL), high availability (data is automatically replicated across zones), and separation of storage and compute, making it ideal for business analysts who only know SQL and need 24/7 access to a multi-petabyte dataset.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud SQL is a managed relational database (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server) designed for transactional workloads at the GB-to-low-TB scale; it does not scale to multi-petabyte analytical datasets and would be cost-prohibitive and operationally impractical at this size.
optionExplanations.C: Storing flat files in Cloud Storage provides durable, available object storage, but it has no native SQL query engine, so business analysts without programming/ETL skills could not directly query the data.
optionExplanations.D: Cloud Datastore (now Firestore in Datastore mode) is a NoSQL document database intended for transactional application data with key-based lookups, not for ad hoc SQL-based analytics over petabyte-scale datasets.

text: The operations manager asks you for a list of recommended practices that she should consider when migrating a J2EE application to the cloud. Which three practices should you recommend? (Choose three.)

options.text: Port the application code to run on Google App Engine
Integrate Cloud Dataflow into the application to capture real-time metrics
Instrument the application with a monitoring tool like Stackdriver Debugger
Select an automation framework to reliably provision the cloud infrastructure
Deploy a continuous integration tool with automated testing in a staging environment
Migrate from MySQL to a managed NoSQL database like Google Cloud Datastore or Bigtable

optionExplanations.A: Re-platforming the J2EE application to run on App Engine (e.g., App Engine flexible environment, which supports Java/J2EE-style applications) reduces operational overhead by leveraging a managed runtime and is a recommended modernization step during cloud migration.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud Dataflow is a stream/batch data processing service for ETL and analytics pipelines; integrating it into the application solely to capture real-time metrics is not a core migration best practice and is not one of the recommended steps for this scenario.
optionExplanations.C: Instrumenting the application with a monitoring/debugging tool such as Cloud Monitoring/Cloud Debugger (formerly part of Stackdriver) is good practice generally, but it is not one of the three practices specifically called out as essential for this migration scenario compared to automation and CI/CD.
optionExplanations.D: Selecting an automation framework (such as Terraform, Deployment Manager, or similar Infrastructure-as-Code tooling) to provision cloud infrastructure ensures repeatable, consistent environments and is a foundational best practice for any cloud migration.
optionExplanations.E: Deploying a continuous integration/continuous delivery pipeline with automated testing in a staging environment reduces the risk of defects reaching production and is a standard recommended practice when modernizing application delivery during a cloud migration.
optionExplanations.F: Migrating from MySQL to a NoSQL database like Datastore or Bigtable is a significant architectural change that may not be necessary or appropriate for a J2EE application migration and is not a generally recommended 'lift and improve' practice without a specific data access pattern justification.

text: A news feed web service has the following code running on Google App Engine. During peak load, users report that they can see news articles they already viewed. What is the most likely cause of this problem?

options.text: The session variable is local to just a single instance
The session variable is being overwritten in Cloud Datastore
The URL of the API needs to be modified to prevent caching
The HTTP Expires header needs to be set to -1 stop caching

optionExplanations.A: When App Engine autoscaling spins up multiple instances, an in-memory/local session variable stored on a single instance is not shared across instances; under peak load, requests get routed to different instances that each have their own (stale or empty) view of which articles a user has seen, causing already-viewed articles to reappear.
optionExplanations.B: If the session state were properly stored in a shared backend like Cloud Datastore and simply being overwritten, the symptom would more likely be inconsistent or lost updates across all load levels, not specifically a peak-load issue tied to instance scaling, making local in-memory state the more likely root cause.
optionExplanations.C: The URL of the API is unrelated to per-user session tracking of viewed articles; modifying it would not address the underlying state management problem causing duplicate article views.
optionExplanations.D: Setting the HTTP Expires header affects client/browser or CDN caching of HTTP responses, not server-side session state that tracks which articles a particular user has already seen, so it would not resolve this issue.

text: An application development team believes their current logging tool will not meet their needs for their new cloud-based product. They want a better tool to capture errors and help them analyze their historical log data. You want to help them find a solution that meets their needs. What should you do?

options.text: Direct them to download and install the Google StackDriver logging agent
Send them a list of online resources about logging best practices
Help them define their requirements and assess viable logging tools
Help them upgrade their current tool to take advantage of any new features

optionExplanations.A: Jumping straight to installing the Cloud Logging (Stackdriver) agent skips the necessary step of understanding the team's actual requirements and may not be the right tool if their needs include capabilities Cloud Logging doesn't provide.
optionExplanations.B: Simply pointing the team to generic online resources is not a hands-on, consultative approach and does not directly help them evaluate whether a specific tool meets their concrete needs.
optionExplanations.C: As a cloud architect, the correct first step is to work with the team to clearly define their functional and non-functional requirements (error capture, historical analysis, retention, integrations) and then evaluate viable tools (such as Cloud Logging/Cloud Monitoring or third-party solutions) against those requirements before recommending a solution.
optionExplanations.D: Helping them upgrade their current tool assumes the existing tool is fundamentally adequate, which contradicts the premise that the team believes their current tool will not meet their new needs; this skips proper requirements analysis.

text: You need to reduce the number of unplanned rollbacks of erroneous production deployments in your company's web hosting platform. Improvement to the QA/ Test processes accomplished an 80% reduction. Which additional two approaches can you take to further reduce the rollbacks? (Choose two.)

options.text: Introduce a green-blue deployment model
Replace the QA environment with canary releases
Fragment the monolithic platform into microservices
Reduce the platform's dependency on relational database systems
Replace the platform's relational database systems with a NoSQL database

optionExplanations.A: A blue-green deployment model lets you run the new (green) version alongside the existing (blue) production version and switch traffic over only once the new version is verified, allowing instant rollback by redirecting traffic back to blue, which directly reduces erroneous-deployment rollback incidents.
optionExplanations.B: Replacing the QA environment entirely with canary releases removes a pre-production validation stage and instead pushes potentially untested code to live users, which increases (rather than decreases) the risk of erroneous production deployments.
optionExplanations.C: Breaking the monolith into microservices allows each service to be deployed, tested, and rolled back independently and in smaller increments, reducing the blast radius and likelihood of a faulty deployment causing a full rollback.
optionExplanations.D: Reducing dependency on relational databases is an architectural change unrelated to deployment rollback frequency and does not directly address the root causes of erroneous production deployments.
optionExplanations.E: Replacing relational databases with NoSQL is a major data-layer architecture change that introduces its own risks and does not specifically target the deployment/rollback process described in the question.

text: To reduce costs, the Director of Engineering has required all developers to move their development infrastructure resources from on-premises virtual machines (VMs) to Google Cloud Platform. These resources go through multiple start/stop events during the day and require state to persist. You have been asked to design the process of running a development environment in Google Cloud while providing cost visibility to the finance department. Which two steps should you take? (Choose two.)

options.text: Use the - -no-auto-delete flag on all persistent disks and stop the VM
Use the - -auto-delete flag on all persistent disks and terminate the VM
Apply VM CPU utilization label and include it in the BigQuery billing export
Use Google BigQuery billing export and labels to associate cost to groups
Store all state into local SSD, snapshot the persistent disks, and terminate the VM
Store all state in Google Cloud Storage, snapshot the persistent disks, and terminate the VM

optionExplanations.A: Using the --no-auto-delete flag preserves the persistent disk (and its state) when the VM is stopped, so developers can stop instances during off hours (saving compute cost) while retaining their disk data for when they resume work, satisfying the requirement to persist state across start/stop cycles.
optionExplanations.B: The --auto-delete flag would delete the persistent disk when the VM is terminated, destroying the developer's state/data, which directly conflicts with the requirement that state must persist across start/stop events.
optionExplanations.C: Labeling based on CPU utilization is not a standard billing export dimension, and CPU utilization is a performance metric, not a cost-allocation mechanism; it would not provide meaningful cost visibility by team/project in BigQuery billing exports.
optionExplanations.D: Exporting billing data to BigQuery and applying resource labels (e.g., team, project, environment) allows the finance department to query and attribute costs to specific groups, directly satisfying the cost visibility requirement.
optionExplanations.E: Local SSD is ephemeral and its data is lost when a VM is stopped or terminated, so it cannot be relied upon to persist state across start/stop cycles, making this an unsuitable approach.
optionExplanations.F: While storing state in Cloud Storage and snapshotting disks before terminating could preserve data, this approach is far more operationally heavy (requires re-provisioning a new VM, restoring from snapshot, and re-uploading state every cycle) compared to simply stopping a VM with --no-auto-delete, making it unnecessarily complex for frequent intra-day start/stop cycles.

text: Your company wants to track whether someone is present in a meeting room reserved for a scheduled meeting. There are 1000 meeting rooms across 5 offices on 3 continents. Each room is equipped with a motion sensor that reports its status every second. The data from the motion detector includes only a sensor ID and several different discrete items of information. Analysts will use this data, together with information about account owners and office locations. Which database type should you use?

options.text: Flat file
NoSQL
Relational
Blobstore

optionExplanations.A: Flat files are not optimized for high-velocity, semi-structured time-series sensor data with frequent writes and would make it cumbersome to query and join with account/location metadata for analysis.
optionExplanations.B: A NoSQL database (such as Cloud Bigtable or Firestore) is well-suited to ingest high-volume, high-velocity sensor data with a flexible schema (sensor ID plus varying discrete attributes) at scale across thousands of devices reporting every second, and can be joined/enriched with account and location data during analysis.
optionExplanations.C: While a relational database could technically store this data, the scale (1000 sensors x 5 offices x continuous per-second writes) and semi-structured nature of the sensor payload make a rigid relational schema less efficient and harder to scale than a NoSQL solution.
optionExplanations.D: Blobstore (an older App Engine API for storing large binary objects like files and images) is designed for unstructured binary data, not for structured/semi-structured time-series sensor readings that need to be queried by sensor ID and time.

text: You set up an autoscaling instance group to serve web traffic for an upcoming launch. After configuring the instance group as a backend service to an HTTP(S) load balancer, you notice that virtual machine (VM) instances are being terminated and re-launched every minute. The instances do not have a public IP address. You have verified the appropriate web response is coming from each instance using the curl command. You want to ensure the backend is configured correctly. What should you do?

options.text: Ensure that a firewall rules exists to allow source traffic on HTTP/HTTPS to reach the load balancer.
Assign a public IP to each instance and configure a firewall rule to allow the load balancer to reach the instance public IP.
Ensure that a firewall rule exists to allow load balancer health checks to reach the instances in the instance group.
Create a tag on each instance with the name of the load balancer. Configure a firewall rule with the name of the load balancer as the source and the instance tag as the destination.

optionExplanations.A: Allowing inbound traffic to the load balancer's external IP is necessary for client traffic, but it does not affect whether the backend instances themselves are reachable by the load balancer's health checker, so it does not explain instances being marked unhealthy and recycled.
optionExplanations.B: Assigning public IPs to backend instances is unnecessary and against best practice for instances behind an HTTP(S) load balancer; the load balancer communicates with backends over Google's internal network using the instances' internal IPs, so this would not fix the health check issue and adds unnecessary attack surface.
optionExplanations.C: Google Cloud HTTP(S) load balancer health checks originate from specific Google IP ranges (130.211.0.0/22 and 35.191.0.0/16); if no firewall rule allows traffic from these ranges to the instances, health checks fail, the instances are marked unhealthy, and the managed instance group's autohealing terminates and relaunches them repeatedly, exactly matching the symptoms described.
optionExplanations.D: While using network tags and firewall rules referencing the load balancer's name as a source is a plausible-sounding configuration, firewall rules cannot use a load balancer's name as a 'source' identifier; the actual fix is allowing the health-check IP ranges, making this option technically incorrect.

text: You write a Python script to connect to Google BigQuery from a Google Compute Engine virtual machine. The script is printing errors that it cannot connect to BigQuery. What should you do to fix the script?

options.text: Install the latest BigQuery API client library for Python
Run your script on a new virtual machine with the BigQuery access scope enabled
Create a new service account with BigQuery access and execute your script with that user
Install the bq component for gcloud with the command gcloud components install bq.

optionExplanations.A: While having the BigQuery client library installed is necessary for the script to function, a missing library typically produces an import/module error rather than a connection/authorization error, so this is not the most likely fix for an authentication-related connection failure.
optionExplanations.B: Compute Engine VMs use the attached service account's access scopes to determine which Google Cloud APIs the VM's default credentials can call; if the BigQuery API access scope was not enabled when the VM was created, API calls to BigQuery will fail with authorization errors, and the fix is to recreate the VM with the appropriate access scope (or use a service account key/ADC with proper IAM roles).
optionExplanations.C: Creating a new service account with BigQuery access could work, but the simplest and most direct fix when running on a GCE VM is to ensure the VM's existing service account has the correct API access scopes enabled, rather than provisioning an entirely new account and switching the execution context.
optionExplanations.D: The bq command-line tool is a separate CLI utility for interacting with BigQuery from the command line; installing it has no effect on a Python script's ability to authenticate to the BigQuery API using client libraries.

text: Your customer is moving an existing corporate application to Google Cloud Platform from an on-premises data center. The business owners require minimal user disruption. There are strict security team requirements for storing passwords. What authentication strategy should they use?

options.text: Use G Suite Password Sync to replicate passwords into Google
Federate authentication via SAML 2.0 to the existing Identity Provider
Provision users in Google using the Google Cloud Directory Sync tool
Ask users to set their Google password to match their corporate password

optionExplanations.A: G Suite Password Sync (GSPS) synchronizes password hashes from Active Directory into Google so users can use the same corporate credentials to sign into Google services, but it actually copies password hash data into Google's systems, which may not satisfy a strict security requirement that passwords never be stored outside the existing identity provider.
optionExplanations.B: SAML 2.0 federation could satisfy security requirements by keeping passwords solely with the existing IdP, but it is more typically used for browser-based SSO to Google Workspace/Cloud Identity rather than as the described provisioning approach, and the question's correct answer focuses on directory synchronization without password storage.
optionExplanations.C: Google Cloud Directory Sync (GCDS) synchronizes user, group, and organizational unit data from an existing LDAP/Active Directory into Google Cloud Identity without transferring or storing passwords in Google's systems, satisfying both the strict password-storage security requirement and minimizing user disruption since accounts are automatically provisioned.
optionExplanations.D: Asking users to manually set a new Google password to match their corporate password creates user friction (disruption), introduces a second password store that must be kept in sync manually, and conflicts with strict requirements around centralized password management.

text: Your company has successfully migrated to the cloud and wants to analyze their data stream to optimize operations. They do not have any existing code for this analysis, so they are exploring all their options. These options include a mix of batch and stream processing, as they are running some hourly jobs and live- processing some data as it comes in. Which technology should they use for this?

options.text: Google Cloud Dataproc
Google Cloud Dataflow
Google Container Engine with Bigtable
Google Compute Engine with Google BigQuery

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Dataproc is primarily designed for running Apache Spark and Hadoop ecosystem jobs (batch-oriented cluster processing); while it can do some streaming via Spark Streaming, it is not the best fit for a scenario requiring a unified batch and stream processing model with no existing code investment.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud Dataflow, based on Apache Beam, provides a unified programming model for both batch and streaming data processing, is fully managed/serverless (autoscaling, no cluster management), and is well suited for a company with no existing code that needs to handle a mix of hourly batch jobs and real-time stream processing.
optionExplanations.C: Running custom processing logic on GKE with Bigtable as storage would require building and maintaining significant custom code and infrastructure, which does not align with the goal of minimal new code and operational simplicity.
optionExplanations.D: Compute Engine with BigQuery would require building custom batch/streaming pipelines on self-managed VMs, which involves significant operational overhead and custom development compared to a managed unified processing service like Dataflow.

text: Your customer is receiving reports that their recently updated Google App Engine application is taking approximately 30 seconds to load for some of their users. This behavior was not reported before the update. What strategy should you take?

options.text: Work with your ISP to diagnose the problem
Open a support ticket to ask for network capture and flow data to diagnose the problem, then roll back your application
Roll back to an earlier known good release initially, then use Stackdriver Trace and Logging to diagnose the problem in a development/test/staging environment
Roll back to an earlier known good release, then push the release again at a quieter period to investigate. Then use Stackdriver Trace and Logging to diagnose the problem

optionExplanations.A: The issue arose immediately after an application update, strongly suggesting the application code itself is the cause rather than the customer's ISP, so working with the ISP would not address the root cause.
optionExplanations.B: Opening a support ticket for network capture data focuses on infrastructure/network-level diagnostics, which is unlikely to be the cause given the timing correlates with an application code update; this also delays mitigation compared to immediately rolling back.
optionExplanations.C: Immediately rolling back to the last known-good release restores service for affected users (minimizing customer impact), and then using Stackdriver Trace (to analyze request latency breakdowns) and Stackdriver Logging in a non-production development/test/staging environment allows the team to safely diagnose the performance regression without re-exposing users to the bug.
optionExplanations.D: Re-deploying the problematic release into production again (even at a quieter time) re-introduces the known issue to live users and risks a poor experience or data issues, which is unnecessary when the same diagnosis can be performed safely in a staging environment.

text: A production database virtual machine on Google Compute Engine has an ext4-formatted persistent disk for data files. The database is about to run out of storage space. How can you remediate the problem with the least amount of downtime?

options.text: In the Cloud Platform Console, increase the size of the persistent disk and use the resize2fs command in Linux.
Shut down the virtual machine, use the Cloud Platform Console to increase the persistent disk size, then restart the virtual machine
In the Cloud Platform Console, increase the size of the persistent disk and verify the new space is ready to use with the fdisk command in Linux
In the Cloud Platform Console, create a new persistent disk attached to the virtual machine, format and mount it, and configure the database service to move the files to the new disk
In the Cloud Platform Console, create a snapshot of the persistent disk restore the snapshot to a new larger disk, unmount the old disk, mount the new disk and restart the database service

optionExplanations.A: Persistent disks on Compute Engine can be resized live (online) without unmounting or stopping the VM; after increasing the disk size in the console or via gcloud, running resize2fs on the ext4 filesystem extends the filesystem to use the new space, all with minimal to no downtime.
optionExplanations.B: Shutting down the VM to resize the disk introduces unnecessary downtime, since GCE persistent disks support online resizing while the VM and filesystem remain mounted and in use.
optionExplanations.C: fdisk is used for partition table manipulation, not for growing an existing ext4 filesystem to use newly added disk space; resize2fs is the correct tool for ext4, making this an incomplete/incorrect remediation step.
optionExplanations.D: Provisioning, formatting, mounting a new disk, and migrating database files to it requires application downtime during the cutover and significant manual effort, which is far more disruptive than simply resizing the existing disk online.
optionExplanations.E: Creating a snapshot, restoring to a larger disk, and swapping disks requires stopping the database (and likely the VM) to safely unmount/remount disks, resulting in significantly more downtime than an online resize.

text: Your application needs to process credit card transactions. You want the smallest scope of Payment Card Industry (PCI) compliance without compromising the ability to analyze transactional data and trends relating to which payment methods are used. How should you design your architecture?

options.text: Create a tokenizer service and store only tokenized data
Create separate projects that only process credit card data
Create separate subnetworks and isolate the components that process credit card data
Streamline the audit discovery phase by labeling all of the virtual machines (VMs) that process PCI data
Enable Logging export to Google BigQuery and use ACLs and views to scope the data shared with the auditor

optionExplanations.A: Implementing a tokenization service that replaces sensitive cardholder data (PAN) with non-sensitive tokens means that only the tokenizer service falls within PCI DSS scope; the rest of the application can analyze transaction trends using tokens/metadata without ever handling raw card data, minimizing the compliance footprint while preserving analytics capability.
optionExplanations.B: Creating separate projects that process credit card data is a network/organizational segmentation strategy, but on its own it does not reduce the sensitivity of the data being stored and analyzed; the raw PAN data would still be in scope for PCI compliance across those projects.
optionExplanations.C: Isolating components into separate subnetworks helps with network segmentation (which can support reducing PCI scope), but without removing the actual cardholder data via tokenization, the systems handling and analyzing that data remain in scope for PCI audits.
optionExplanations.D: Labeling VMs that process PCI data may help with audit discovery and inventory, but it does not reduce the actual scope of what falls under PCI compliance, since the systems still process raw cardholder data.
optionExplanations.E: Exporting logs to BigQuery with ACLs and views helps manage audit access to logs, but it is unrelated to reducing PCI scope for systems that store and process actual credit card transaction data.

text: You have been asked to select the storage system for the click-data of your company's large portfolio of websites. This data is streamed in from a custom website analytics package at a typical rate of 6,000 clicks per minute. With bursts of up to 8,500 clicks per second. It must have been stored for future analysis by your data science and user experience teams. Which storage infrastructure should you choose?

options.text: Google Cloud SQL
Google Cloud Bigtable
Google Cloud Storage
Google Cloud Datastore

optionExplanations.A: Cloud SQL is a relational database designed for transactional (OLTP) workloads at moderate scale; it cannot reliably sustain bursts of 8,500 writes per second from streaming click data and is not optimized for this kind of high-throughput time-series ingestion.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud Bigtable is a high-throughput, low-latency NoSQL wide-column database specifically designed for massive-scale time-series and event data such as clickstream data, easily handling tens of thousands of writes per second with linear scalability, making it the ideal choice for this use case and subsequent analysis.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud Storage is an object store optimized for storing large files/blobs, not for high-velocity individual record writes (thousands per second) with low-latency random access needed for streaming click events.
optionExplanations.D: Cloud Datastore (Firestore in Datastore mode) is a NoSQL document database better suited for transactional application state with moderate write throughput; it is not designed for the extremely high write throughput (8,500 writes/sec bursts) of large-scale clickstream ingestion compared to Bigtable.

text: You are creating a solution to remove backup files older than 90 days from your backup Cloud Storage bucket. You want to optimize ongoing Cloud Storage spend. What should you do?

options.text: Write a lifecycle management rule in XML and push it to the bucket with gsutil
Write a lifecycle management rule in JSON and push it to the bucket with gsutil
Schedule a cron script using gsutil ls גlr gs://backups/** to find and remove items older than 90 days
Schedule a cron script using gsutil ls גl gs://backups/** to find and remove items older than 90 days and schedule it with cron

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Storage Object Lifecycle Management configurations are specified using JSON, not XML; gsutil's lifecycle command (gsutil lifecycle set) expects a JSON configuration file, so writing the rule in XML would not work.
optionExplanations.B: Writing a Cloud Storage Object Lifecycle Management rule in JSON (specifying an Age condition of 90 days and a Delete action) and applying it with 'gsutil lifecycle set' automates the removal of objects older than 90 days without any custom scripting or scheduling infrastructure, directly optimizing storage spend with minimal ongoing management.
optionExplanations.C: Writing and scheduling a custom cron script to list and delete objects requires building, hosting, and maintaining a separate compute resource (e.g., a VM or Cloud Function) just to run the cron job, which is unnecessary operational overhead when native Object Lifecycle Management can do this declaratively.
optionExplanations.D: Similar to option C, this approach requires custom scripting and a scheduling mechanism (cron) running somewhere, adding operational complexity and cost compared to using Cloud Storage's built-in lifecycle management feature.

text: Your company is forecasting a sharp increase in the number and size of Apache Spark and Hadoop jobs being run on your local datacenter. You want to utilize the cloud to help you scale this upcoming demand with the least amount of operations work and code change. Which product should you use?

options.text: Google Cloud Dataflow
Google Cloud Dataproc
Google Compute Engine
Google Kubernetes Engine

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Dataflow uses the Apache Beam programming model and would require significant rewriting of existing Apache Spark and Hadoop jobs to a different programming paradigm, which conflicts with the goal of minimal code change.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud Dataproc is a managed Hadoop and Spark service that allows existing Spark/Hadoop jobs to run with little to no modification, while Google handles cluster provisioning, scaling, and management, directly meeting the requirement to scale with minimal operations work and minimal code change.
optionExplanations.C: Running Spark/Hadoop directly on Compute Engine would require the team to manually install, configure, and manage Hadoop/Spark clusters themselves, resulting in significant operational overhead compared to a managed service.
optionExplanations.D: GKE would require containerizing and orchestrating Spark/Hadoop workloads manually (e.g., via the Spark Kubernetes operator), which involves substantially more setup and operational effort than using the purpose-built managed Dataproc service.

text: The database administration team has asked you to help them improve the performance of their new database server running on Google Compute Engine. The database is for importing and normalizing their performance statistics and is built with MySQL running on Debian Linux. They have an n1-standard-8 virtual machine with 80 GB of SSD persistent disk. What should they change to get better performance from this system?

options.text: Increase the virtual machine's memory to 64 GB
Create a new virtual machine running PostgreSQL
Dynamically resize the SSD persistent disk to 500 GB
Migrate their performance metrics warehouse to BigQuery
Modify all of their batch jobs to use bulk inserts into the database

optionExplanations.A: An n1-standard-8 already provides 30 GB of memory; while more memory could help with caching, the question emphasizes a small 80 GB SSD persistent disk, and persistent disk performance (IOPS/throughput) in GCE scales with disk size, making disk size the more direct performance bottleneck for a database doing heavy I/O.
optionExplanations.B: Switching database engines from MySQL to PostgreSQL is a major migration effort that does not address the underlying infrastructure performance bottleneck (disk I/O throughput tied to a small persistent disk).
optionExplanations.C: On Google Compute Engine, SSD persistent disk IOPS and throughput scale proportionally with the size of the volume; increasing the 80 GB SSD persistent disk to 500 GB significantly increases available IOPS and throughput, directly improving database performance for import/normalization workloads, and this can be done dynamically (online resize) with minimal disruption.
optionExplanations.D: Migrating the entire performance metrics warehouse to BigQuery is a significant architectural change that goes beyond fixing a performance bottleneck on an existing OLTP-style MySQL server and isn't a direct 'tuning' fix for the described symptom.
optionExplanations.E: While bulk inserts can improve write efficiency, the primary constraint described relates to the underlying disk's I/O capacity (a small 80 GB SSD), so optimizing application-level insert patterns alone would not resolve the fundamental disk throughput limitation as effectively as resizing the disk.

text: You want to optimize the performance of an accurate, real-time, weather-charting application. The data comes from 50,000 sensors sending 10 readings a second, in the format of a timestamp and sensor reading. Where should you store the data?

options.text: Google BigQuery
Google Cloud SQL
Google Cloud Bigtable
Google Cloud Storage

optionExplanations.A: BigQuery is excellent for ad hoc SQL analytics over large datasets but is not designed for very low-latency, high-throughput continuous writes from 500,000 readings/sec (50,000 sensors x 10 readings/sec) needed to support a real-time charting application.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud SQL, a relational database, would not scale to handle 500,000 writes per second from sensor data, making it unsuitable for this high-velocity time-series ingestion scenario.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud Bigtable is purpose-built for high-throughput, low-latency time-series data (timestamp + reading per sensor), easily handling massive sustained write rates (hundreds of thousands of operations per second) and enabling fast row-key based reads needed to power a real-time charting application.
optionExplanations.D: Cloud Storage is designed for storing objects/files, not for high-frequency, low-latency writes of individual time-series data points, and would not support the real-time query performance needed for live charting.

text: Your company's user-feedback portal comprises a standard LAMP stack replicated across two zones. It is deployed in the us-central1 region and uses autoscaled managed instance groups on all layers, except the database. Currently, only a small group of select customers have access to the portal. The portal meets a 99,99% availability SLA under these conditions. However next quarter, your company will be making the portal available to all users, including unauthenticated users. You need to develop a resiliency testing strategy to ensure the system maintains the SLA once they introduce additional user load. What should you do?

options.text: Capture existing users input, and replay captured user load until autoscale is triggered on all layers. At the same time, terminate all resources in one of the zones
Create synthetic random user input, replay synthetic load until autoscale logic is triggered on at least one layer, and introduce גchaosגto the system by terminating random resources on both zones
Expose the new system to a larger group of users, and increase group size each day until autoscale logic is triggered on all layers. At the same time, terminate random resources on both zones

Capture existing users input, and replay captured user load until resource utilization crosses 80%. Also, derive estimated number of users based on existing user's usage of the app, and deploy enough resources to handle 200% of expected load

optionExplanations.A: Replaying only existing (captured) user traffic does not represent the new, much larger and more diverse population of unauthenticated users the system will soon serve, so it would not adequately validate the system's behavior under the upcoming real-world load pattern.
optionExplanations.B: Generating synthetic random user input that more realistically represents diverse (including unauthenticated) traffic, replaying it until autoscaling triggers, and simultaneously injecting 'chaos' by terminating random resources across both zones tests both elasticity and resiliency to failures under realistic new-user load patterns, which is exactly the kind of resiliency testing needed to validate the SLA under the new traffic profile.
optionExplanations.C: Gradually exposing the system to real production users as a form of testing risks violating the SLA for those real users if failures occur, and 'testing in production' with random resource termination is risky compared to controlled load testing in a non-production or canary environment first.
optionExplanations.D: This approach uses only existing captured (not representative of new unauthenticated user) traffic and focuses on capacity provisioning (200% headroom) rather than actively testing resiliency to simultaneous autoscaling and zone-level failures, so it does not fully validate resiliency under the new conditions.

text: One of the developers on your team deployed their application in Google Container Engine with the Dockerfile below. They report that their application deployments are taking too long. You want to optimize this Dockerfile for faster deployment times without adversely affecting the app's functionality. Which two actions should you take? (Choose two.)

options.text: Remove Python after running pip
Remove dependencies from requirements.txt
Use a slimmed-down base image like Alpine Linux
Use larger machine types for your Google Container Engine node pools
Copy the source after he package dependencies (Python and pip) are installed

optionExplanations.A: Removing Python after running pip would break the application's runtime entirely, since the containerized app needs the Python interpreter to execute; this is not a viable optimization.
optionExplanations.B: Removing dependencies from requirements.txt could reduce image size, but doing so risks breaking the application's functionality if those dependencies are actually required, making it not a safe general recommendation for 'without adversely affecting functionality.'
optionExplanations.C: Using a slimmed-down base image like Alpine Linux significantly reduces the base image size (and thus the amount of data that needs to be pulled/pushed during deployment), speeding up build and deployment times without changing application functionality.
optionExplanations.D: Using larger machine types for GKE node pools increases compute capacity and cost but does not address the root cause of slow deployments, which is typically related to large image size and inefficient Docker layer caching, not node CPU/memory.
optionExplanations.E: Structuring the Dockerfile so that source code is copied after dependencies (Python packages via pip) are installed allows Docker's layer caching to reuse the dependency-installation layer across builds when only source code changes, dramatically speeding up incremental builds and deployments.

text: Your solution is producing performance bugs in production that you did not see in staging and test environments. You want to adjust your test and deployment procedures to avoid this problem in the future. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy fewer changes to production
Deploy smaller changes to production
Increase the load on your test and staging environments
Deploy changes to a small subset of users before rolling out to production

optionExplanations.A: Deploying fewer changes to production reduces deployment frequency but does nothing to catch performance issues before they reach production users; it merely reduces opportunities to ship (and to catch) bugs, without improving the testing/deployment process itself.
optionExplanations.B: Deploying smaller changes can reduce the blast radius of an issue, but on its own it does not address the root cause: that staging/test environments are not catching performance bugs that manifest under production conditions.
optionExplanations.C: While increasing load on test/staging environments to better match production traffic patterns could help surface performance issues earlier, it is harder to perfectly replicate production conditions (real user behavior, data volume, scale) in staging compared to controlled exposure in production itself.
optionExplanations.D: Using a canary/phased rollout strategy -- deploying changes to a small subset of production users (and production traffic/data) before a full rollout -- allows real production conditions to surface performance bugs while limiting the impact (blast radius) to a small percentage of users, and issues can be caught and rolled back before full deployment.

text: A small number of API requests to your microservices-based application take a very long time. You know that each request to the API can traverse many services. You want to know which service takes the longest in those cases. What should you do?

options.text: Set timeouts on your application so that you can fail requests faster
Send custom metrics for each of your requests to Stackdriver Monitoring
Use Stackdriver Monitoring to look for insights that show when your API latencies are high
Instrument your application with Stackdriver Trace in order to break down the request latencies at each microservice

optionExplanations.A: Setting timeouts would cause slow requests to fail faster, but it does not provide any visibility into which specific service in the call chain is responsible for the latency, so it does not address the diagnostic need.
optionExplanations.B: Sending custom metrics to Cloud Monitoring (Stackdriver Monitoring) could provide aggregate performance data per service, but without distributed tracing context (correlating a single request across services), it would be difficult to pinpoint which service is the bottleneck for a specific slow request.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud Monitoring can surface high-level latency trends and alerts, but it does not provide the per-request, per-service breakdown of where time is spent across a distributed call chain that's needed to identify the specific slow service for individual slow requests.
optionExplanations.D: Cloud Trace (Stackdriver Trace) is purpose-built for distributed tracing -- instrumenting each microservice allows you to see a waterfall/breakdown of latency for each hop a request takes, making it possible to pinpoint exactly which service contributes the most latency in slow requests.

text: During a high traffic portion of the day, one of your relational databases crashes, but the replica is never promoted to a master. You want to avoid this in the future. What should you do?

options.text: Use a different database
Choose larger instances for your database
Create snapshots of your database more regularly
Implement routinely scheduled failovers of your databases

optionExplanations.A: Switching to a different database engine does not address the underlying operational gap, which is the lack of an automated or tested failover process for promoting a replica when the primary fails.
optionExplanations.B: Choosing larger instance sizes addresses capacity/performance concerns, not the failover mechanism itself; a larger instance can still fail without an automatic or tested promotion process for the replica.
optionExplanations.C: More frequent snapshots improve the recovery point objective (RPO) for restoring from backups, but they do not address the failover process for promoting a standby replica to master during an outage.
optionExplanations.D: Implementing routinely scheduled failover drills/tests ensures the replica promotion process is exercised, validated, and functioning correctly, so that when a real crash occurs, the team (and any automation) is prepared to promote the replica successfully -- directly addressing the described failure of the replica not being promoted.

text: Your organization requires that metrics from all applications be retained for 5 years for future analysis in possible legal proceedings. Which approach should you use?

options.text: Grant the security team access to the logs in each Project
Configure Stackdriver Monitoring for all Projects, and export to BigQuery
Configure Stackdriver Monitoring for all Projects with the default retention policies
Configure Stackdriver Monitoring for all Projects, and export to Google Cloud Storage

optionExplanations.A: Granting the security team access to logs in each project addresses access control but does not address the core requirement of long-term (5-year) retention, since default log retention periods are far shorter and logs would still age out and be deleted.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud Monitoring (Stackdriver Monitoring) metrics and logs have limited default retention; exporting them to BigQuery allows the data to be retained indefinitely (or per a custom retention/table expiration policy set to meet the 5-year requirement) and remains queryable for legal/analysis purposes, which directly satisfies the long-term retention and future-analysis requirement.
optionExplanations.C: Relying on default retention policies means logs/metrics will be automatically deleted after the default retention window (typically much shorter than 5 years), failing to meet the long-term retention requirement.
optionExplanations.D: While exporting to Cloud Storage is also a valid long-term retention pattern for logs and could meet a 5-year retention requirement with appropriate lifecycle policies, in this scenario the requirement also implies the data should remain available for 'analysis,' and BigQuery export better supports structured querying/analysis of metrics data over Cloud Storage's object storage model.

text: Your company has decided to build a backup replica of their on-premises user authentication PostgreSQL database on Google Cloud Platform. The database is 4 TB, and large updates are frequent. Replication requires private address space communication. Which networking approach should you use?

options.text: Google Cloud Dedicated Interconnect
Google Cloud VPN connected to the data center network
A NAT and TLS translation gateway installed on-premises
A Google Compute Engine instance with a VPN server installed connected to the data center network

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Dedicated Interconnect provides a high-bandwidth, low-latency, private (non-internet) physical connection between the on-premises data center and Google Cloud's VPC network, which is well-suited for replicating a large (4 TB) database with frequent large updates over a private address space, and offers consistent throughput and lower latency than VPN.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud VPN encrypts traffic over the public internet between on-premises and GCP; while it does provide private IP connectivity, its throughput is capped (per tunnel, typically up to ~3 Gbps) and is subject to internet variability, making it less ideal than a dedicated connection for frequent large-volume replication of a 4 TB database.
optionExplanations.C: A NAT and TLS translation gateway is not a standard Google Cloud networking pattern for establishing private connectivity between on-premises and GCP for database replication; this does not provide the private address space connectivity required.
optionExplanations.D: Running a self-managed VPN server on a Compute Engine instance is an unmanaged, less reliable, and higher-maintenance approach compared to Cloud VPN or Interconnect, and would still face the bandwidth/latency limitations of internet-based VPN tunnels for large, frequent data transfers.

text: Auditors visit your teams every 12 months and ask to review all the Google Cloud Identity and Access Management (Cloud IAM) policy changes in the previous 12 months. You want to streamline and expedite the analysis and audit process. What should you do?

options.text: Create custom Google Stackdriver alerts and send them to the auditor
Enable Logging export to Google BigQuery and use ACLs and views to scope the data shared with the auditor
Use cloud functions to transfer log entries to Google Cloud SQL and use ACLs and views to limit an auditor's view
Enable Google Cloud Storage (GCS) log export to audit logs into a GCS bucket and delegate access to the bucket

optionExplanations.A: Custom Stackdriver (Cloud Monitoring) alerts are designed for real-time notification of conditions/incidents, not for providing auditors a comprehensive historical record of IAM policy changes over a 12-month period.
optionExplanations.B: While exporting logs to BigQuery with ACLs/views is a valid pattern for sharing scoped log data with auditors for general log analysis, Cloud Audit Logs (specifically Admin Activity logs capturing IAM policy changes) combined with a long-term storage destination is the more direct fit; BigQuery export is more suited to scenarios requiring SQL-based querying/analysis rather than simple delegated read access to raw audit log archives.
optionExplanations.C: Using Cloud Functions to copy log entries into Cloud SQL introduces unnecessary custom infrastructure and operational overhead (a function, a database, ACL/view management) compared to natively exporting Cloud Audit Logs to a storage destination.
optionExplanations.D: Configuring Cloud Audit Logs export (Admin Activity logs, which capture all IAM policy changes and are enabled by default and retained) to a Cloud Storage bucket, and then granting the auditor delegated read access (via IAM) to that bucket, provides a simple, low-maintenance way to give auditors direct access to a complete historical record of IAM policy changes for review during their annual audits.

text: You are designing a large distributed application with 30 microservices. Each of your distributed microservices needs to connect to a database back-end. You want to store the credentials securely. Where should you store the credentials?

options.text: In the source code
In an environment variable
In a secret management system
In a config file that has restricted access through ACLs

optionExplanations.A: Storing credentials directly in source code is a major security anti-pattern: it exposes secrets to anyone with repository access, gets captured in version control history, and makes credential rotation extremely difficult across 30 microservices.
optionExplanations.B: Environment variables can leak through process listings, crash dumps, logging, or container introspection, and they still require a separate secure mechanism to populate them at deploy time, so they are not a complete or secure solution on their own.
optionExplanations.C: Secret Manager is purpose-built for securely storing, versioning, and controlling access to sensitive data like database credentials, with fine-grained IAM permissions and audit logging, making it the recommended way to manage credentials across many microservices.
optionExplanations.D: Restricted ACLs on a config file reduce risk somewhat, but the file still sits on disk (or in an image/repo) where it can be copied, is harder to rotate and audit centrally, and lacks the versioning and access-logging capabilities of a dedicated secret manager.

text: A lead engineer wrote a custom tool that deploys virtual machines in the legacy data center. He wants to migrate the custom tool to the new cloud environment. You want to advocate for the adoption of Google Cloud Deployment Manager. What are two business risks of migrating to Cloud Deployment Manager? (Choose two.)

options.text: Cloud Deployment Manager uses Python
Cloud Deployment Manager APIs could be deprecated in the future
Cloud Deployment Manager is unfamiliar to the company's engineers
Cloud Deployment Manager requires a Google APIs service account to run
Cloud Deployment Manager can be used to permanently delete cloud resources
Cloud Deployment Manager only supports automation of Google Cloud resources

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Deployment Manager using Python (for templates) is a technical implementation detail and a learning curve issue rather than a fundamental business risk such as vendor lock-in or service continuity.
optionExplanations.B: Because Cloud Deployment Manager is a proprietary Google Cloud-specific configuration tool, there is a real business risk that Google could change, deprecate, or retire its APIs, forcing a costly rewrite of all deployment automation.
optionExplanations.C: Unfamiliarity is a training/adoption concern that can be addressed through onboarding and documentation; it represents a temporary skills gap rather than a strategic business risk tied to the tool itself.
optionExplanations.D: Requiring a Google service account to operate is a normal, expected aspect of any GCP-native automation tool and does not represent a meaningful business risk beyond standard IAM management.
optionExplanations.E: The ability to delete cloud resources is a necessary and expected capability of any infrastructure-as-code tool (to manage the full resource lifecycle) and is mitigated through IAM controls and review processes, not a unique business risk of adopting Cloud Deployment Manager.
optionExplanations.F: Because Cloud Deployment Manager only manages Google Cloud resources, migrating from a tool that manages on-premises VMs creates a real business risk of vendor lock-in, since the organization would need an additional or different tool for any non-GCP infrastructure.

text: A development manager is building a new application. He asks you to review his requirements and identify what cloud technologies he can use to meet them. The application must: 1. Be based on open-source technology for cloud portability 2. Dynamically scale compute capacity based on demand 3. Support continuous software delivery 4. Run multiple segregated copies of the same application stack 5. Deploy application bundles using dynamic templates 6. Route network traffic to specific services based on URL Which combination of technologies will meet all of his requirements?

options.text: Google Kubernetes Engine, Jenkins, and Helm
Google Kubernetes Engine and Cloud Load Balancing
Google Kubernetes Engine and Cloud Deployment Manager
Google Kubernetes Engine, Jenkins, and Cloud Load Balancing

optionExplanations.A: GKE, Jenkins, and Helm cover container orchestration, CI/CD pipelines, and templated deployments, but this combination omits a load balancing/routing component needed to route traffic to specific services based on URL.
optionExplanations.B: GKE provides container orchestration with autoscaling and Cloud Load Balancing provides URL-based routing, but this combination lacks a continuous delivery tool (like Jenkins) to satisfy the continuous software delivery requirement.
optionExplanations.C: GKE plus Cloud Deployment Manager addresses container orchestration and infrastructure provisioning, but Deployment Manager is a GCP-specific (not fully open-source) tool and does not provide continuous software delivery or URL-based traffic routing.
optionExplanations.D: GKE (open-source Kubernetes, dynamic scaling, namespaces for segregated stacks, Helm-style templated deployments), Jenkins (continuous software delivery pipelines), and Cloud Load Balancing (URL maps for routing traffic to specific services) together satisfy all six stated requirements, making this the correct combination.

text: You have created several pre-emptible Linux virtual machine instances using Google Compute Engine. You want to properly shut down your application before the virtual machines are preempted. What should you do?

options.text: Create a shutdown script named k99.shutdown in the /etc/rc.6.d/ directory
Create a shutdown script registered as a xinetd service in Linux and configure a Stackdriver endpoint check to call the service
Create a shutdown script and use it as the value for a new metadata entry with the key shutdown-script in the Cloud Platform Console when you create the new virtual machine instance

Create a shutdown script, registered as a xinetd service in Linux, and use the gcloud compute instances add-metadata command to specify the service URL as the value for a new metadata entry with the key shutdown-script-url

optionExplanations.A: Manually placing a script in /etc/rc.6.d/ relies on OS-level shutdown sequencing and is not the GCP-recommended mechanism; Compute Engine specifically looks for a 'shutdown-script' metadata key to run before preemption, so this approach bypasses the supported integration point.
optionExplanations.B: Registering the script as an xinetd service and using a Stackdriver (Cloud Monitoring) endpoint check does not tie into the preemption notice mechanism at all; Compute Engine sends a ACPI G2 soft-off signal that triggers the shutdown-script metadata, not an external health check.
optionExplanations.C: Setting the shutdown script as the value of the 'shutdown-script' metadata key is the documented Google Cloud approach: when a preemptible VM receives the preemption notice, Compute Engine automatically executes this script, giving the application up to 30 seconds to shut down gracefully.
optionExplanations.D: Using 'shutdown-script-url' to point to an external xinetd service URL adds unnecessary complexity and an external dependency; the simpler and recommended pattern is to embed the script content directly via the 'shutdown-script' metadata key.

text: Your organization has a 3-tier web application deployed in the same network on Google Cloud Platform. Each tier (web, API, and database) scales independently of the others. Network traffic should flow through the web to the API tier and then on to the database tier. Traffic should not flow between the web and the database tier. How should you configure the network?

options.text: Add each tier to a different subnetwork
Set up software based firewalls on individual VMs
Add tags to each tier and set up routes to allow the desired traffic flow
Add tags to each tier and set up firewall rules to allow the desired traffic flow

optionExplanations.A: Subnetworks are primarily used for IP address range segmentation and regional organization; placing each tier in a different subnet does not by itself control which tiers can communicate with each other, since VPC firewall rules (not subnet boundaries) govern traffic flow.
optionExplanations.B: Software-based firewalls on individual VMs would require configuring and maintaining iptables or similar rules on every instance across all auto-scaled groups, which is operationally complex and does not leverage GCP's centralized, scalable VPC firewall capability.
optionExplanations.C: Routes determine how packets are forwarded (next hops) within the network, but they do not enforce allow/deny security policies; routes alone cannot prevent the web tier from communicating directly with the database tier.
optionExplanations.D: Applying network tags to instances in each tier and writing firewall rules that allow traffic only from the web tag to the API tag, and from the API tag to the database tag (while denying web-to-database), is the standard GCP approach to enforce tier-to-tier traffic flow regardless of how each tier scales.

text: Your development team has installed a new Linux kernel module on the batch servers in Google Compute Engine (GCE) virtual machines (VMs) to speed up the nightly batch process. Two days after the installation, 50% of the batch servers failed the nightly batch run. You want to collect details on the failure to pass back to the development team. Which three actions should you take? (Choose three.)

options.text: Use Stackdriver Logging to search for the module log entries
Read the debug GCE Activity log using the API or Cloud Console
Use gcloud or Cloud Console to connect to the serial console and observe the logs
Identify whether a live migration event of the failed server occurred, using in the activity log
Adjust the Google Stackdriver timeline to match the failure time, and observe the batch server metrics
Export a debug VM into an image, and run the image on a local server where kernel log messages will be displayed on the native screen

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Logging (Stackdriver Logging) aggregates syslog and kernel module log entries from the VMs, allowing the team to search across all affected instances for errors related to the new kernel module without having to log into each VM individually.
optionExplanations.B: The GCE Activity/Audit log records administrative API operations (such as instance creation, metadata changes, restarts) but does not capture application or kernel-level error details from inside the guest OS, so it would not surface the batch job failure details needed here.
optionExplanations.C: The serial console output captures boot-time and kernel messages, including kernel panics or module load failures, even when the instance is unresponsive over SSH, making it a key tool for diagnosing a faulty kernel module.
optionExplanations.D: While checking for a live migration event could rule out an infrastructure-level cause, it is not central to diagnosing a kernel module problem introduced by the development team's own change, and is less directly useful than logs and serial console output.
optionExplanations.E: Adjusting the Cloud Monitoring (Stackdriver) timeline to the failure window lets you correlate CPU, memory, disk, and network metrics with the exact time of the batch failures, helping pinpoint resource exhaustion or anomalies caused by the new kernel module.
optionExplanations.F: Exporting a VM image and running it on a local server to view kernel messages on a physical screen is impractical, slow, and unnecessary when Cloud Logging and the serial console already provide remote access to the same kernel log information.

text: Your company wants to try out the cloud with low risk. They want to archive approximately 100 TB of their log data to the cloud and test the analytics features available to them there, while also retaining that data as a long-term disaster recovery backup. Which two steps should you take? (Choose two.)

options.text: Load logs into Google BigQuery
Load logs into Google Cloud SQL
Import logs into Google Stackdriver
Insert logs into Google Cloud Bigtable
Upload log files into Google Cloud Storage

optionExplanations.A: BigQuery is well suited for running ad hoc analytics queries over large volumes of log data using SQL, directly satisfying the requirement to 'test the analytics features available' in the cloud.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud SQL is a relational OLTP database not designed for storing or analyzing 100 TB of log data; it would be costly, would not scale well for this volume, and is not optimized for log analytics workloads.
optionExplanations.C: Stackdriver (Cloud Logging/Monitoring) is designed for ingesting and analyzing logs generated by GCP resources and applications in near real-time, not for bulk-importing 100 TB of historical log archives for long-term retention and analytics.
optionExplanations.D: Cloud Bigtable is optimized for high-throughput, low-latency NoSQL workloads (e.g., time-series or IoT data) and is overkill and costly for simply archiving log data for occasional analytics and disaster recovery purposes.
optionExplanations.E: Cloud Storage provides extremely durable, low-cost object storage ideal for archiving 100 TB of raw log files as a long-term disaster recovery backup, and it integrates directly with BigQuery for loading or querying the data via external tables.

text: You created a pipeline that can deploy your source code changes to your infrastructure in instance groups for self-healing. One of the changes negatively affects your key performance indicator. You are not sure how to fix it, and investigation could take up to a week. What should you do?

options.text: Log in to a server, and iterate on the fox locally
Revert the source code change, and rerun the deployment pipeline
Log into the servers with the bad code change, and swap in the previous code
Change the instance group template to the previous one, and delete all instances

optionExplanations.A: Logging into a server to iterate on a fix locally violates immutable infrastructure principles, creates configuration drift across the instance group, and would not be replicated to other instances or survive autohealing/scaling events.
optionExplanations.B: Reverting the source code change and rerunning the deployment pipeline restores the known-good state quickly through the same automated, repeatable process used for deployments, minimizing the time the negative KPI impact persists while a proper fix is investigated.
optionExplanations.C: Manually swapping code on individual servers bypasses the deployment pipeline, creates inconsistency between instances (especially as the managed instance group scales or self-heals and creates new instances from the original template), and is not a sustainable fix.
optionExplanations.D: Changing the instance template and deleting all instances is a much more disruptive and slower operation than simply reverting the code and redeploying through the existing pipeline, and risks additional downtime during instance recreation.

text: Your organization wants to control IAM policies for different departments independently, but centrally. Which approach should you take?

options.text: Multiple Organizations with multiple Folders
Multiple Organizations, one for each department
A single Organization with Folders for each department
A single Organization with multiple projects, each with a central owner

optionExplanations.A: An organization can only have one Cloud Resource Manager hierarchy root tied to a single Google Workspace/Cloud Identity domain; multiple Organizations is not a supported or practical configuration for a single company wanting centralized control.
optionExplanations.B: Using a separate Organization per department would fragment central visibility, billing, and policy management entirely, directly contradicting the requirement for centralized control.
optionExplanations.C: A single Organization resource with Folders representing each department allows IAM policies and Organization Policies to be set at each Folder level (delegating control to department admins) while still inheriting from and being visible to the central Organization, satisfying both independent and centralized control.
optionExplanations.D: Relying on individual project owners without a Folder structure provides no hierarchical mechanism for departments to manage their own sets of projects independently, and central oversight would have to be manually replicated project-by-project.

text: You deploy your custom Java application to Google App Engine. It fails to deploy and gives you the following stack trace. What should you do?

options.text: Upload missing JAR files and redeploy your application.
Digitally sign all of your JAR files and redeploy your application
Recompile the CLoakedServlet class using and MD5 hash instead of SHA1

optionExplanations.A: If the stack trace indicates a 'NoClassDefFoundError' or similar, missing JAR dependencies could be a cause, but the described scenario (a 'CloakedServlet'/signature-related stack trace) points to a JAR signing/verification issue rather than simply missing files.
optionExplanations.B: App Engine's Java runtime can fail deployment when JAR files are unsigned or have invalid/mismatched digital signatures (often surfaced as security manager or class-loading verification errors); digitally signing the JAR files and redeploying resolves this class of deployment error.
optionExplanations.C: Recompiling a class to use MD5 instead of SHA1 hashing is not a real or supported remediation for an App Engine deployment failure and does not address JAR signature verification issues; MD5 is also a weaker, deprecated hash algorithm that should not be introduced for security purposes.

text: You are designing a mobile chat application. You want to ensure people cannot spoof chat messages, by providing a message were sent by a specific user. What should you do?

options.text: Tag messages client side with the originating user identifier and the destination user.
Encrypt the message client side using block-based encryption with a shared key.
Use public key infrastructure (PKI) to encrypt the message client side using the originating user's private key.
Use a trusted certificate authority to enable SSL connectivity between the client application and the server.

optionExplanations.A: Client-side tags identifying the sender and recipient can be trivially forged by a malicious client, since there is no cryptographic proof binding the message to the claimed sender's identity.
optionExplanations.B: Block-based encryption with a shared key provides confidentiality but does not provide non-repudiation or sender authentication, since any party holding the shared key could have produced the encrypted message.
optionExplanations.C: Using PKI to have the originating user sign or encrypt the message with their private key allows the recipient (or server) to verify the message's authenticity using the corresponding public key, cryptographically proving the message originated from that specific user and preventing spoofing.
optionExplanations.D: TLS/SSL between the client and server secures the transport channel and authenticates the server (and optionally the client to the server), but it does not provide end-to-end proof to other users that a specific message originated from a specific sender, since the server could still be the source of a spoofed message.

text: As part of implementing their disaster recovery plan, your company is trying to replicate their production MySQL database from their private data center to their GCP project using a Google Cloud VPN connection. They are experiencing latency issues and a small amount of packet loss that is disrupting the replication. What should they do?

options.text: Configure their replication to use UDP.
Configure a Google Cloud Dedicated Interconnect.
Restore their database daily using Google Cloud SQL.
Add additional VPN connections and load balance them.
Send the replicated transaction to Google Cloud Pub/Sub.

optionExplanations.A: MySQL replication relies on TCP for reliable, ordered delivery; switching to UDP would not fix latency or packet loss and would actually make replication less reliable since UDP provides no delivery guarantees.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud Dedicated Interconnect provides a private, high-bandwidth, low-latency physical connection directly between the on-premises network and Google's network, bypassing the public internet and avoiding the variable latency and packet loss commonly seen over a Cloud VPN tunnel that traverses the internet.
optionExplanations.C: Switching to daily restores using Cloud SQL changes the architecture entirely (away from continuous replication) and would not address the underlying network latency/packet loss issue; it also introduces a much larger recovery point objective (RPO).
optionExplanations.D: Adding more VPN tunnels and load balancing them does not fundamentally fix the latency and packet loss caused by traversing the public internet; Cloud VPN tunnels are still subject to internet routing variability regardless of how many are used in parallel.
optionExplanations.E: Routing replicated transactions through Cloud Pub/Sub would require significant re-architecture of the MySQL replication mechanism (which uses its own binlog-based protocol) and does not solve the underlying network connectivity quality issue.

text: Your customer support tool logs all email and chat conversations to Cloud Bigtable for retention and analysis. What is the recommended approach for sanitizing this data of personally identifiable information or payment card information before initial storage?

options.text: Hash all data using SHA256
Encrypt all data using elliptic curve cryptography
De-identify the data with the Cloud Data Loss Prevention API
Use regular expressions to find and redact phone numbers, email addresses, and credit card numbers

optionExplanations.A: Hashing all data with SHA256 would make the stored conversations unusable for retention and analysis purposes (since hashes are one-way and cannot be searched or read as text), and it does not selectively target only PII/PCI fields.
optionExplanations.B: Encrypting all data with elliptic curve cryptography protects data at rest/in transit generally, but it does not identify or remove specific sensitive elements like credit card numbers from within free-text conversations, and encrypted data is still unusable for analysis without decryption.
optionExplanations.C: The Cloud Data Loss Prevention (DLP) API is Google's recommended service for automatically inspecting and de-identifying (e.g., redacting, masking, tokenizing) sensitive data such as PII and payment card information before it is stored, and it is purpose-built for exactly this use case at scale.
optionExplanations.D: Manually written regular expressions are brittle, error-prone, and difficult to maintain across the many formats of phone numbers, emails, and credit card numbers globally, and they lack the built-in detectors, confidence scoring, and broad infoType coverage that Cloud DLP provides out of the box.

text: You are using Cloud Shell and need to install a custom utility for use in a few weeks. Where can you store the file so it is in the default execution path and persists across sessions?

options.text: ~/bin
Cloud Storage
/google/scripts
/usr/local/bin

optionExplanations.A: The home directory in Cloud Shell (including ~/bin, which is included in the default PATH) is backed by a persistent 5GB disk that is preserved across sessions, making it the correct location for custom utilities that need to remain available and executable.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud Storage can store the file durably, but it is not part of the local filesystem or execution PATH of Cloud Shell, so the utility would need to be downloaded into the Cloud Shell environment each session before it could be executed.
optionExplanations.C: /google/scripts is not a standard or persistent location in Cloud Shell, and files placed in arbitrary system directories outside the home directory are not preserved across sessions since the underlying VM is ephemeral.
optionExplanations.D: /usr/local/bin resides on the ephemeral portion of the Cloud Shell VM's filesystem and is reset between sessions, so any files placed there would not persist for use weeks later.

text: You want to create a private connection between your instances on Compute Engine and your on-premises data center. You require a connection of at least 20 Gbps. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. How should you set up the connection?

options.text: Create a VPC and connect it to your on-premises data center using Dedicated Interconnect.
Create a VPC and connect it to your on-premises data center using a single Cloud VPN.
Create a Cloud Content Delivery Network (Cloud CDN) and connect it to your on-premises data center using Dedicated Interconnect.
Create a Cloud Content Delivery Network (Cloud CDN) and connect it to your on-premises datacenter using a single Cloud VPN.

optionExplanations.A: Dedicated Interconnect provides a direct physical connection to Google's network with per-link capacities up to 10 Gbps (and can be combined for up to 80 Gbps via multiple links), offering the private, high-bandwidth connection needed to meet a 20 Gbps requirement, and is the Google-recommended option for large, dedicated private connections to a VPC.
optionExplanations.B: A single Cloud VPN tunnel is capped at around 3 Gbps and traverses the public internet, which cannot meet a 20 Gbps requirement nor provide the fully private connectivity desired.
