optionExplanations.C: Cloud CDN is a content caching service for accelerating delivery of web content to end users globally; it is not a mechanism for establishing a private network connection between a VPC and an on-premises data center.
optionExplanations.D: Cloud CDN combined with Cloud VPN is conceptually mismatched (CDN is for content delivery, not private connectivity) and a single Cloud VPN cannot provide 20 Gbps of throughput regardless.

text: You are analyzing and defining business processes to support your startup's trial usage of GCP, and you don't yet know what consumer demand for your product will be. Your manager requires you to minimize GCP service costs and adhere to Google best practices. What should you do?

options.text: Utilize free tier and sustained use discounts. Provision a staff position for service cost management.
Utilize free tier and sustained use discounts. Provide training to the team about service cost management.
Utilize free tier and committed use discounts. Provision a staff position for service cost management.
Utilize free tier and committed use discounts. Provide training to the team about service cost management.

optionExplanations.A: Committed use discounts require a 1- or 3-year usage commitment, which is inappropriate when consumer demand is unknown and usage may need to scale up or down significantly; provisioning a dedicated staff position adds ongoing labor cost that is not justified for a startup trial.
optionExplanations.B: Combining the free tier with sustained use discounts (which automatically apply for workloads run for a significant portion of the billing month, with no upfront commitment) minimizes cost without locking into long-term commitments, and training the existing team on cost management avoids the added expense of a dedicated headcount, aligning with both cost minimization and trial-stage flexibility.
optionExplanations.C: Committed use discounts lock the company into a usage commitment despite unknown future demand, which could lead to paying for unused capacity, and hiring a dedicated staff member for cost management is an unnecessary fixed cost during a low-risk trial phase.
optionExplanations.D: Committed use discounts require predictable, sustained usage commitments that are inappropriate for a startup that doesn't yet know its consumer demand, since under-utilizing the commitment would increase rather than minimize costs.

text: You are building a continuous deployment pipeline for a project stored in a Git source repository and want to ensure that code changes can be verified before deploying to production. What should you do?

options.text: Use Spinnaker to deploy builds to production using the red/black deployment strategy so that changes can easily be rolled back.
Use Spinnaker to deploy builds to production and run tests on production deployments.
Use Jenkins to build the staging branches and the master branch. Build and deploy changes to production for 10% of users before doing a complete rollout.

Use Jenkins to monitor tags in the repository. Deploy staging tags to a staging environment for testing. After testing, tag the repository for production and deploy that to the production environment.

optionExplanations.A: Spinnaker's red/black (blue/green) strategy enables easy rollback after deployment, but it focuses on the deployment/rollback mechanism rather than on verifying code changes (e.g., via testing in a staging environment) before they reach production.
optionExplanations.B: Running tests directly on production deployments means changes are validated only after they are already live in production, which does not satisfy the requirement to verify changes before they reach production.
optionExplanations.C: Building staging and master branches and rolling out to 10% of production users (canary) still exposes real production traffic to unverified code, rather than fully validating changes in an isolated staging environment first.
optionExplanations.D: Using Jenkins to build from tags, deploy staging-tagged builds to a staging environment for testing, and only promote to production after creating a separate production tag ensures that code changes are fully verified in staging before any production deployment occurs, directly meeting the requirement.

text: You have an outage in your Compute Engine managed instance group: all instances keep restarting after 5 seconds. You have a health check configured, but autoscaling is disabled. Your colleague, who is a Linux expert, offered to look into the issue. You need to make sure that he can access the VMs. What should you do?

options.text: Grant your colleague the IAM role of project Viewer
Perform a rolling restart on the instance group
Disable the health check for the instance group. Add his SSH key to the project-wide SSH Keys
Disable autoscaling for the instance group. Add his SSH key to the project-wide SSH Keys

optionExplanations.A: The project Viewer IAM role grants only read-only access to view resources in the Cloud Console/API and does not grant SSH access to the guest OS of the VMs, so it would not let the colleague log in to investigate.
optionExplanations.B: Performing a rolling restart would not stop the instances from continuing to restart every 5 seconds (the underlying issue is the failing health check causing repeated restarts) and does not provide the access needed for debugging.
optionExplanations.C: Disabling the failing health check stops the instance group from continuously recreating/restarting the instances (since the health check is what's triggering the restarts), and adding the colleague's SSH key to project-wide SSH keys grants him access to SSH into the VMs to investigate, addressing both the immediate instability and the access requirement.
optionExplanations.D: Autoscaling is already disabled per the scenario, so disabling it again has no effect on the restart-loop issue, which is being driven by the health check, not the autoscaler; this option fails to address the actual root cause of the restarts.

text: Your company is migrating its on-premises data center into the cloud. As part of the migration, you want to integrate Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) for workload orchestration. Parts of your architecture must also be PCI DSS-compliant. Which of the following is most accurate?

options.text: App Engine is the only compute platform on GCP that is certified for PCI DSS hosting.
GKE cannot be used under PCI DSS because it is considered shared hosting.
GKE and GCP provide the tools you need to build a PCI DSS-compliant environment.
All Google Cloud services are usable because Google Cloud Platform is certified PCI-compliant.

optionExplanations.A: App Engine is not the only PCI DSS-compatible compute platform on GCP; GKE, Compute Engine, and other services can also be used to build PCI DSS-compliant environments when configured appropriately.
optionExplanations.B: GKE can absolutely be used within a PCI DSS scope; while shared infrastructure introduces additional considerations (such as network segmentation and namespace isolation), Google provides guidance and controls to use GKE compliantly, so it is not categorically excluded.
optionExplanations.C: GKE and the broader GCP platform provide the necessary building blocks (network policies, encryption, IAM, audit logging, VPC Service Controls, etc.) that customers can use to architect and operate a PCI DSS-compliant environment, though the customer remains responsible for implementing controls correctly under the shared responsibility model.
optionExplanations.D: PCI DSS compliance is a shared responsibility; not every GCP service or configuration automatically results in a compliant environment just because the underlying infrastructure has attained relevant certifications—customers must still implement compliant architectures and controls.

text: Your company has multiple on-premises systems that serve as sources for reporting. The data has not been maintained well and has become degraded over time. You want to use Google-recommended practices to detect anomalies in your company data. What should you do?

options.text: Upload your files into Cloud Storage. Use Cloud Datalab to explore and clean your data.
Upload your files into Cloud Storage. Use Cloud Dataprep to explore and clean your data.
Connect Cloud Datalab to your on-premises systems. Use Cloud Datalab to explore and clean your data.
Connect Cloud Dataprep to your on-premises systems. Use Cloud Dataprep to explore and clean your data.

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Datalab (a Jupyter-notebook-based exploration tool, now largely succeeded by Vertex AI Workbench) is geared toward custom code-based data science exploration and is less aligned with Google's recommended visual, guided data-cleaning workflow for this scenario than Cloud Dataprep.
optionExplanations.B: Uploading the degraded files to Cloud Storage and using Cloud Dataprep (a visual, serverless data preparation service built on Cloud Dataflow) to explore, profile, and clean the data follows Google's recommended pattern for detecting anomalies and preparing messy data for downstream analysis.
optionExplanations.C: Connecting Cloud Datalab directly to on-premises systems is not a typical or recommended pattern; Datalab is designed to operate against GCP-hosted data sources such as BigQuery, Cloud Storage, and Bigtable, and direct on-prem connections add unnecessary complexity and security exposure.
optionExplanations.D: Connecting Cloud Dataprep directly to on-premises systems is not the recommended approach; data should first be staged in Cloud Storage, after which Dataprep can be used for exploration and cleaning in a scalable, managed way.

text: Google Cloud Platform resources are managed hierarchically using organization, folders, and projects. When Cloud Identity and Access Management (IAM) policies exist at these different levels, what is the effective policy at a particular node of the hierarchy?

options.text: The effective policy is determined only by the policy set at the node
The effective policy is the policy set at the node and restricted by the policies of its ancestors
The effective policy is the union of the policy set at the node and policies inherited from its ancestors
The effective policy is the intersection of the policy set at the node and policies inherited from its ancestors

optionExplanations.A: Considering only the policy at the node ignores IAM's hierarchical inheritance model, where policies set on parent resources (organization, folders) also apply to child resources.
optionExplanations.B: IAM policies are additive, not restrictive; a policy set higher in the hierarchy does not narrow or restrict permissions granted at a lower node—it adds to them.
optionExplanations.C: In Google Cloud's resource hierarchy, the effective IAM policy for a resource is the union of the policy directly attached to that resource and all policies inherited from its ancestors (organization, folders, parent projects), meaning permissions are cumulative down the hierarchy.
optionExplanations.D: IAM policy inheritance is not an intersection (which would mean only permissions granted at every level apply); instead, permissions granted at any level in the hierarchy are inherited and combined (unioned) at descendant resources.

text: You are migrating your on-premises solution to Google Cloud in several phases. You will use Cloud VPN to maintain a connection between your on-premises systems and Google Cloud until the migration is completed. You want to make sure all your on-premise systems remain reachable during this period. How should you organize your networking in Google Cloud?

options.text: Use the same IP range on Google Cloud as you use on-premises
Use the same IP range on Google Cloud as you use on-premises for your primary IP range and use a secondary range that does not overlap with the range you use on-premises
Use an IP range on Google Cloud that does not overlap with the range you use on-premises
Use an IP range on Google Cloud that does not overlap with the range you use on-premises for your primary IP range and use a secondary range with the same IP range as you use on-premises

optionExplanations.A: Using the same IP range on Google Cloud as on-premises would create overlapping address spaces, causing routing conflicts over the Cloud VPN connection and making it impossible to reliably reach both environments simultaneously during the migration.
optionExplanations.B: Even if non-overlapping secondary ranges are added, having the primary range overlap with the on-premises network would still cause routing ambiguity for the primary range's resources, undermining reachability during the transition period.
optionExplanations.C: Choosing a non-overlapping IP range for the Google Cloud VPC ensures that routes over the Cloud VPN tunnel can unambiguously distinguish between on-premises and cloud resources, keeping all systems on both sides reachable throughout the phased migration.
optionExplanations.D: Using a non-overlapping primary range alongside a secondary range that duplicates the on-premises range introduces the same routing conflict for that secondary range, jeopardizing connectivity to systems using those addresses.

text: You have found an error in your App Engine application caused by missing Cloud Datastore indexes. You have created a YAML file with the required indexes and want to deploy these new indexes to Cloud Datastore. What should you do?

options.text: Point gcloud datastore create-indexes to your configuration file
Upload the configuration file to App Engine's default Cloud Storage bucket, and have App Engine detect the new indexes
In the GCP Console, use Datastore Admin to delete the current indexes and upload the new configuration file
Create an HTTP request to the built-in python module to send the index configuration file to your application

optionExplanations.A: The 'gcloud datastore indexes create' (formerly 'gcloud datastore create-indexes') command pointed at the index.yaml configuration file is the standard way to deploy new composite indexes to Cloud Datastore/Firestore in Datastore mode, ensuring the missing indexes required by the application's queries are created.
optionExplanations.B: Simply uploading the YAML file to a Cloud Storage bucket does not trigger any index creation process; App Engine does not automatically scan Cloud Storage buckets for index configuration changes.
optionExplanations.C: Datastore Admin in the console allows viewing and managing existing indexes but does not provide a mechanism to bulk-upload a new index configuration file; the gcloud command-line deployment is the supported method for applying index.yaml changes.
optionExplanations.D: There is no built-in Python module or HTTP endpoint on App Engine applications designed to receive and apply an index configuration file; index management is handled through the gcloud CLI or Cloud Console tooling, not through application-level HTTP requests.

text: You have an application that will run on Compute Engine. You need to design an architecture that takes into account a disaster recovery plan that requires your application to fail over to another region in case of a regional outage. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy the application on two Compute Engine instances in the same project but in a different region. Use the first instance to serve traffic, and use the HTTP load balancing service to fail over to the standby instance in case of a disaster.
Deploy the application on a Compute Engine instance. Use the instance to serve traffic, and use the HTTP load balancing service to fail over to an instance on your premises in case of a disaster.

Deploy the application on two Compute Engine instance groups, each in the same project but in a different region. Use the first instance group to serve traffic, and use the HTTP load balancing service to fail over to the standby instance group in case of a disaster.

Deploy the application on two Compute Engine instance groups, each in a separate project and a different region. Use the first instance group to serve traffic, and use the HTTP load balancing service to fail over to the standby instance group in case of a disaster.

optionExplanations.A: Single standalone instances (rather than managed instance groups) lack autoscaling and self-healing; relying on individual VMs for failover provides less resilience and does not represent the recommended highly available architecture pattern.
optionExplanations.B: Failing over to an on-premises instance does not address a 'fail over to another region' requirement within Google Cloud, and mixing cloud and on-premises failover via HTTP load balancing introduces additional complexity and latency not aligned with a cloud regional DR design.
optionExplanations.C: Deploying two managed instance groups in different regions within the same project, with the primary group serving traffic and HTTP(S) Load Balancing configured to fail over to the standby group's region during an outage, directly satisfies the regional failover requirement while leveraging GCP's global load balancing and autoscaling/self-healing capabilities.
optionExplanations.D: Splitting the deployment across two separate projects adds unnecessary organizational and IAM complexity without providing any additional disaster recovery benefit over having both instance groups within a single project, making this needlessly complex compared to option C.

text: You are deploying an application on App Engine that needs to integrate with an on-premises database. For security purposes, your on-premises database must not be accessible through the public internet. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy your application on App Engine standard environment and use App Engine firewall rules to limit access to the open on-premises database.
Deploy your application on App Engine standard environment and use Cloud VPN to limit access to the on-premises database.
Deploy your application on App Engine flexible environment and use App Engine firewall rules to limit access to the on-premises database.
Deploy your application on App Engine flexible environment and use Cloud VPN to limit access to the on-premises database.

optionExplanations.A: App Engine standard environment runs in a sandboxed environment that does not support VPC connectivity in the way needed to reach private on-premises resources, and App Engine firewall rules control inbound traffic to the app, not outbound private connectivity to on-premises systems.
optionExplanations.B: App Engine standard environment has limited (and historically no, prior to Serverless VPC Access) support for connecting to VPC networks and Cloud VPN tunnels, making it unsuitable for reliably reaching a private on-premises database without exposing it publicly.
optionExplanations.C: App Engine flexible environment runs within a VPC and can leverage VPN connectivity, but App Engine firewall rules govern inbound access to the App Engine app itself, not the path to reach a private on-premises database—Cloud VPN is the component that actually provides that private connectivity.
optionExplanations.D: App Engine flexible environment instances run as Compute Engine VMs within your VPC, allowing them to communicate over a Cloud VPN tunnel to the on-premises network, keeping the database private and inaccessible from the public internet while still being reachable by the application.

text: You are working in a highly secured environment where public Internet access from the Compute Engine VMs is not allowed. You do not yet have a VPN connection to access an on-premises file server. You need to install specific software on a Compute Engine instance. How should you install the software?

options.text: Upload the required installation files to Cloud Storage. Configure the VM on a subnet with a Private Google Access subnet. Assign only an internal IP address to the VM. Download the installation files to the VM using gsutil.
Upload the required installation files to Cloud Storage and use firewall rules to block all traffic except the IP address range for Cloud Storage. Download the files to the VM using gsutil.

Upload the required installation files to Cloud Source Repositories. Configure the VM on a subnet with a Private Google Access subnet. Assign only an internal IP address to the VM. Download the installation files to the VM using gcloud.

Upload the required installation files to Cloud Source Repositories and use firewall rules to block all traffic except the IP address range for Cloud Source Repositories. Download the files to the VM using gsutil.

optionExplanations.A: Uploading the installation files to Cloud Storage and enabling Private Google Access on the VM's subnet allows a VM with only an internal IP address to reach Cloud Storage (and other Google APIs) over Google's private network without any public internet access, satisfying the security requirement while allowing 'gsutil' to download the files.
optionExplanations.B: Assigning a VM only an internal IP and then trying to allow traffic to Cloud Storage's IP ranges via firewall rules is unnecessarily complex and still implies the VM may have or need a path to the internet; Private Google Access is the purpose-built mechanism for this, making this option more convoluted than necessary.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud Source Repositories is designed for storing source code (Git repositories), not binary installation packages/files, making it an awkward and non-standard choice for distributing installer files compared to Cloud Storage.
optionExplanations.D: Similar to option C, Cloud Source Repositories is not intended for distributing installation binaries, and combining it with restrictive firewall rules adds complexity without leveraging the simpler, purpose-built Private Google Access + Cloud Storage pattern.

text: Your company is moving 75 TB of data into Google Cloud. You want to use Cloud Storage and follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.text: Move your data onto a Transfer Appliance. Use a Transfer Appliance Rehydrator to decrypt the data into Cloud Storage.
Move your data onto a Transfer Appliance. Use Cloud Dataprep to decrypt the data into Cloud Storage.
Install gsutil on each server that contains data. Use resumable transfers to upload the data into Cloud Storage.
Install gsutil on each server containing data. Use streaming transfers to upload the data into Cloud Storage.

optionExplanations.A: For very large datasets (commonly cited around 20TB+ or when network transfer would take too long), Google recommends using a Transfer Appliance to physically ship encrypted data to Google, after which the Transfer Appliance Rehydrator decrypts and loads the data into the specified Cloud Storage bucket—an appropriate approach for 75 TB.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud Dataprep is a data cleaning and preparation tool for structured/semi-structured data within Cloud Storage/BigQuery; it is not used to decrypt or rehydrate data from a Transfer Appliance, which requires the dedicated Rehydrator process.
optionExplanations.C: While gsutil with resumable transfers is suitable for moving smaller datasets or for workloads with adequate network bandwidth, transferring 75 TB over typical network connections would take an impractically long time and is not the Google-recommended approach at this scale.
optionExplanations.D: Streaming transfers with gsutil are intended for smaller, real-time data uploads and are not designed for or recommended for bulk transfer of 75 TB of data; they also lack the resumability needed for such a large transfer over standard networks.

text: You have an application deployed on Google Kubernetes Engine using a Deployment named echo-deployment. The deployment is exposed using a Service called echo-service. You need to perform an update to the application with minimal downtime to the application. What should you do?

options.text: Use kubectl set image deployment/echo-deployment <new-image>
Use the rolling update functionality of the Instance Group behind the Kubernetes cluster
Update the deployment yaml file with the new container image. Use kubectl delete deployment/echo-deployment and kubectl create גf <yaml-file>
Update the service yaml file which the new container image. Use kubectl delete service/echo-service and kubectl create גf <yaml-file>

optionExplanations.A: The 'kubectl set image deployment/echo-deployment <new-image>' command triggers a Kubernetes Deployment's built-in rolling update strategy, incrementally replacing old Pods with new ones while keeping the Service available, achieving the update with minimal downtime.
optionExplanations.B: GKE Deployments are managed by the Kubernetes control plane, not by the underlying Compute Engine Managed Instance Group's rolling update mechanism; performing a rolling update at the instance group/node level would not correctly orchestrate a Pod-level application update.
optionExplanations.C: Deleting the entire Deployment and recreating it would terminate all running Pods first, causing an outage until the new Deployment's Pods become ready, which directly violates the 'minimal downtime' requirement.
optionExplanations.D: Deleting and recreating the Service does not update the application's container image at all (Services route traffic to Pods based on label selectors but don't define container images), and deleting the Service would cause a temporary loss of the stable network endpoint/DNS name for the application.

text: Your company is using BigQuery as its enterprise data warehouse. Data is distributed over several Google Cloud projects. All queries on BigQuery need to be billed on a single project. You want to make sure that no query costs are incurred on the projects that contain the data. Users should be able to query the datasets, but not edit them. How should you configure users' access roles?

options.text: Add all users to a group. Grant the group the role of BigQuery user on the billing project and BigQuery dataViewer on the projects that contain the data.
Add all users to a group. Grant the group the roles of BigQuery dataViewer on the billing project and BigQuery user on the projects that contain the data.
Add all users to a group. Grant the group the roles of BigQuery jobUser on the billing project and BigQuery dataViewer on the projects that contain the data.
Add all users to a group. Grant the group the roles of BigQuery dataViewer on the billing project and BigQuery jobUser on the projects that contain the data.

optionExplanations.A: Granting BigQuery user on the billing project would let users run and pay for queries from that project, but it does not address the requirement that no costs be incurred on the data projects; the dataViewer role on the data projects is correct, but pairing it with 'user' on the billing project still allows job creation rights that aren't strictly needed and does not match the recommended minimal-permission pattern as well as jobUser does.
optionExplanations.B: This reverses the roles: dataViewer on the billing project gives no ability to run jobs there, and BigQuery user on the data projects would allow users to create jobs (and incur cost) directly against the data projects, violating the requirement that no query costs be incurred there.
optionExplanations.C: Granting BigQuery jobUser on the billing project lets users create and run query jobs that are billed to that project, while granting BigQuery dataViewer on the data projects lets them read the underlying datasets without being able to modify them or run billable jobs there - this is exactly the least-privilege configuration that centralizes billing while allowing read-only cross-project querying.
optionExplanations.D: BigQuery dataViewer on the billing project does not grant the ability to run query jobs there, and BigQuery jobUser on the data projects would let users execute jobs (and accrue costs) on the data projects, which directly contradicts the requirement that no costs be incurred on the projects containing the data.

text: You have developed an application using Cloud ML Engine that recognizes famous paintings from uploaded images. You want to test the application and allow specific people to upload images for the next 24 hours. Not all users have a Google Account. How should you have users upload images?

options.text: Have users upload the images to Cloud Storage. Protect the bucket with a password that expires after 24 hours.
Have users upload the images to Cloud Storage using a signed URL that expires after 24 hours.
Create an App Engine web application where users can upload images. Configure App Engine to disable the application after 24 hours. Authenticate users via Cloud Identity.
Create an App Engine web application where users can upload images for the next 24 hours. Authenticate users via Cloud Identity.

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Storage buckets do not support a 'password protection' mechanism with a built-in expiration; access to Cloud Storage is controlled via IAM and ACLs, not bucket-level passwords, so this approach is not technically feasible as described.
optionExplanations.B: A signed URL can be generated with a specific expiration (up to 7 days, or longer with service account-based signing) and grants temporary upload (PUT) permission to a specific object without requiring the uploader to have a Google Account, which directly satisfies the requirement to allow non-Google-Account users time-limited upload access.
optionExplanations.C: App Engine does not have a built-in feature to 'disable' an application after a fixed time, and Cloud Identity authentication requires users to have Google/Cloud Identity accounts, which conflicts with the requirement that not all users have a Google Account.
optionExplanations.D: Building a full App Engine application is unnecessary overhead for a simple temporary upload use case, and authenticating via Cloud Identity would exclude users who do not have a Google Account, failing the stated requirement.

text: Your web application must comply with the requirements of the European Union's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). You are responsible for the technical architecture of your web application. What should you do?

options.text: Ensure that your web application only uses native features and services of Google Cloud Platform, because Google already has various certifications and provides גpass-onגcompliance when you use native features.
Enable the relevant GDPR compliance setting within the GCPConsole for each of the services in use within your application.
Ensure that Cloud Security Scanner is part of your test planning strategy in order to pick up any compliance gaps.
Define a design for the security of data in your web application that meets GDPR requirements.

optionExplanations.A: Using only native GCP services does not automatically make an application GDPR-compliant; Google provides a compliant infrastructure and tooling (a shared responsibility model), but the customer is still responsible for how they design and operate their application and handle personal data.
optionExplanations.B: There is no single 'GDPR compliance setting' that can be toggled on for GCP services; compliance is achieved through proper architecture, data handling practices, and contractual agreements (e.g., Google's Data Processing Addendum), not a console switch.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud Security Scanner (now part of Web Security Scanner) helps identify common web vulnerabilities like XSS and outdated libraries, but it does not assess or ensure GDPR compliance, which concerns data protection, consent, data subject rights, and data residency.
optionExplanations.D: GDPR compliance is fundamentally an architectural and design responsibility - the architect must design the system's data flows, storage, encryption, access controls, retention, and deletion capabilities to meet GDPR requirements (e.g., right to erasure, data minimization, breach notification), making this the correct and most comprehensive answer.

text: You need to set up Microsoft SQL Server on GCP. Management requires that there's no downtime in case of a data center outage in any of the zones within a GCP region. What should you do?

options.text: Configure a Cloud SQL instance with high availability enabled.
Configure a Cloud Spanner instance with a regional instance configuration.
Set up SQL Server on Compute Engine, using Always On Availability Groups using Windows Failover Clustering. Place nodes in different subnets.
Set up SQL Server Always On Availability Groups using Windows Failover Clustering. Place nodes in different zones.

optionExplanations.A: Cloud SQL does not support Microsoft SQL Server with a managed high-availability configuration in the way required here (and even Cloud SQL for SQL Server's HA uses a regional persistent disk failover, which historically has limitations); more importantly, the question implies a self-managed SQL Server requirement, making this not the best fit for an enterprise HA SQL Server deployment with Always On requirements.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud Spanner is a distributed relational database but is not Microsoft SQL Server - it cannot run SQL Server workloads, so it does not meet the explicit requirement to set up SQL Server.
optionExplanations.C: Always On Availability Groups require nodes to be part of the same Windows Failover Cluster typically within the same subnet/zone constructs for proper failover; placing nodes in different subnets without considering zone placement does not directly address the zone-outage resilience requirement as precisely as placing them in different zones.
optionExplanations.D: Configuring SQL Server Always On Availability Groups with Windows Failover Clustering and placing the cluster nodes in different zones within the same region ensures that if one zone experiences an outage, a node in another zone can take over with no downtime, directly satisfying the zone-resilience requirement for a self-managed SQL Server deployment on Compute Engine.

text: The development team has provided you with a Kubernetes Deployment file. You have no infrastructure yet and need to deploy the application. What should you do?

options.text: Use gcloud to create a Kubernetes cluster. Use Deployment Manager to create the deployment.
Use gcloud to create a Kubernetes cluster. Use kubectl to create the deployment.
Use kubectl to create a Kubernetes cluster. Use Deployment Manager to create the deployment.
Use kubectl to create a Kubernetes cluster. Use kubectl to create the deployment.

optionExplanations.A: Deployment Manager is Google Cloud's infrastructure-as-code tool for provisioning GCP resources (like the GKE cluster itself), not for applying Kubernetes-native Deployment manifests; it is not the appropriate tool to apply a Kubernetes Deployment YAML file to a cluster.
optionExplanations.B: Using gcloud (e.g., gcloud container clusters create) is the standard way to provision a GKE cluster, and kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml is the standard Kubernetes-native way to deploy a Deployment manifest to that cluster - this is the correct, idiomatic combination of tools for the task.
optionExplanations.C: kubectl is a Kubernetes API client used to manage workloads on an existing cluster; it cannot create the underlying GKE cluster infrastructure itself, so this option is not feasible.
optionExplanations.D: kubectl cannot create a GKE cluster (cluster creation requires gcloud, the Console, Terraform, or Deployment Manager acting on the GCP control plane), so the first step is invalid even though using kubectl for the deployment itself would be correct.

text: You need to evaluate your team readiness for a new GCP project. You must perform the evaluation and create a skills gap plan which incorporates the business goal of cost optimization. Your team has deployed two GCP projects successfully to date. What should you do?

options.text: Allocate budget for team training. Set a deadline for the new GCP project.
Allocate budget for team training. Create a roadmap for your team to achieve Google Cloud certification based on job role.
Allocate budget to hire skilled external consultants. Set a deadline for the new GCP project.
Allocate budget to hire skilled external consultants. Create a roadmap for your team to achieve Google Cloud certification based on job role.

optionExplanations.A: Allocating budget for team training directly addresses the skills gap and supports cost optimization (in-house skills reduce reliance on external spend); setting a deadline for the new project provides the structured timeline needed to plan training around, making this the most balanced and cost-conscious approach for a team that has already shown some success with two prior deployments.
optionExplanations.B: While building a certification roadmap is valuable for long-term skills development, it does not directly tie to the immediate need to evaluate readiness against a specific upcoming project deadline, and certification timelines are typically longer-term than what's needed to address an immediate skills gap for a project.
optionExplanations.C: Hiring external consultants does not build internal team capability (failing the 'skills gap plan' requirement) and increases ongoing costs, which conflicts with the stated business goal of cost optimization.
optionExplanations.D: Hiring external consultants conflicts with the cost optimization goal, and while a certification roadmap helps long-term, this combination does not efficiently address an immediate project need with a focus on internal skill development and cost control.

text: You are designing an application for use only during business hours. For the minimum viable product release, you'd like to use a managed product that automatically `scales to zero` so you don't incur costs when there is no activity. Which primary compute resource should you choose?

options.text: Cloud Functions
Compute Engine
Google Kubernetes Engine
AppEngine flexible environment

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Functions is a fully managed, event-driven serverless compute platform that automatically scales down to zero instances when there is no traffic, meaning you incur no compute cost during idle periods - this directly matches the 'scales to zero' requirement for a low-traffic, business-hours-only MVP.
optionExplanations.B: Compute Engine VMs do not scale to zero on their own; even with managed instance groups and autoscaling, there is typically a minimum of one running instance, and you continue to pay for that instance even when idle.
optionExplanations.C: GKE requires at least one running node (and the cluster management overhead) to host pods; it does not natively scale the entire cluster to zero nodes for a simple application, so it does not meet the 'scales to zero' requirement as cleanly as Cloud Functions.
optionExplanations.D: App Engine flexible environment runs on Compute Engine VMs and requires a minimum of one instance to always be running - it does not scale to zero, unlike App Engine standard environment or Cloud Functions.

text: You are creating an App Engine application that uses Cloud Datastore as its persistence layer. You need to retrieve several root entities for which you have the identifiers. You want to minimize the overhead in operations performed by Cloud Datastore. What should you do?

options.text: Create the Key object for each Entity and run a batch get operation
Create the Key object for each Entity and run multiple get operations, one operation for each entity
Use the identifiers to create a query filter and run a batch query operation
Use the identifiers to create a query filter and run multiple query operations, one operation for each entity

optionExplanations.A: When you already know the entity identifiers (keys), constructing Key objects and performing a batch get (e.g., ndb.get_multi or datastore batch lookup) retrieves multiple entities in a single round trip, which is the most efficient approach and minimizes RPC overhead to Datastore.
optionExplanations.B: Issuing multiple individual get operations (one per entity) incurs the overhead of multiple round trips to Datastore, which is less efficient than a single batch get when the keys are already known.
optionExplanations.C: Running a query (even a 'batch' query) to look up entities by key incurs additional query-planning and index-scanning overhead compared to a direct key-based get, which is the most efficient lookup method when identifiers are already known.
optionExplanations.D: Running multiple separate query operations, one per entity, is the least efficient option - it combines the overhead of query execution with the overhead of multiple round trips, making it far from optimal.

text: You need to upload files from your on-premises environment to Cloud Storage. You want the files to be encrypted on Cloud Storage using customer-supplied encryption keys. What should you do?

options.text: Supply the encryption key in a .boto configuration file. Use gsutil to upload the files.
Supply the encryption key using gcloud config. Use gsutil to upload the files to that bucket.
Use gsutil to upload the files, and use the flag --encryption-key to supply the encryption key.
Use gsutil to create a bucket, and use the flag --encryption-key to supply the encryption key. Use gsutil to upload the files to that bucket.

optionExplanations.A: Customer-supplied encryption keys (CSEK) for gsutil are configured via the 'encryption_key' (and optionally 'decryption_key1' etc.) settings in the .boto configuration file; gsutil reads this file and applies the specified key when uploading objects, which is the documented and correct approach for CSEK with gsutil.
optionExplanations.B: gcloud config is used for configuring gcloud CLI properties (project, account, region, etc.) and does not provide a mechanism for supplying customer-supplied encryption keys for gsutil object uploads; CSEK configuration is done through the .boto file, not gcloud config.
optionExplanations.C: gsutil does not have a global '--encryption-key' command-line flag for cp/upload operations; customer-supplied encryption keys must be configured via the .boto configuration file (encryption_key setting), not passed as a per-command flag.
optionExplanations.D: There is no '--encryption-key' flag for gsutil mb (make bucket) either; customer-supplied encryption keys are applied at the object level via .boto configuration during upload, not set as a bucket-level property through this flag.

text: Your customer wants to capture multiple GBs of aggregate real-time key performance indicators (KPIs) from their game servers running on Google Cloud Platform and monitor the KPIs with low latency. How should they capture the KPIs?

options.text: Store time-series data from the game servers in Google Bigtable, and view it using Google Data Studio.
Output custom metrics to Stackdriver from the game servers, and create a Dashboard in Stackdriver Monitoring Console to view them.
Schedule BigQuery load jobs to ingest analytics files uploaded to Cloud Storage every ten minutes, and visualize the results in Google Data Studio.
Insert the KPIs into Cloud Datastore entities, and run ad hoc analysis and visualizations of them in Cloud Datalab.

optionExplanations.A: Bigtable is well-suited for high-throughput time-series storage, but Data Studio is a reporting/visualization tool with refresh intervals not designed for true low-latency, real-time monitoring of streaming KPIs - it introduces latency that conflicts with the 'low latency' monitoring requirement.
optionExplanations.B: Outputting custom metrics directly to Cloud Monitoring (Stackdriver) and building a Monitoring dashboard provides near real-time ingestion and visualization of custom KPIs with low latency, which is purpose-built for exactly this kind of operational, real-time metrics monitoring use case.
optionExplanations.C: Scheduling BigQuery load jobs every ten minutes introduces significant latency (up to 10+ minutes of delay) and is a batch-oriented pattern, which does not satisfy the requirement for low-latency, real-time KPI monitoring.
optionExplanations.D: Cloud Datastore combined with ad hoc analysis in Datalab is a batch/interactive analysis pattern, not designed for real-time, low-latency monitoring of streaming high-volume KPI data; it also adds operational overhead to write and query large volumes of time-series data.

text: You have a Python web application with many dependencies that requires 0.1 CPU cores and 128 MB of memory to operate in production. You want to monitor and maximize machine utilization. You also want to reliably deploy new versions of the application. Which set of steps should you take?

options.text: Perform the following: 1. Create a managed instance group with f1-micro type machines. 2. Use a startup script to clone the repository, check out the production branch, install the dependencies, and start the Python app. 3. Restart the instances to automatically deploy new production releases.

Perform the following: 1. Create a managed instance group with n1-standard-1 type machines. 2. Build a Compute Engine image from the production branch that contains all of the dependencies and automatically starts the Python app. 3. Rebuild the Compute Engine image, and update the instance template to deploy new production releases.

Perform the following: 1. Create a Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) cluster with n1-standard-1 type machines. 2. Build a Docker image from the production branch with all of the dependencies, and tag it with the version number. 3. Create a Kubernetes Deployment with the imagePullPolicy set to 'IfNotPresent' in the staging namespace, and then promote it to the production namespace after testing.

Perform the following: 1. Create a GKE cluster with n1-standard-4 type machines. 2. Build a Docker image from the master branch with all of the dependencies, and tag it with 'latest'. 3. Create a Kubernetes Deployment in the default namespace with the imagePullPolicy set to 'Always'. Restart the pods to automatically deploy new production releases.

optionExplanations.A: f1-micro instances are heavily resource-constrained (shared CPU, 0.6 GB memory) and using a startup script to clone a repo and install dependencies on every restart is slow, fragile, and not a reliable deployment mechanism - it doesn't ensure consistent, repeatable deployments and wastes time/resources reinstalling dependencies on each boot.
optionExplanations.B: An n1-standard-1 (1 vCPU, 3.75 GB memory) machine can host multiple instances of an app needing only 0.1 CPU/128 MB, allowing good utilization; building a versioned Compute Engine image with all dependencies baked in ensures fast, reliable, repeatable deployments, and updating the instance template (then performing a rolling update) is the Google-recommended pattern for reliable VM-based deployments with good resource utilization.
optionExplanations.C: While GKE with Docker images and version tags is a reasonable practice, using imagePullPolicy 'IfNotPresent' can lead to stale images being used if the tag is reused, and for an app with such minimal resource needs (0.1 CPU/128MB), n1-standard-1 nodes in GKE add cluster management overhead; this option is more complex than necessary and the staging-to-production promotion via namespaces isn't the most precise match for 'maximize machine utilization' as directly as right-sizing VM images.
optionExplanations.D: Using n1-standard-4 (4 vCPU) machines for an app needing only 0.1 CPU is a poor utilization choice (oversized), tagging images with 'latest' and using imagePullPolicy 'Always' makes deployments non-deterministic and harder to roll back reliably, and restarting pods to deploy is not as controlled or reliable as a proper rolling deployment strategy - this combination violates both the utilization and reliability goals.

text: Your company wants to start using Google Cloud resources but wants to retain their on-premises Active Directory domain controller for identity management. What should you do?

options.text: Use the Admin Directory API to authenticate against the Active Directory domain controller.
Use Google Cloud Directory Sync to synchronize Active Directory usernames with cloud identities and configure SAML SSO.
Use Cloud Identity-Aware Proxy configured to use the on-premises Active Directory domain controller as an identity provider.
Use Compute Engine to create an Active Directory (AD) domain controller that is a replica of the on-premises AD domain controller using Google Cloud Directory Sync.

optionExplanations.A: The Admin Directory API is part of Google Workspace Admin SDK for managing Google Workspace users/groups, not a mechanism for authenticating against an on-premises Active Directory domain controller; it does not provide identity federation with on-prem AD.
optionExplanations.B: Google Cloud Directory Sync (GCDS) synchronizes user, group, and other identity data from an on-premises LDAP/Active Directory into Cloud Identity, while configuring SAML-based SSO allows users to authenticate against the existing on-premises AD (via an identity provider like AD FS) - this combination lets the company retain on-premises AD as the identity source while enabling access to Google Cloud resources, which is exactly the Google-recommended hybrid identity pattern.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) is used to control access to applications based on identity and context, but it relies on Cloud Identity/Google accounts as its identity source - it cannot be configured to use an on-premises AD domain controller directly as an identity provider.
optionExplanations.D: Creating a replica AD domain controller on Compute Engine using GCDS is not how GCDS works (GCDS only performs one-way synchronization of directory data to Cloud Identity, it does not replicate or create AD domain controllers), so this option misrepresents the tool's functionality and adds unnecessary infrastructure to manage.

text: You are running a cluster on Kubernetes Engine (GKE) to serve a web application. Users are reporting that a specific part of the application is not responding anymore. You notice that all pods of your deployment keep restarting after 2 seconds. The application writes logs to standard output. You want to inspect the logs to find the cause of the issue. Which approach can you take?

options.text: Review the Stackdriver logs for each Compute Engine instance that is serving as a node in the cluster.
Review the Stackdriver logs for the specific GKE container that is serving the unresponsive part of the application.
Connect to the cluster using gcloud credentials and connect to a container in one of the pods to read the logs.
Review the Serial Port logs for each Compute Engine instance that is serving as a node in the cluster.

optionExplanations.A: Reviewing logs at the Compute Engine node level would show node-level system logs, not the application's stdout/stderr logs from individual containers, which is what's needed to diagnose why the application pods are crash-looping.
optionExplanations.B: GKE automatically collects container stdout/stderr and forwards it to Cloud Logging (Stackdriver Logging); filtering logs for the specific GKE container/pod that's failing lets you see the application's own log output (e.g., stack traces, errors) leading up to the crash, which is the most direct way to diagnose a CrashLoopBackOff caused by an application error.
optionExplanations.C: Since the pods keep restarting every 2 seconds (a CrashLoopBackOff), there typically isn't enough time to exec into the container before it crashes again, and even if possible, this is a much less efficient and less scalable approach than centralized logging via Cloud Logging.
optionExplanations.D: Serial port (console) logs capture boot-time and OS-level kernel messages for the VM, not the stdout of containers running on Kubernetes; they would not contain the application's log output.

text: You are using a single Cloud SQL instance to serve your application from a specific zone. You want to introduce high availability. What should you do?

options.text: Create a read replica instance in a different region
Create a failover replica instance in a different region
Create a read replica instance in the same region, but in a different zone
Create a failover replica instance in the same region, but in a different zone

optionExplanations.A: A read replica is intended for offloading read traffic and reporting, not automatic failover - if the primary instance fails, a read replica does not automatically become the primary, so it does not provide high availability by itself; additionally, cross-region replicas add latency for synchronous-like protection.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud SQL failover replicas must be created in the same region as the primary instance (in a different zone) to support automatic failover; a 'failover replica in a different region' is not how Cloud SQL HA is configured and would not provide the expected automatic failover behavior.
optionExplanations.C: A read replica in a different zone improves read scalability and provides some resilience, but it does not provide automatic failover - manual intervention (and promotion) would be required, and during that process there would be downtime, so it does not constitute a true HA configuration.
optionExplanations.D: Configuring a Cloud SQL failover replica (HA configuration) in the same region but a different zone enables automatic failover - if the primary instance or its zone becomes unavailable, Cloud SQL automatically redirects traffic to the standby in another zone with minimal downtime, which is the correct way to introduce high availability for Cloud SQL.

text: Your company is running a stateless application on a Compute Engine instance. The application is used heavily during regular business hours and lightly outside of business hours. Users are reporting that the application is slow during peak hours. You need to optimize the application's performance. What should you do?

options.text: Create a snapshot of the existing disk. Create an instance template from the snapshot. Create an autoscaled managed instance group from the instance template.
Create a snapshot of the existing disk. Create a custom image from the snapshot. Create an autoscaled managed instance group from the custom image.
Create a custom image from the existing disk. Create an instance template from the custom image. Create an autoscaled managed instance group from the instance template.
Create an instance template from the existing disk. Create a custom image from the instance template. Create an autoscaled managed instance group from the custom image.

optionExplanations.A: A snapshot is a point-in-time backup of a disk and cannot be used directly to create an instance template - instance templates require a custom image (or existing disk/image reference), so this sequence is not technically valid as the basis for a managed instance group.
optionExplanations.B: While creating a custom image from a snapshot is technically possible, it is an extra, unnecessary step compared to creating the custom image directly from the existing disk; the more direct and Google-recommended path is to create the image straight from the disk.
optionExplanations.C: Creating a custom image directly from the existing disk captures the current application state and configuration, then creating an instance template from that image, and finally creating an autoscaled managed instance group, is the correct and most direct sequence to enable horizontal autoscaling for a stateless application to handle peak-hour load.
optionExplanations.D: An instance template cannot be created directly from a disk and then converted into a custom image - this reverses the correct relationship (images are used to create templates, not the other way around), making this sequence technically invalid.

text: Your web application has several VM instances running within a VPC. You want to restrict communications between instances to only the paths and ports you authorize, but you don't want to rely on static IP addresses or subnets because the app can autoscale. How should you restrict communications?

options.text: Use separate VPCs to restrict traffic
Use firewall rules based on network tags attached to the compute instances
Use Cloud DNS and only allow connections from authorized hostnames
Use service accounts and configure the web application to authorize particular service accounts to have access

optionExplanations.A: Splitting instances across separate VPCs adds significant networking complexity (requiring VPC peering or other connectivity) and does not, by itself, provide fine-grained port/path-level traffic restriction between instances; it's a heavier-handed and less flexible approach than firewall rules.
optionExplanations.B: Network tags can be attached to Compute Engine instances (including those in a managed instance group via instance templates) and firewall rules can target instances by tag rather than by IP address - this allows traffic restrictions to automatically apply to new instances as the group autoscales, without needing to update static IPs or subnet ranges, making it the correct approach.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud DNS provides name resolution but does not enforce network-level access control between instances; firewall rules, not DNS, are what restrict actual network communication paths and ports.
optionExplanations.D: While service accounts can be used as firewall rule targets/sources in GCP (an alternative to tags), the application itself authorizing based on service accounts is an application-layer control, not a network-layer restriction on communication paths and ports as requested; firewall rules based on tags are the more standard and direct VPC-level mechanism for this requirement.

text: You are using Cloud SQL as the database backend for a large CRM deployment. You want to scale as usage increases and ensure that you don't run out of storage, maintain 75% CPU usage cores, and keep replication lag below 60 seconds. What are the correct steps to meet your requirements?

options.text: 1. Enable automatic storage increase for the instance. 2. Create a Stackdriver alert when CPU usage exceeds 75%, and change the instance type to reduce CPU usage. 3. Create a Stackdriver alert for replication lag, and shard the database to reduce replication time.

1. Enable automatic storage increase for the instance. 2. Change the instance type to a 32-core machine type to keep CPU usage below 75%. 3. Create a Stackdriver alert for replication lag, and deploy memcache to reduce load on the master.

1. Create a Stackdriver alert when storage exceeds 75%, and increase the available storage on the instance to create more space. 2. Deploy memcached to reduce CPU load. 3. Change the instance type to a 32-core machine type to reduce replication lag.

1. Create a Stackdriver alert when storage exceeds 75%, and increase the available storage on the instance to create more space. 2. Deploy memcached to reduce CPU load. 3. Create a Stackdriver alert for replication lag, and change the instance type to a 32-core machine type to reduce replication lag.

optionExplanations.A: Enabling automatic storage increase directly addresses the 'don't run out of storage' requirement; alerting at 75% CPU and changing the instance type (vertical scaling to a larger machine) addresses sustained high CPU usage; and alerting on replication lag combined with sharding the database addresses the requirement to keep replication lag below 60 seconds by distributing write load - this set of steps maps each requirement to the correct corrective action.
optionExplanations.B: Proactively jumping to a 32-core machine type without first monitoring/alerting on actual CPU usage is not a measured, cost-effective scaling approach, and deploying memcache primarily reduces read load on the database rather than directly addressing replication lag in a targeted way (it does not fundamentally fix lag caused by write throughput or single-threaded replication).
optionExplanations.C: Alerting on storage at 75% (rather than enabling automatic storage increase) is more reactive and risks the instance going read-only if storage fills before the alert is actioned; deploying memcached primarily helps with read load, not CPU usage from writes; and changing to a 32-core machine to address replication lag conflates CPU capacity with replication lag, which is often caused by single-threaded replication processes that don't necessarily benefit linearly from more cores.
optionExplanations.D: Similar to C, relying on a storage alert instead of automatic storage increase is reactive, deploying memcached doesn't directly address CPU load tied to writes effectively, and addressing replication lag solely via instance resizing without considering sharding or other replication-specific tuning may not adequately resolve persistent lag issues.

text: You are tasked with building an online analytical processing (OLAP) marketing analytics and reporting tool. This requires a relational database that can operate on hundreds of terabytes of data. What is the Google-recommended tool for such applications?

options.text: Cloud Spanner, because it is globally distributed
Cloud SQL, because it is a fully managed relational database
Cloud Firestore, because it offers real-time synchronization across devices
BigQuery, because it is designed for large-scale processing of tabular data

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Spanner is designed for globally distributed, strongly consistent transactional (OLTP) workloads; while it can scale to large data volumes, it is not the recommended tool for OLAP-style analytical reporting at the hundreds-of-terabytes scale - that's BigQuery's purpose-built domain.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud SQL is a fully managed relational database for OLTP workloads (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server) but has storage limits (in the terabyte range per instance) and is not designed for OLAP analytical queries over hundreds of terabytes of data.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud Firestore is a NoSQL document database optimized for mobile/web app data synchronization and transactional workloads, not for OLAP analytical processing or relational/SQL-based reporting over massive datasets.
optionExplanations.D: BigQuery is Google's serverless, highly scalable enterprise data warehouse purpose-built for OLAP workloads, capable of running SQL analytics over petabytes of data with a columnar storage format optimized for large-scale tabular data processing - making it the Google-recommended tool for this marketing analytics and reporting use case.

text: You have deployed an application to Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE), and are using the Cloud SQL proxy container to make the Cloud SQL database available to the services running on Kubernetes. You are notified that the application is reporting database connection issues. Your company policies require a post- mortem. What should you do?

options.text: Use gcloud sql instances restart.
Validate that the Service Account used by the Cloud SQL proxy container still has the Cloud Build Editor role.
In the GCP Console, navigate to Stackdriver Logging. Consult logs for (GKE) and Cloud SQL.
In the GCP Console, navigate to Cloud SQL. Restore the latest backup. Use kubectl to restart all pods.

optionExplanations.A: Restarting the Cloud SQL instance is a disruptive action that could cause data loss or further downtime, and it does not help diagnose the root cause of the connection issue - jumping straight to a restart without investigation also conflicts with the post-mortem requirement to understand what happened.
optionExplanations.B: The Cloud SQL proxy requires the Cloud SQL Client IAM role (and Cloud SQL Admin API access), not the Cloud Build Editor role, which is unrelated to database connectivity - checking for this role would not help diagnose the issue.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud Logging (Stackdriver Logging) aggregates logs from both GKE (application/container logs) and Cloud SQL (including the Cloud SQL proxy logs and database logs); reviewing these logs allows you to identify the root cause of the connectivity issue without taking disruptive action, which satisfies both the troubleshooting need and the post-mortem requirement to document findings.
optionExplanations.D: Restoring from backup and restarting all pods is a drastic, disruptive action that could cause data loss and downtime, and it does not address diagnosing the actual cause of the connection issue, which is required for a proper post-mortem analysis.

text: Your company pushes batches of sensitive transaction data from its application server VMs to Cloud Pub/Sub for processing and storage. What is the Google- recommended way for your application to authenticate to the required Google Cloud services?

options.text: Ensure that VM service accounts are granted the appropriate Cloud Pub/Sub IAM roles.
Ensure that VM service accounts do not have access to Cloud Pub/Sub, and use VM access scopes to grant the appropriate Cloud Pub/Sub IAM roles.
Generate an OAuth2 access token for accessing Cloud Pub/Sub, encrypt it, and store it in Cloud Storage for access from each VM.
Create a gateway to Cloud Pub/Sub using a Cloud Function, and grant the Cloud Function service account the appropriate Cloud Pub/Sub IAM roles.

optionExplanations.A: Attaching a service account to the VM instances and granting that service account the appropriate Pub/Sub IAM roles (e.g., roles/pubsub.publisher) is the Google-recommended approach - it leverages Application Default Credentials automatically available to the VM, avoiding the need to manage and distribute long-lived credentials or keys.
optionExplanations.B: Access scopes are a legacy, instance-level mechanism that broadly define the API scope of OAuth tokens available to the VM but do not replace IAM roles for fine-grained permission control; relying on access scopes instead of IAM roles on the service account is not the recommended best practice and access scopes alone, without IAM permissions on the service account, would not grant access.
optionExplanations.C: Generating, encrypting, and manually distributing OAuth2 access tokens via Cloud Storage is an insecure, manual, and error-prone pattern that introduces credential management risk (tokens can expire, leak, or be mismanaged) - this is explicitly against Google's recommended practice of using service account-based Application Default Credentials.
optionExplanations.D: Introducing a Cloud Function as a Pub/Sub gateway adds unnecessary architectural complexity, latency, and an additional point of failure/cost when the VMs can authenticate directly to Pub/Sub using their own attached service account and IAM roles.

text: You want to establish a Compute Engine application in a single VPC across two regions. The application must communicate over VPN to an on- premises network. How should you deploy the VPN?

options.text: Use VPC Network Peering between the VPC and the on-premises network.
Expose the VPC to the on-premises network using IAM and VPC Sharing.
Create a global Cloud VPN Gateway with VPN tunnels from each region to the on-premises peer gateway.
Deploy Cloud VPN Gateway in each region. Ensure that each region has at least one VPN tunnel to the on-premises peer gateway.

optionExplanations.A: VPC Network Peering connects two VPC networks within Google Cloud (or to another GCP organization's VPC) - it is not used to connect a VPC to an on-premises network, which requires Cloud VPN or Cloud Interconnect.
optionExplanations.B: VPC Sharing (Shared VPC) allows multiple projects within an organization to share a common VPC network; it is unrelated to establishing connectivity to an on-premises network, and IAM controls access to resources, not network-level VPN connectivity.
optionExplanations.C: There is no 'global Cloud VPN Gateway' construct - Cloud VPN gateways are regional resources, so this option misrepresents the available product capabilities.
optionExplanations.D: Cloud VPN gateways are regional resources, so to connect a single VPC spanning two regions to an on-premises network, you deploy a Cloud VPN Gateway in each region with at least one VPN tunnel per region to the on-premises peer gateway - this ensures both regions have direct, low-latency connectivity to on-premises rather than backhauling traffic through a single region.

text: Your applications will be writing their logs to BigQuery for analysis. Each application should have its own table. Any logs older than 45 days should be removed. You want to optimize storage and follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.text: Configure the expiration time for your tables at 45 days
Make the tables time-partitioned, and configure the partition expiration at 45 days
Rely on BigQuery's default behavior to prune application logs older than 45 days
Create a script that uses the BigQuery command line tool (bq) to remove records older than 45 days

optionExplanations.A: Table expiration deletes the entire table (including all its data) after the specified time from creation - it does not selectively remove only rows/data older than 45 days while keeping the table and newer data, so it does not meet the requirement to retain recent logs while removing old ones.
optionExplanations.B: Configuring tables as time-partitioned (e.g., partitioned by ingestion time or a timestamp column) and setting the partition expiration to 45 days causes BigQuery to automatically delete data in partitions older than 45 days while keeping the table structure and recent data intact - this is the Google-recommended, storage-optimized, and maintenance-free approach to this requirement.
optionExplanations.C: BigQuery does not have a default behavior that automatically prunes data after a fixed number of days - data persists indefinitely unless expiration settings (table or partition expiration) are explicitly configured.
optionExplanations.D: Writing and maintaining a custom script to delete old records via DML (DELETE statements) using bq is operationally burdensome, can incur additional query costs, and is not the Google-recommended approach when built-in partition expiration accomplishes the same goal automatically and more efficiently.

text: You want your Google Kubernetes Engine cluster to automatically add or remove nodes based on CPU load. What should you do?

options.text: Configure a HorizontalPodAutoscaler with a target CPU usage. Enable the Cluster Autoscaler from the GCP Console.
Configure a HorizontalPodAutoscaler with a target CPU usage. Enable autoscaling on the managed instance group for the cluster using the gcloud command.
Create a deployment and set the maxUnavailable and maxSurge properties. Enable the Cluster Autoscaler using the gcloud command.
Create a deployment and set the maxUnavailable and maxSurge properties. Enable autoscaling on the cluster managed instance group from the GCP Console.

optionExplanations.A: A HorizontalPodAutoscaler (HPA) automatically adjusts the number of pod replicas based on observed CPU utilization (or other metrics), and the GKE Cluster Autoscaler automatically adds or removes nodes from the node pool based on the resource requests of pods that are pending or underutilized - together, these two mechanisms enable both pod-level and node-level (infrastructure) autoscaling driven by CPU load, which is exactly what's required.
optionExplanations.B: While HPA correctly scales pods based on CPU usage, manually enabling autoscaling on the underlying managed instance group via gcloud directly (bypassing the GKE Cluster Autoscaler) is not the recommended approach in GKE, because the Cluster Autoscaler is specifically designed to coordinate node scaling with pod scheduling needs - using raw MIG autoscaling can conflict with GKE's cluster management and is not how node autoscaling should be configured for GKE.
optionExplanations.C: maxUnavailable and maxSurge are properties of a Deployment's RollingUpdate strategy, controlling how pods are replaced during updates - they have nothing to do with scaling the number of pods based on CPU load, so this does not address the autoscaling requirement at the pod level.
optionExplanations.D: Similar to C, maxUnavailable/maxSurge govern rolling update behavior, not CPU-based autoscaling, and manually configuring the underlying instance group autoscaling from the Console rather than using GKE's Cluster Autoscaler is not the recommended way to scale GKE nodes.

text: You need to develop procedures to verify resilience of disaster recovery for remote recovery using GCP. Your production environment is hosted on- premises. You need to establish a secure, redundant connection between your on-premises network and the GCP network. What should you do?

options.text: Verify that Dedicated Interconnect can replicate files to GCP. Verify that direct peering can establish a secure connection between your networks if Dedicated Interconnect fails.
Verify that Dedicated Interconnect can replicate files to GCP. Verify that Cloud VPN can establish a secure connection between your networks if Dedicated Interconnect fails.
Verify that the Transfer Appliance can replicate files to GCP. Verify that direct peering can establish a secure connection between your networks if the Transfer Appliance fails.
Verify that the Transfer Appliance can replicate files to GCP. Verify that Cloud VPN can establish a secure connection between your networks if the Transfer Appliance fails.

optionExplanations.A: Dedicated Interconnect provides a private, high-throughput physical connection for network traffic, but it is not a file replication tool - describing it as something that 'replicates files' misrepresents its function; additionally, direct peering provides connectivity to Google's network for accessing Google APIs/services over the public internet, but it is not an encrypted, secure failover connection comparable to a VPN for connecting on-premises and GCP networks.
optionExplanations.B: Dedicated Interconnect provides a private, dedicated, high-bandwidth, low-latency connection between on-premises and GCP (used for ongoing data transfer/replication of workloads, not literally 'file replication' as a product feature, but functionally supports replication traffic), and Cloud VPN provides an encrypted IPsec tunnel over the internet that can serve as a secure backup connection if the Dedicated Interconnect link fails - this combination of a primary dedicated connection with a secure VPN failover is the standard Google-recommended resilient hybrid connectivity design.
optionExplanations.C: The Transfer Appliance is a physical device used for one-time, offline bulk data migration to GCP (shipped to Google and data uploaded from it) - it is not a connectivity solution and cannot serve as an ongoing 'connection' for disaster recovery replication, nor does direct peering provide a secure encrypted failover path.
optionExplanations.D: The Transfer Appliance is designed for one-time bulk data migration, not for an ongoing redundant connection used in disaster recovery testing - it is not a substitute for a network connectivity solution like Dedicated Interconnect, making this option's premise incorrect even though Cloud VPN as a failover mechanism is valid in concept.

text: Your company operates nationally and plans to use GCP for multiple batch workloads, including some that are not time-critical. You also need to use GCP services that are HIPAA-certified and manage service costs. How should you design to meet Google best practices?

options.text: Provision preemptible VMs to reduce cost. Discontinue use of all GCP services and APIs that are not HIPAA-compliant.
Provision preemptible VMs to reduce cost. Disable and then discontinue use of all GCP services and APIs that are not HIPAA-compliant.
Provision standard VMs in the same region to reduce cost. Discontinue use of all GCP services and APIs that are not HIPAA-compliant.
Provision standard VMs to the same region to reduce cost. Disable and then discontinue use of all GCP services and APIs that are not HIPAA-compliant.

optionExplanations.A: Preemptible VMs are appropriate for cost reduction on non-time-critical batch workloads, but simply 'discontinuing use' of non-HIPAA-compliant services without first disabling/decommissioning them properly could leave residual access or data exposure - the recommended practice is to disable and then discontinue, ensuring a controlled wind-down.
optionExplanations.B: Preemptible VMs are well-suited for non-time-critical batch workloads because they offer significant cost savings (up to ~80% cheaper) with the tradeoff of potential interruption, which is acceptable here; properly disabling (turning off access/use) and then discontinuing use of non-HIPAA-compliant GCP services and APIs ensures that protected health information (PHI) is never processed by services outside Google's HIPAA Business Associate Agreement (BAA) coverage, which is the correct, methodical approach to both cost management and compliance.
optionExplanations.C: Standard (non-preemptible) VMs cost more than preemptible VMs for workloads that can tolerate interruption, so this does not align with the goal of managing/reducing service costs for non-time-critical batch workloads.
optionExplanations.D: Using standard VMs instead of preemptible VMs for non-time-critical batch workloads misses an opportunity for significant cost savings, which conflicts with the stated goal of managing service costs.

text: Your customer wants to do resilience testing of their authentication layer. This consists of a regional managed instance group serving a public REST API that reads from and writes to a Cloud SQL instance. What should you do?

options.text: Engage with a security company to run web scrapers that look your for users' authentication data om malicious websites and notify you if any is found.
Deploy intrusion detection software to your virtual machines to detect and log unauthorized access.
Schedule a disaster simulation exercise during which you can shut off all VMs in a zone to see how your application behaves.
Configure a read replica for your Cloud SQL instance in a different zone than the master, and then manually trigger a failover while monitoring KPIs for our REST API.

optionExplanations.A: Engaging a security company to scan the dark web/malicious sites for leaked credentials addresses data breach detection, not the resilience (availability/fault-tolerance) of the authentication layer's infrastructure, so it does not address the stated resilience testing goal.
optionExplanations.B: Intrusion detection software helps detect security breaches and unauthorized access, which is a security monitoring concern, not a test of the system's resilience to infrastructure failures (e.g., zone outages) as implied by 'resilience testing.'
optionExplanations.C: Conducting a disaster simulation (chaos engineering style exercise) by shutting off all VMs in a zone directly tests whether the regional managed instance group and the overall architecture can withstand a zonal failure and continue serving the authentication API without downtime, which is precisely what 'resilience testing' of the authentication layer requires.
