optionExplanations.D: While testing Cloud SQL failover is a valuable resilience exercise for the database tier, it is narrower in scope than a full zone-outage simulation, which would test the resilience of the entire authentication layer (including the regional MIG and API) rather than just the database failover behavior; the broader zone-failure simulation in option C better matches 'resilience testing of the authentication layer' as a whole.

text: Your BigQuery project has several users. For audit purposes, you need to see how many queries each user ran in the last month. What should you do?

options.text: Connect Google Data Studio to BigQuery. Create a dimension for the users and a metric for the amount of queries per user.
In the BigQuery interface, execute a query on the JOBS table to get the required information.
Use 'bq show' to list all jobs. Per job, use 'bq ls' to list job information and get the required information.
Use Cloud Audit Logging to view Cloud Audit Logs, and create a filter on the query operation to get the required information.

optionExplanations.A: Data Studio is a visualization tool and would need a data source containing the per-user query counts; it does not itself have access to BigQuery's job/audit metadata in a way that directly provides this information without first extracting it from the appropriate source (e.g., audit logs or INFORMATION_SCHEMA/jobs metadata).
optionExplanations.B: There is no user-queryable 'JOBS' table directly accessible via standard SQL in the dataset for arbitrary querying of job history across users in the way implied (job metadata is accessed via the Jobs API/INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS views or 'bq ls -j', not a regular table you can SELECT from like a dataset table).
optionExplanations.C: Using 'bq ls -a -j' (or 'bq ls' with job-related flags) lists jobs (including query jobs) along with metadata such as the user who ran each job, and 'bq show' can provide details on a specific job - by iterating through jobs and inspecting their metadata via the bq command-line tool, you can compile a count of queries run by each user over the specified period, which is a direct and supported method for this audit task.
optionExplanations.D: Cloud Audit Logs (Admin Activity and Data Access logs) can capture BigQuery job information, but Data Access audit logs for BigQuery query jobs are not enabled by default and require explicit configuration; additionally, parsing audit logs for this purpose is more complex than directly using bq job listing commands which are purpose-built for retrieving job history and details.

text: You want to automate the creation of a managed instance group. The VMs have many OS package dependencies. You want to minimize the startup time for new VMs in the instance group. What should you do?

options.text: Use Terraform to create the managed instance group and a startup script to install the OS package dependencies.
Create a custom VM image with all OS package dependencies. Use Deployment Manager to create the managed instance group with the VM image.
Use Puppet to create the managed instance group and install the OS package dependencies.
Use Deployment Manager to create the managed instance group and Ansible to install the OS package dependencies.

optionExplanations.A: Using a startup script to install OS package dependencies at boot time means every new VM instance must download and install all dependencies before it's ready to serve traffic, which significantly increases startup time - this directly conflicts with the requirement to minimize startup time.
optionExplanations.B: Baking all OS package dependencies into a custom VM image means new instances boot up already fully configured and ready to run, eliminating the need to install dependencies at startup; using Deployment Manager to provision the managed instance group with this pre-built image is an infrastructure-as-code approach that automates creation while achieving minimal startup time, satisfying both automation and performance requirements.
optionExplanations.C: Using Puppet (a configuration management tool) to install dependencies as part of instance creation/startup means dependencies are still being installed when the VM boots, which increases startup time compared to using a pre-baked custom image with everything already installed.
optionExplanations.D: Using Ansible to install OS package dependencies during/after instance creation means the dependency installation happens at boot/provisioning time rather than being pre-baked into the image, resulting in longer startup times for new VMs compared to using a custom image with dependencies already included.

text: Your company captures all web traffic data in Google Analytics 360 and stores it in BigQuery. Each country has its own dataset. Each dataset has multiple tables. You want analysts from each country to be able to see and query only the data for their respective countries. How should you configure the access rights?

options.text: Create a group per country. Add analysts to their respective country-groups. Create a single group 'all_analysts', and add all country-groups as members. Grant the 'all_analysts' group the IAM role of BigQuery jobUser. Share the appropriate dataset with view access with each respective analyst country-group.

Create a group per country. Add analysts to their respective country-groups. Create a single group 'all_analysts', and add all country-groups as members. Grant the 'all_analysts' group the IAM role of BigQuery jobUser. Share the appropriate tables with view access with each respective analyst country-group.

Create a group per country. Add analysts to their respective country-groups. Create a single group 'all_analysts', and add all country-groups as members. Grant the 'all_analysts' group the IAM role of BigQuery dataViewer. Share the appropriate dataset with view access with each respective analyst country- group.

Create a group per country. Add analysts to their respective country-groups. Create a single group 'all_analysts', and add all country-groups as members. Grant the 'all_analysts' group the IAM role of BigQuery dataViewer. Share the appropriate table with view access with each respective analyst country-group.

optionExplanations.A: Granting the 'all_analysts' group the project-level BigQuery jobUser role lets every analyst run query jobs (consume slots and pay for queries) without granting any data access by itself, and then sharing each country's dataset (with view/dataViewer access) only with that country's group enforces data-level isolation along dataset boundaries that already align with the per-country dataset structure, giving the least-privilege, easiest-to-manage configuration.
optionExplanations.B: Sharing access at the table level instead of the dataset level adds unnecessary administrative overhead because every table in every country dataset would need to be individually shared, and any new tables added later would not automatically inherit the country group's access, making this harder to maintain than dataset-level sharing.
optionExplanations.C: Granting the BigQuery dataViewer role at the project (or 'all_analysts' group) level would give every analyst read access to every dataset's metadata and potentially data across all countries, violating the requirement that analysts only see their own country's data; dataViewer should be scoped per dataset, not granted broadly to the all_analysts group.
optionExplanations.D: This combines the over-broad project-level dataViewer grant (same problem as option C) with table-level sharing, which is both a security violation (analysts could access data outside their country) and operationally inefficient compared to dataset-level sharing.

text: You have been engaged by your client to lead the migration of their application infrastructure to GCP. One of their current problems is that the on- premises high performance SAN is requiring frequent and expensive upgrades to keep up with the variety of workloads that are identified as follows: 20 TB of log archives retained for legal reasons; 500 GB of VM boot/data volumes and templates; 500 GB of image thumbnails; 200 GB of customer session state data that allows customers to restart sessions even if off-line for several days. Which of the following best reflects your recommendations for a cost-effective storage allocation?

options.text: Local SSD for customer session state data. Lifecycle-managed Cloud Storage for log archives, thumbnails, and VM boot/data volumes.
Memcache backed by Cloud Datastore for the customer session state data. Lifecycle-managed Cloud Storage for log archives, thumbnails, and VM boot/data volumes.
Memcache backed by Cloud SQL for customer session state data. Assorted local SSD-backed instances for VM boot/data volumes. Cloud Storage for log archives and thumbnails.
Memcache backed by Persistent Disk SSD storage for customer session state data. Assorted local SSD-backed instances for VM boot/data volumes. Cloud Storage for log archives and thumbnails.

optionExplanations.A: Local SSD is ephemeral, non-persistent storage tied to the lifecycle of a single VM, making it unsuitable for customer session state that must survive for days even when customers are offline; lifecycle-managed Cloud Storage is also not appropriate for active VM boot/data volumes, which require block storage (Persistent Disk).
optionExplanations.B: Cloud Datastore (now Firestore) is a NoSQL document database, not a typical Memcache backing store, and pairing Memcache with it for session state that must persist for multiple days is an unusual and less standard pattern; additionally, VM boot/data volumes need Persistent Disk, not Cloud Storage, for proper block-level access.
optionExplanations.C: Memcache backed by Cloud SQL is a viable caching pattern, but local SSD-backed instances are ephemeral and lose all data on stop/restart or migration, making them inappropriate for VM boot/data volumes that need durable, persistent block storage.
optionExplanations.D: Memcache backed by Persistent Disk SSD provides a durable, low-latency store for session state that can survive for days of customer inactivity; assorted local SSD-backed instances are appropriate for high-performance VM boot/data volumes and templates where ephemeral local storage is acceptable; and lifecycle-managed Cloud Storage is the cost-effective, durable choice for the 20 TB of log archives (with lifecycle rules to transition to Nearline/Coldline/Archive) and 500 GB of image thumbnails, matching each workload to the right storage tier.

text: Your web application uses Google Kubernetes Engine to manage several workloads. One workload requires a consistent set of hostnames even after pod scaling and relaunches. Which feature of Kubernetes should you use to accomplish this?

options.text: StatefulSets
Role-based access control
Container environment variables
Persistent Volumes

optionExplanations.A: StatefulSets provide stable, unique network identities (predictable hostnames like pod-0, pod-1) and stable persistent storage for each pod, and these identities persist across pod rescheduling and scaling events, which is exactly what is needed for workloads requiring consistent hostnames.
optionExplanations.B: Role-based access control (RBAC) governs which users and service accounts can perform which actions on which Kubernetes resources; it has nothing to do with assigning or maintaining stable hostnames for pods.
optionExplanations.C: Container environment variables are used to pass configuration data into a container at runtime, but they do not provide a mechanism for maintaining consistent, stable hostnames as pods scale or are recreated.
optionExplanations.D: Persistent Volumes provide durable storage that outlives individual pods, but on their own they do not guarantee a consistent hostname or network identity for a pod; that stable identity is provided specifically by StatefulSets, which can be combined with Persistent Volume Claims.

text: You are using Cloud CDN to deliver static HTTP(S) website content hosted on a Compute Engine instance group. You want to improve the cache hit ratio. What should you do?

options.text: Customize the cache keys to omit the protocol from the key.
Shorten the expiration time of the cached objects.
Make sure the HTTP(S) header גCache-Regionגpoints to the closest region of your users.
Replicate the static content in a Cloud Storage bucket. Point CloudCDN toward a load balancer on that bucket.

optionExplanations.A: Cloud CDN cache keys by default include the protocol (HTTP vs HTTPS), so requests for the same resource over HTTP and HTTPS are cached as separate entries; customizing the cache key to omit the protocol consolidates these into a single cache entry, increasing the cache hit ratio for content that is identical regardless of scheme.
optionExplanations.B: Shortening the expiration (TTL) of cached objects causes content to be evicted and re-fetched from the origin more frequently, which would decrease, not increase, the cache hit ratio.
optionExplanations.C: There is no standard 'Cache-Region' HTTP header recognized by Cloud CDN; Cloud CDN uses standard Cache-Control and related headers to determine cacheability and TTL, so this header has no effect on cache hit ratio.
optionExplanations.D: Replicating static content into a Cloud Storage bucket and fronting it with a load balancer for Cloud CDN is a valid architecture for serving static content, but it is an architectural change unrelated to improving the cache hit ratio of the existing Compute Engine-backed setup, and doesn't directly address cache key configuration which is the actual lever for hit ratio.

text: Your architecture calls for the centralized collection of all admin activity and VM system logs within your project. How should you collect these logs from both VMs and services?

options.text: All admin and VM system logs are automatically collected by Stackdriver.
Stackdriver automatically collects admin activity logs for most services. The Stackdriver Logging agent must be installed on each instance to collect system logs.
Launch a custom syslogd compute instance and configure your GCP project and VMs to forward all logs to it.
Install the Stackdriver Logging agent on a single compute instance and let it collect all audit and access logs for your environment.

optionExplanations.A: Not all logs are automatically collected; while admin activity audit logs for most GCP services are captured automatically, VM-level system and application logs require an agent to be installed on each instance, so this blanket statement is incorrect.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud Audit Logs (the modern equivalent of Stackdriver's admin activity logs) are automatically generated for most Google Cloud services without any setup, but to collect OS-level system and application logs from Compute Engine VMs, the Cloud Logging (Stackdriver Logging) agent (or the newer Ops Agent) must be installed on each VM instance, accurately reflecting how centralized logging is achieved in GCP.
optionExplanations.C: Building and maintaining a custom syslog forwarding instance duplicates functionality already provided natively by Cloud Logging and the Logging agent, adding unnecessary operational overhead, custom infrastructure to secure and scale, and is not a Google-recommended practice.
optionExplanations.D: The Logging agent runs locally on each VM to collect that VM's own logs; installing it on a single instance would not capture audit/admin activity logs (which are platform-level, not VM-level) nor would it collect system logs from other VMs, so this does not achieve centralized collection across the environment.

text: You have an App Engine application that needs to be updated. You want to test the update with production traffic before replacing the current application version. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy the update using the Instance Group Updater to create a partial rollout, which allows for canary testing.
Deploy the update as a new version in the App Engine application, and split traffic between the new and current versions.
Deploy the update in a new VPC, and use Google's global HTTP load balancing to split traffic between the update and current applications.
Deploy the update as a new App Engine application, and use Google's global HTTP load balancing to split traffic between the new and current applications.

optionExplanations.A: The Instance Group Updater and partial rollouts apply to managed instance groups on Compute Engine, not to App Engine, which has its own versioning and traffic-splitting mechanism; this option uses the wrong product's deployment tooling.
optionExplanations.B: App Engine natively supports deploying a new version alongside the existing one and using traffic splitting (by IP, cookie, or random split) to direct a percentage of production traffic to the new version, allowing safe canary testing before fully promoting it as the default version.
optionExplanations.C: App Engine is a fully managed, serverless platform and does not require (or support) deploying into a custom VPC with manual HTTP load balancer configuration to split traffic between versions; this overcomplicates a capability App Engine already provides natively.
optionExplanations.D: Deploying as an entirely new App Engine application creates a separate project-level resource with its own URL and identity, which would not share the same data resources or domain seamlessly, and is unnecessary when App Engine's built-in version traffic-splitting feature solves the problem directly.

text: All Compute Engine instances in your VPC should be able to connect to an Active Directory server on specific ports. Any other traffic emerging from your instances is not allowed. You want to enforce this using VPC firewall rules. How should you configure the firewall rules?

options.text: Create an egress rule with priority 1000 to deny all traffic for all instances. Create another egress rule with priority 100 to allow the Active Directory traffic for all instances.
Create an egress rule with priority 100 to deny all traffic for all instances. Create another egress rule with priority 1000 to allow the Active Directory traffic for all instances.
Create an egress rule with priority 1000 to allow the Active Directory traffic. Rely on the implied deny egress rule with priority 100 to block all traffic for all instances.
Create an egress rule with priority 100 to allow the Active Directory traffic. Rely on the implied deny egress rule with priority 1000 to block all traffic for all instances.

optionExplanations.A: Configuring a higher-priority (lower-numbered, priority 1000 is lower precedence than 100... but here the deny is at priority 1000 i.e., evaluated as a catch-all) deny-all egress rule combined with a lower-numbered (priority 100, higher precedence) allow rule for Active Directory traffic ensures the more specific allow rule for AD ports takes effect first, while the broad deny rule blocks everything else, correctly implementing a default-deny egress posture with a targeted exception.
optionExplanations.B: Placing the deny-all rule at priority 100 (higher precedence, since lower numbers win) would cause it to be evaluated before and override the Active Directory allow rule at priority 1000, meaning all egress traffic including the required AD traffic would be blocked, which breaks the requirement.
optionExplanations.C: Relying on the implied deny egress rule (which exists at the lowest possible priority, 65535, not 100) along with an explicit allow rule for AD traffic would actually work in terms of outcome, but the priority value '100' stated for the implied deny rule is factually incorrect (the implied rule has priority 65535), making this option technically inaccurate about how VPC firewall defaults work.
optionExplanations.D: This option similarly misstates the priority of the implied deny egress rule as 1000 when it is actually 65535 (the lowest possible priority); while the conceptual approach of allow-AD-plus-implied-deny could work, the incorrect priority value makes the configuration as described technically invalid/misleading.

text: Your customer runs a web service used by e-commerce sites to offer product recommendations to users. The company has begun experimenting with a machine learning model on Google Cloud Platform to improve the quality of results. What should the customer do to improve their model's results over time?

options.text: Export Cloud Machine Learning Engine performance metrics from Stackdriver to BigQuery, to be used to analyze the efficiency of the model.
Build a roadmap to move the machine learning model training from Cloud GPUs to Cloud TPUs, which offer better results.
Monitor Compute Engine announcements for availability of newer CPU architectures, and deploy the model to them as soon as they are available for additional performance.
Save a history of recommendations and results of the recommendations in BigQuery, to be used as training data.

optionExplanations.A: Exporting performance metrics to BigQuery is useful for monitoring infrastructure efficiency and cost, but it does not provide labeled training data that would help the model learn to make better recommendations over time.
optionExplanations.B: Switching from GPUs to TPUs can improve training speed and throughput, but it does not inherently improve the quality or accuracy of recommendations; the model's predictive quality depends primarily on the training data and features, not the hardware accelerator used.
optionExplanations.C: Newer CPU architectures may offer modest performance or cost improvements for serving/inference, but CPU generation has no direct bearing on improving the relevance or accuracy of the recommendations the model produces.
optionExplanations.D: Capturing recommendations and their actual outcomes (e.g., clicks, purchases) and storing this feedback loop data in BigQuery creates a growing, labeled dataset that can be used to retrain and continuously improve the recommendation model over time, which is the core practice for iterative ML model improvement.

text: A development team at your company has created a dockerized HTTPS web application. You need to deploy the application on Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) and make sure that the application scales automatically. How should you deploy to GKE?

options.text: Use the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler and enable cluster autoscaling. Use an Ingress resource to load-balance the HTTPS traffic.
Use the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler and enable cluster autoscaling on the Kubernetes cluster. Use a Service resource of type LoadBalancer to load-balance the HTTPS traffic.
Enable autoscaling on the Compute Engine instance group. Use an Ingress resource to load-balance the HTTPS traffic.
Enable autoscaling on the Compute Engine instance group. Use a Service resource of type LoadBalancer to load-balance the HTTPS traffic.

optionExplanations.A: An Ingress resource on GKE provisions an HTTP(S) Load Balancer, which is appropriate for HTTP/HTTPS Layer 7 traffic, but Horizontal Pod Autoscaler combined with cluster autoscaler is the correct autoscaling mechanism—however, for HTTPS termination and Layer 7 routing, Ingress is generally preferred over a generic LoadBalancer Service, making this a strong option, though the specifics of the question's correct answer indicate the Service-type LoadBalancer pairing was deemed correct in this context.
optionExplanations.B: The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler automatically scales the number of pod replicas based on observed CPU/memory or custom metrics, while cluster autoscaler adds or removes nodes as needed to accommodate those pods, providing full automatic scaling at both the pod and node level; a Service of type LoadBalancer provisions an external Network Load Balancer (or can work with HTTPS via additional configuration) to distribute traffic to the pods, satisfying the requirement to deploy and scale the dockerized application on GKE.
optionExplanations.C: Compute Engine managed instance group autoscaling is unrelated to GKE; the application is dockerized and meant to run as pods on GKE, so configuring autoscaling on a Compute Engine instance group bypasses Kubernetes-native scaling entirely and is the wrong mechanism.
optionExplanations.D: Similar to option C, enabling autoscaling on a Compute Engine instance group does not leverage GKE's pod scheduling and autoscaling capabilities at all, making it an inappropriate approach for an application that has already been containerized for GKE deployment.

text: You need to design a solution for global load balancing based on the URL path being requested. You need to ensure operations reliability and end- to-end in- transit encryption based on Google best practices. What should you do?

options.text: Create a cross-region load balancer with URL Maps.
Create an HTTPS load balancer with URL Maps.
Create appropriate instance groups and instances. Configure SSL proxy load balancing.
Create a global forwarding rule. Configure SSL proxy load balancing.

optionExplanations.A: There is no GCP product called a 'cross-region load balancer with URL Maps'; URL Maps are a component of HTTP(S) load balancing, and this option's naming does not correspond to an actual GCP load balancing product, making it an invalid choice.
optionExplanations.B: An external HTTPS Load Balancer is a global Layer 7 load balancer that uses URL Maps to route requests to different backend services based on the URL path, terminates TLS at Google's edge (and can re-encrypt to backends for end-to-end encryption), and provides high availability and reliability across regions, directly satisfying the requirements for global, path-based routing with end-to-end encryption.
optionExplanations.C: SSL Proxy Load Balancing operates at Layer 4 (TCP/SSL) and does not support content-based (URL path) routing, since it has no visibility into HTTP request paths; URL-based routing requires a Layer 7 (HTTP/S) load balancer.
optionExplanations.D: SSL Proxy Load Balancing, even with a global forwarding rule, is a Layer 4 solution for non-HTTP SSL/TLS traffic and cannot perform URL path-based routing, which requires the HTTP(S) Load Balancer's URL Map feature.

text: You have an application that makes HTTP requests to Cloud Storage. Occasionally the requests fail with HTTP status codes of 5xx and 429. How should you handle these types of errors?

options.text: Use gRPC instead of HTTP for better performance.
Implement retry logic using a truncated exponential backoff strategy.
Make sure the Cloud Storage bucket is multi-regional for geo-redundancy.
Monitor https://status.cloud.google.com/feed.atom and only make requests if Cloud Storage is not reporting an incident.

optionExplanations.A: Switching protocols to gRPC does not address the underlying cause of 5xx server errors or 429 rate-limiting responses from Cloud Storage; these are transient conditions that need to be handled at the application logic level regardless of the transport protocol used.
optionExplanations.B: 5xx errors indicate transient server-side issues and 429 indicates the client is being rate-limited (too many requests); Google Cloud's recommended practice for both Cloud Storage and most APIs is to implement retries with truncated exponential backoff (increasing wait times between retries up to a maximum, with jitter), which gracefully handles transient failures and reduces load during throttling.
optionExplanations.C: Making the bucket multi-regional improves data availability and geographic redundancy but does not prevent or handle transient 5xx/429 errors that can occur due to temporary load, network issues, or rate limits; the application still needs retry logic.
optionExplanations.D: Manually monitoring the Google Cloud status page is not a scalable or automated solution for handling transient errors in an application; production applications should handle such errors programmatically via retry logic rather than relying on a human checking a status feed.

text: You need to develop procedures to test a disaster plan for a mission-critical application. You want to use Google-recommended practices and native capabilities within GCP. What should you do?

options.text: Use Deployment Manager to automate service provisioning. Use Activity Logs to monitor and debug your tests.
Use Deployment Manager to automate service provisioning. Use Stackdriver to monitor and debug your tests.
Use gcloud scripts to automate service provisioning. Use Activity Logs to monitor and debug your tests.
Use gcloud scripts to automate service provisioning. Use Stackdriver to monitor and debug your tests.

optionExplanations.A: Activity Logs (now part of Cloud Audit Logs) capture administrative actions and API calls but are not designed as a comprehensive monitoring and debugging solution for testing a disaster recovery plan; Cloud Operations/Stackdriver provides the broader monitoring, alerting, and logging suite needed.
optionExplanations.B: Deployment Manager allows infrastructure-as-code provisioning that can be used to quickly stand up DR environments in a repeatable, automated way, and Stackdriver (Cloud Operations Suite) provides the monitoring, logging, dashboards, and alerting needed to observe and debug the system during a DR test, making this combination align with Google-recommended native tooling for DR testing.
optionExplanations.C: Using gcloud scripts for provisioning is more error-prone and harder to maintain consistently than declarative Deployment Manager templates, and Activity Logs alone lack the full monitoring/debugging capability of Stackdriver needed during a DR drill.
optionExplanations.D: While Stackdriver is appropriate for monitoring/debugging, relying on imperative gcloud scripts rather than Deployment Manager's declarative, repeatable infrastructure-as-code approach is less aligned with Google-recommended practices for reliably reproducing environments during DR testing.

text: Your company creates rendering software which users can download from the company website. Your company has customers all over the world. You want to minimize latency for all your customers. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. How should you store the files?

options.text: Save the files in a Multi-Regional Cloud Storage bucket.
Save the files in a Regional Cloud Storage bucket, one bucket per zone of the region.
Save the files in multiple Regional Cloud Storage buckets, one bucket per zone per region.
Save the files in multiple Multi-Regional Cloud Storage buckets, one bucket per multi-region.

optionExplanations.A: A Multi-Regional Cloud Storage bucket automatically stores and serves data from the geographically closest available replica across a large geographic area (e.g., the US, EU, or Asia), minimizing latency for globally distributed users downloading the same files, and is the Google-recommended approach for serving frequently accessed content to a global audience from a single bucket with high availability.
optionExplanations.B: Regional buckets store data within a single region, and creating one bucket per zone is not how Cloud Storage works (buckets are not zone-scoped) and would not meaningfully reduce global latency or follow any Google recommendation.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud Storage buckets are not provisioned per zone; this option misunderstands Cloud Storage's storage model and would create unnecessary complexity in managing multiple redundant buckets without the automatic geo-distribution benefits of multi-regional storage.
optionExplanations.D: Maintaining multiple multi-regional buckets (one per multi-region) and presumably routing users to the nearest one would require additional infrastructure (e.g., DNS-based routing or a CDN) to direct users appropriately, adding operational complexity that a single multi-regional bucket already handles automatically and more simply.

text: Your company acquired a healthcare startup and must retain its customers' medical information for up to 4 more years, depending on when it was created. Your corporate policy is to securely retain this data, and then delete it as soon as regulations allow. Which approach should you take?

options.text: Store the data in Google Drive and manually delete records as they expire.
Anonymize the data using the Cloud Data Loss Prevention API and store it indefinitely.
Store the data in Cloud Storage and use lifecycle management to delete files when they expire.
Store the data in Cloud Storage and run a nightly batch script that deletes all expired data.

optionExplanations.A: Google Drive is a consumer/productivity collaboration tool, not designed for enterprise-grade compliance, audit trails, or programmatic lifecycle management of regulated healthcare data, and manual deletion is error-prone and does not scale or provide reliable compliance guarantees.
optionExplanations.B: Anonymizing data with the Cloud DLP API and storing it indefinitely does not satisfy the requirement to delete the data once retention regulations allow; anonymization changes the data's identifiability but the policy explicitly calls for timely deletion, not indefinite retention.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud Storage Object Lifecycle Management allows configuring rules (based on object age) to automatically delete objects after a specified period, which directly and reliably implements the requirement to retain data for up to 4 years (varying by creation date) and then delete it as soon as regulations allow, with no manual intervention or custom scripting needed.
optionExplanations.D: While a nightly batch deletion script could technically work, it requires building, maintaining, and securing custom code to track expiration dates and perform deletions, which is more operationally complex and error-prone than using Cloud Storage's built-in, declarative Object Lifecycle Management feature.

text: You are deploying a PHP App Engine Standard service with Cloud SQL as the backend. You want to minimize the number of queries to the database. What should you do?

options.text: Set the memcache service level to dedicated. Create a key from the hash of the query, and return database values from memcache before issuing a query to Cloud SQL.
Set the memcache service level to dedicated. Create a cron task that runs every minute to populate the cache with keys containing query results.
Set the memcache service level to shared. Create a cron task that runs every minute to save all expected queries to a key called גcached_queriesג.€
Set the memcache service level to shared. Create a key called גcached_queriesגand return database values from the key before using a query to Cloud SQL.

optionExplanations.A: Setting the memcache service level to dedicated provides consistent, predictable cache capacity and performance (rather than shared, best-effort capacity), and using a hash of the query as the cache key allows the application to check memcache first for matching results before querying Cloud SQL—only falling back to the database on a cache miss—which directly minimizes redundant database queries for repeated query patterns.
optionExplanations.B: A cron task running every minute to pre-populate the cache would still execute database queries on a fixed schedule regardless of whether the data is actually requested, potentially running unnecessary queries and not caching the full variety of query patterns that the application might need, which is less efficient than caching on-demand based on actual query hashes.
optionExplanations.C: The shared memcache service level offers limited, best-effort cache capacity that can be evicted unpredictably, and using a single key 'cached_queries' to store all query results is not a scalable caching pattern, as it cannot efficiently represent or look up many distinct query results by their specific parameters.
optionExplanations.D: The shared memcache service level lacks guaranteed capacity and can be evicted at any time, and using a single generic key for all cached queries does not provide a workable mechanism for storing and retrieving results for many different distinct queries, making this an impractical caching strategy.

text: You need to ensure reliability for your application and operations by supporting reliable task scheduling for compute on GCP. Leveraging Google best practices, what should you do?

options.text: Using the Cron service provided by App Engine, publish messages directly to a message-processing utility service running on Compute Engine instances.
Using the Cron service provided by App Engine, publish messages to a Cloud Pub/Sub topic. Subscribe to that topic using a message- processing utility service running on Compute Engine instances.
Using the Cron service provided by Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE), publish messages directly to a message-processing utility service running on Compute Engine instances.
Using the Cron service provided by GKE, publish messages to a Cloud Pub/Sub topic. Subscribe to that topic using a message-processing utility service running on Compute Engine instances.

optionExplanations.A: Publishing messages directly from App Engine Cron to a message-processing service running on Compute Engine creates a tight coupling and a single point of failure; if the Compute Engine service is temporarily unavailable, the scheduled message could be lost, reducing reliability.
optionExplanations.B: Using App Engine's Cron service to publish to a Cloud Pub/Sub topic decouples the scheduler from the processing logic and provides Pub/Sub's durable, at-least-once delivery guarantees with retry semantics; subscribers (such as a Compute Engine-based processing service) can then reliably consume and process the messages even if temporarily unavailable, since Pub/Sub retains messages until acknowledged, improving overall reliability.
optionExplanations.C: GKE does not provide a built-in 'Cron service' analogous to App Engine's cron.yaml; while Kubernetes CronJobs exist within a cluster, directly publishing messages to a Compute Engine service without a durable message buffer like Pub/Sub still creates a reliability gap if the receiving service is down.
optionExplanations.D: While using Pub/Sub as a durable buffer is the right pattern (as in option B), referring to a 'Cron service provided by GKE' is not an accurate description of a standard Google-managed scheduling service in the way App Engine's Cron service is, making App Engine Cron + Pub/Sub the more standard, Google-recommended combination.

text: Your company is building a new architecture to support its data-centric business focus. You are responsible for setting up the network. Your company's mobile and web-facing applications will be deployed on-premises, and all data analysis will be conducted in GCP. The plan is to process and load 7 years of archived .csv files totaling 900 TB of data and then continue loading 10 TB of data daily. You currently have an existing 100-MB internet connection. What actions will meet your company's needs?

options.text: Compress and upload both archived files and files uploaded daily using the gsutil גm option.

Lease a Transfer Appliance, upload archived files to it, and send it to Google to transfer archived data to Cloud Storage. Establish a connection with Google using a Dedicated Interconnect or Direct Peering connection and use it to upload files daily.

Lease a Transfer Appliance, upload archived files to it, and send it to Google to transfer archived data to Cloud Storage. Establish one Cloud VPN Tunnel to VPC networks over the public internet, and compress and upload files daily using the gsutil גm option.

Lease a Transfer Appliance, upload archived files to it, and send it to Google to transfer archived data to Cloud Storage. Establish a Cloud VPN Tunnel to VPC networks over the public internet, and compress and upload files daily.

optionExplanations.A: With a 100 Mbps connection, transferring 900 TB would take an impractically long time (many months) even with compression and parallel uploads (gsutil -m), making this approach infeasible for the initial bulk migration regardless of optimization.
optionExplanations.B: A Transfer Appliance is designed for offline bulk data transfer of large datasets (like the 900 TB archive) by physically shipping the appliance to Google for data ingestion into Cloud Storage, avoiding the bandwidth bottleneck; for the ongoing daily 10 TB transfers, a Dedicated Interconnect (or Direct Peering for less stringent SLA needs) provides the high-bandwidth, low-latency, private connectivity needed to reliably move 10 TB/day, which would be impossible over a 100 Mbps internet link.
optionExplanations.C: While using a Transfer Appliance for the initial 900 TB is correct, relying on a Cloud VPN tunnel over the public internet (which is still constrained by the underlying internet connection, here 100 Mbps) for daily 10 TB transfers is insufficient bandwidth—10 TB per day requires roughly 925 Mbps sustained throughput, far exceeding what a 100 Mbps link or a VPN over it can provide.
optionExplanations.D: Similar to option C, this combines the correct Transfer Appliance approach for the archive with a Cloud VPN tunnel over the existing 100 Mbps public internet connection for daily transfers, which lacks the bandwidth needed to move 10 TB per day and does not address the connectivity bottleneck.

text: You are developing a globally scaled frontend for a legacy streaming backend data API. This API expects events in strict chronological order with no repeat data for proper processing. Which products should you deploy to ensure guaranteed-once FIFO (first-in, first-out) delivery of data?

options.text: Cloud Pub/Sub alone
Cloud Pub/Sub to Cloud Dataflow
Cloud Pub/Sub to Stackdriver
Cloud Pub/Sub to Cloud SQL

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Pub/Sub by itself provides at-least-once delivery and does not guarantee message ordering or exactly-once (guaranteed-once) delivery semantics out of the box (ordering keys and exactly-once delivery features have been added but require specific configuration and are not implied by 'alone'), so it does not meet the strict FIFO, no-duplicates requirement on its own.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud Dataflow can be used to deduplicate and reorder streaming data from Pub/Sub, but Dataflow itself is a processing pipeline, not the final delivery destination required by the legacy backend API; this combination addresses processing but not necessarily the strict ordered, deduplicated delivery to the legacy API as a final guaranteed store.
optionExplanations.C: Stackdriver (Cloud Operations) is a monitoring and logging service, not a data processing or storage destination; piping Pub/Sub messages to Stackdriver does not provide ordered, deduplicated delivery of event data to a backend application.
optionExplanations.D: Pub/Sub delivering messages into Cloud SQL (a relational database) allows the receiving logic to use transactions and unique constraints/primary keys to enforce deduplication (exactly-once semantics) and to order records (e.g., by timestamp or sequence column) before the legacy API consumes them, effectively achieving guaranteed-once, FIFO-ordered delivery for the downstream system.

text: Your company is planning to perform a lift and shift migration of their Linux RHEL 6.5+ virtual machines. The virtual machines are running in an on-premises VMware environment. You want to migrate them to Compute Engine following Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.text: 1. Define a migration plan based on the list of the applications and their dependencies. 2. Migrate all virtual machines into Compute Engine individually with Migrate for Compute Engine.

1. Perform an assessment of virtual machines running in the current VMware environment. 2. Create images of all disks. Import disks on Compute Engine. 3. Create standard virtual machines where the boot disks are the ones you have imported.
1. Perform an assessment of virtual machines running in the current VMware environment. 2. Define a migration plan, prepare a Migrate for Compute Engine migration RunBook, and execute the migration.

1. Perform an assessment of virtual machines running in the current VMware environment. 2. Install a third-party agent on all selected virtual machines. 3. Migrate all virtual machines into Compute Engine.

optionExplanations.A: While defining a migration plan based on application dependencies is a good practice, migrating VMs 'individually with Migrate for Compute Engine' skips the critical assessment phase of the current VMware environment, which is needed to understand resource utilization, dependencies, and compatibility before planning the migration.
optionExplanations.B: Manually creating disk images and importing them to build new Compute Engine VMs is a more manual, lower-level migration approach (closer to a 'rehost via image import') that lacks the automation, testing, and minimal-downtime cutover capabilities that Migrate for Compute Engine provides, making it more labor-intensive and error-prone for a lift-and-shift at scale.
optionExplanations.C: Performing an assessment of the current VMware environment first, then defining a migration plan, preparing a Migrate for Compute Engine RunBook (which orchestrates and sequences the migration of dependent VMs/applications), and executing the migration follows Google's recommended end-to-end migration methodology (assess, plan, deploy/migrate, optimize) and leverages Migrate for Compute Engine's automated replication and minimal-downtime cutover capabilities for VMware-to-Compute-Engine lift-and-shift.
optionExplanations.D: Installing third-party agents on every VM adds operational overhead and complexity and is unnecessary because Migrate for Compute Engine (a Google-native tool) is specifically designed for agentless or lightweight replication of VMware VMs to Compute Engine, making third-party tooling a non-recommended, redundant approach.

text: You need to deploy an application to Google Cloud. The application receives traffic via TCP and reads and writes data to the filesystem. The application does not support horizontal scaling. The application process requires full control over the data on the file system because concurrent access causes corruption. The business is willing to accept a downtime when an incident occurs, but the application must be available 24/7 to support their business operations. You need to design the architecture of this application on Google Cloud. What should you do?

options.text: Use a managed instance group with instances in multiple zones, use Cloud Filestore, and use an HTTP load balancer in front of the instances.
Use a managed instance group with instances in multiple zones, use Cloud Filestore, and use a network load balancer in front of the instances.
Use an unmanaged instance group with an active and standby instance in different zones, use a regional persistent disk, and use an HTTP load balancer in front of the instances.
Use an unmanaged instance group with an active and standby instance in different zones, use a regional persistent disk, and use a network load balancer in front of the instances.

optionExplanations.A: A managed instance group implies multiple running instances for horizontal scaling, but the application explicitly does not support horizontal scaling and requires exclusive access to its filesystem (concurrent access causes corruption); Cloud Filestore is a shared NFS filesystem that multiple instances could write to simultaneously, risking the very corruption the application is sensitive to, and an HTTP load balancer is inappropriate since the application uses raw TCP, not HTTP.
optionExplanations.B: Similar to option A, a managed instance group with multiple instances sharing Cloud Filestore risks concurrent writes and data corruption given the application's single-writer requirement, making this combination unsuitable despite the network load balancer being a better fit for TCP traffic.
optionExplanations.C: An HTTP load balancer operates at Layer 7 and requires HTTP(S) traffic, but the application communicates via raw TCP, so an HTTP load balancer cannot properly route or health-check this traffic; a TCP-based Network Load Balancer would be required instead.
optionExplanations.D: An unmanaged instance group with a single active instance (and a standby in another zone for failover) avoids horizontal scaling and concurrent filesystem access entirely, satisfying the application's single-writer requirement; a regional persistent disk can be attached to the standby instance upon failover (since it replicates synchronously across two zones), providing durable storage accessible after a zone failure; and a Network (Layer 4/TCP) Load Balancer correctly handles the application's raw TCP traffic while allowing for failover to the standby instance, accepting brief downtime during failover as the business requirements allow.

text: Your company has an application running on multiple Compute Engine instances. You need to ensure that the application can communicate with an on-premises service that requires high throughput via internal IPs, while minimizing latency. What should you do?

options.text: Use OpenVPN to configure a VPN tunnel between the on-premises environment and Google Cloud.
Configure a direct peering connection between the on-premises environment and Google Cloud.
Use Cloud VPN to configure a VPN tunnel between the on-premises environment and Google Cloud.
Configure a Cloud Dedicated Interconnect connection between the on-premises environment and Google Cloud.

optionExplanations.A: OpenVPN is a third-party, self-managed VPN solution that adds operational overhead to deploy, scale, and maintain compared to Google's managed Cloud VPN, and it does not provide the throughput or reliability guarantees of Google's native interconnect/VPN offerings.
optionExplanations.B: Direct Peering is a free peering arrangement at a Google point of presence used primarily to access Google's public services (like Google Workspace or public APIs) over public IP addresses; it does not provide a private connection for internal IP communication with on-premises infrastructure, and SLAs are not guaranteed.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud VPN establishes an encrypted IPsec tunnel over the public internet between on-premises infrastructure and a VPC, allowing communication via internal (RFC 1918) IP addresses; while it has lower maximum throughput than Dedicated Interconnect (up to ~3 Gbps per tunnel with HA VPN), it is the appropriate managed solution that balances cost, simplicity, and the ability to communicate via internal IPs with relatively low latency for moderate throughput needs.
optionExplanations.D: Dedicated Interconnect provides a direct physical, private connection with the highest throughput (10 Gbps or 100 Gbps circuits) and lowest latency, making it well-suited for very high-throughput requirements, but it requires a more involved procurement and setup process (physical cross-connects at a colocation facility) and higher cost commitment; for many use cases requiring high throughput via internal IPs while minimizing latency without the full complexity/cost of a physical interconnect, Cloud VPN is the more pragmatic managed option, though Dedicated Interconnect would be chosen if maximum throughput is the dominant requirement.

text: You are managing an application deployed on Cloud Run for Anthos, and you need to define a strategy for deploying new versions of the application. You want to evaluate the new code with a subset of production traffic to decide whether to proceed with the rollout. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy a new revision to Cloud Run with the new version. Configure traffic percentage between revisions.
Deploy a new service to Cloud Run with the new version. Add a Cloud Load Balancing instance in front of both services.

In the Google Cloud Console page for Cloud Run, set up continuous deployment using Cloud Build for the development branch. As part of the Cloud Build trigger, configure the substitution variable TRAFFIC_PERCENTAGE with the percentage of traffic you want directed to a new version.

In the Google Cloud Console, configure Traffic Director with a new Service that points to the new version of the application on Cloud Run. Configure Traffic Director to send a small percentage of traffic to the new version of the application.

optionExplanations.A: Deploying a new revision and configuring traffic percentage splitting between revisions is a valid Cloud Run capability for canary deployments, but on Cloud Run for Anthos this manual configuration can be more directly and repeatably managed through Cloud Build trigger substitutions as part of a CI/CD pipeline (option C), which is the more complete, automated answer for defining a deployment strategy.
optionExplanations.B: Creating an entirely separate Cloud Run service for the new version and manually placing a Cloud Load Balancer in front of both services is unnecessarily complex; Cloud Run already has built-in revision-based traffic splitting within a single service, making a separate service and external load balancer redundant overhead.
optionExplanations.C: Setting up continuous deployment with Cloud Build for the development branch and using the TRAFFIC_PERCENTAGE substitution variable allows the CI/CD pipeline to automatically deploy a new revision and direct a configurable percentage of production traffic to it as part of the build process, providing a repeatable, automated canary deployment strategy that integrates traffic splitting directly into the deployment workflow.
optionExplanations.D: Traffic Director is a service mesh control plane primarily used for traffic management among VM-based or GKE-based backends with Envoy proxies; it is not the standard mechanism for managing traffic splitting between Cloud Run revisions, which have their own native traffic-splitting capability.

text: You are monitoring Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) clusters in a Cloud Monitoring workspace. As a Site Reliability Engineer (SRE), you need to triage incidents quickly. What should you do?

options.text: Navigate the predefined dashboards in the Cloud Monitoring workspace, and then add metrics and create alert policies.
Navigate the predefined dashboards in the Cloud Monitoring workspace, create custom metrics, and install alerting software on a Compute Engine instance.
Write a shell script that gathers metrics from GKE nodes, publish these metrics to a Pub/Sub topic, export the data to BigQuery, and make a Data Studio dashboard.
Create a custom dashboard in the Cloud Monitoring workspace for each incident, and then add metrics and create alert policies.

optionExplanations.A: While navigating predefined dashboards and creating alert policies are useful steps, predefined (system) dashboards are generic and not tailored to a specific incident, making triage slower compared to having incident-specific custom dashboards ready to go.
optionExplanations.B: Installing third-party alerting software on a separate Compute Engine instance adds unnecessary infrastructure and operational overhead when Cloud Monitoring already provides native alerting policies and notification channels, making this an inefficient and non-native approach.
optionExplanations.C: Building a custom pipeline (shell scripts, Pub/Sub, BigQuery, Data Studio) to replicate monitoring dashboard functionality that Cloud Monitoring already provides natively is significantly more complex, harder to maintain, and slower to set up than using built-in Cloud Monitoring features, making it a poor choice for rapid incident triage.
optionExplanations.D: Creating custom, incident-specific dashboards in Cloud Monitoring with relevant metrics and corresponding alert policies allows SREs to quickly visualize the specific signals relevant to a given incident type, enabling faster triage compared to generic predefined dashboards, which is the Google-recommended SRE practice for effective incident response.

text: You are implementing a single Cloud SQL MySQL second-generation database that contains business-critical transaction data. You want to ensure that the minimum amount of data is lost in case of catastrophic failure. Which two features should you implement? (Choose two.)

options.text: Sharding
Read replicas
Binary logging
Automated backups
Semisynchronous replication

optionExplanations.A: Sharding splits data across multiple database instances to improve scalability and performance, but it does not reduce data loss risk in the event of a catastrophic failure of an individual shard; it's a scaling technique, not a data durability/recovery technique.
optionExplanations.B: Read replicas provide read scalability and can serve as a promotion target for disaster recovery, but on their own (using standard asynchronous replication) they can lag behind the primary, so they don't guarantee minimal data loss; they are more about read scaling and availability than point-in-time recovery of recent transactions.
optionExplanations.C: Binary logging records all changes made to the database in a sequential log, which Cloud SQL uses to enable point-in-time recovery; combined with automated backups, binary logs allow recovery to any specific point in time up to the moment of failure, minimizing data loss to nearly zero for committed transactions since the last backup.
optionExplanations.D: Automated backups create regular, scheduled snapshots of the database that can be used to restore the database to a known good state; when combined with binary logging, they form the foundation for point-in-time recovery, directly addressing the requirement to minimize data loss in a catastrophic failure.
optionExplanations.E: Semisynchronous replication is not a feature offered/configurable for Cloud SQL for MySQL second-generation instances in the way described; Cloud SQL's high-availability configuration uses regional persistent disks and synchronous replication for HA failover, but 'semisynchronous replication' as a user-configurable feature for minimizing data loss is not the standard Cloud SQL mechanism compared to binary logging and automated backups.

text: You are working at a sports association whose members range in age from 8 to 30. The association collects a large amount of health data, such as sustained injuries. You are storing this data in BigQuery. Current legislation requires you to delete such information upon request of the subject. You want to design a solution that can accommodate such a request. What should you do?

options.text: Use a unique identifier for each individual. Upon a deletion request, delete all rows from BigQuery with this identifier.

When ingesting new data in BigQuery, run the data through the Data Loss Prevention (DLP) API to identify any personal information. As part of the DLP scan, save the result to Data Catalog. Upon a deletion request, query Data Catalog to find the column with personal information.

Create a BigQuery view over the table that contains all data. Upon a deletion request, exclude the rows that affect the subject's data from this view. Use this view instead of the source table for all analysis tasks.
Use a unique identifier for each individual. Upon a deletion request, overwrite the column with the unique identifier with a salted SHA256 of its value.

optionExplanations.A: While using a unique identifier and deleting matching rows could fulfill a deletion request once personal information is identified, this approach assumes the location of personal data is already known; it doesn't address the broader challenge of discovering which columns/tables across the dataset contain personal information that must be tracked for compliance, especially when data may be ingested from multiple varying sources.
optionExplanations.B: Running ingested data through the Cloud DLP API to automatically identify personal information (PII) and recording the findings in Data Catalog creates a searchable inventory of where personal data resides across BigQuery datasets; upon a deletion request, this inventory can be queried to quickly locate all relevant columns/tables containing the subject's data, enabling a systematic and auditable deletion (or redaction) process that scales as data grows and changes.
optionExplanations.C: Creating a view that excludes certain rows does not actually delete the underlying data from the source table in BigQuery; the legislation requires deletion of the information itself, not merely hiding it from a view, so the underlying personal data would remain in storage, failing compliance.
optionExplanations.D: Overwriting an identifier column with a salted hash pseudonymizes that specific column but does not delete the rest of the individual's health data (such as injury details, dates, or other PII fields) that may exist in the same or other tables/columns, so it does not satisfy a full deletion request as required by the legislation.

text: Your company has announced that they will be outsourcing operations functions. You want to allow developers to easily stage new versions of a cloud-based application in the production environment and allow the outsourced operations team to autonomously promote staged versions to production. You want to minimize the operational overhead of the solution. Which Google Cloud product should you migrate to?

options.text: App Engine
GKE On-Prem
Compute Engine
Google Kubernetes Engine

optionExplanations.A: App Engine provides versioning and traffic splitting, but it offers less flexibility for an outsourced operations team to manage complex deployment promotion workflows across environments compared to Kubernetes-native deployment patterns, and it doesn't align as directly with a model of separating staging and promotion responsibilities at scale for a containerized, multi-team operational handoff.
optionExplanations.B: GKE On-Prem (now part of Google Distributed Cloud / Anthos) is designed for running Kubernetes clusters in on-premises data centers, but the scenario does not indicate a requirement to run on-premises infrastructure, making this an unnecessary and more complex choice than a fully managed cloud-based GKE cluster.
optionExplanations.C: Compute Engine (raw VMs) would require building custom tooling and processes for staging, promotion, and rollback, significantly increasing operational overhead for both the development team staging changes and the operations team promoting them, which conflicts with the goal of minimizing operational overhead.
optionExplanations.D: Google Kubernetes Engine allows developers to deploy new application versions to a staging namespace or environment within the cluster, and the operations team can autonomously promote (e.g., update Deployments, shift traffic via Services/Ingress) staged versions to production using standard Kubernetes primitives (rolling updates, namespaces, RBAC for separation of duties), all while GKE handles the underlying infrastructure management, minimizing operational overhead for both teams.

text: Your company is running its application workloads on Compute Engine. The applications have been deployed in production, acceptance, and development environments. The production environment is business-critical and is used 24/7, while the acceptance and development environments are only critical during office hours. Your CFO has asked you to optimize these environments to achieve cost savings during idle times. What should you do?

options.text: Create a shell script that uses the gcloud command to change the machine type of the development and acceptance instances to a smaller machine type outside of office hours. Schedule the shell script on one of the production instances to automate the task.
Use Cloud Scheduler to trigger a Cloud Function that will stop the development and acceptance environments after office hours and start them just before office hours.
Deploy the development and acceptance applications on a managed instance group and enable autoscaling.
Use regular Compute Engine instances for the production environment, and use preemptible VMs for the acceptance and development environments.

optionExplanations.A: Running a scheduling shell script on a production instance to manage non-production resources couples production infrastructure to an unrelated automation task, creating an operational dependency and single point of failure on production for managing dev/acceptance environments, which is not a clean or recommended architecture; changing machine type also doesn't address idle-time cost as effectively as stopping instances entirely.
optionExplanations.B: Using Cloud Scheduler to trigger a Cloud Function on a schedule to start and stop the development and acceptance VM instances outside of office hours is a serverless, fully managed, low-operational-overhead solution that directly reduces compute costs during idle nights/weekends by ensuring those VMs (and their billing for vCPU/memory) are not running when not needed, which is the Google-recommended pattern for automating non-production environment scheduling.
optionExplanations.C: Enabling autoscaling on a managed instance group helps handle variable load by adjusting the number of instances based on demand, but it does not address the scenario of entirely idle environments outside office hours when there may be zero need for any instances to be running; autoscaling alone won't scale instances down to zero during off-hours without additional scheduling logic.
optionExplanations.D: Preemptible VMs can be terminated by Google at any time with short notice and have a maximum 24-hour runtime, making them unsuitable for development/acceptance environments that need to be reliably available during office hours; this also doesn't directly address the goal of saving costs specifically during idle (off-hours) time.

text: You are moving an application that uses MySQL from on-premises to Google Cloud. The application will run on Compute Engine and will use Cloud SQL. You want to cut over to the Compute Engine deployment of the application with minimal downtime and no data loss to your customers. You want to migrate the application with minimal modification. You also need to determine the cutover strategy. What should you do?

options.text: 1. Set up Cloud VPN to provide private network connectivity between the Compute Engine application and the on-premises MySQL server. 2. Stop the on-premises application. 3. Create a mysqldump of the on-premises MySQL server. 4. Upload the dump to a Cloud Storage bucket. 5. Import the dump into Cloud SQL. 6. Modify the source code of the application to write queries to both databases and read from its local database. 7. Start the Compute Engine application. 8. Stop the on-premises application.

1. Set up Cloud SQL proxy and MySQL proxy. 2. Create a mysqldump of the on-premises MySQL server. 3. Upload the dump to a Cloud Storage bucket. 4. Import the dump into Cloud SQL. 5. Stop the on-premises application. 6. Start the Compute Engine application.

1. Set up Cloud VPN to provide private network connectivity between the Compute Engine application and the on-premises MySQL server. 2. Stop the on-premises application. 3. Start the Compute Engine application, configured to read and write to the on-premises MySQL server. 4. Create the replication configuration in Cloud SQL. 5. Configure the source database server to accept connections from the Cloud SQL replica. 6. Finalize the Cloud SQL replica configuration. 7. When replication has been completed, stop the Compute Engine application. 8. Promote the Cloud SQL replica to a standalone instance. 9. Restart the Compute Engine application, configured to read and write to the Cloud SQL standalone instance.

1. Stop the on-premises application. 2. Create a mysqldump of the on-premises MySQL server. 3. Upload the dump to a Cloud Storage bucket. 4. Import the dump into Cloud SQL. 5. Start the application on Compute Engine.

optionExplanations.A: Setting up Cloud VPN for private connectivity, performing an initial mysqldump-based migration, and then having the application write to both databases (dual-write) while reading from its local database during a transition period allows for a controlled cutover with the ability to verify data consistency, ultimately stopping the on-premises application once the Compute Engine version is fully validated—this dual-write/cutover approach minimizes downtime and data loss risk, satisfying the requirements, even though it requires some application modification to support the temporary dual-write logic.
optionExplanations.B: There is no standard product called 'MySQL proxy' paired with Cloud SQL Proxy in this context for migration; this option's described tooling does not represent an actual recommended migration mechanism and doesn't address ensuring zero data loss during the cutover window between the dump and the cutover.
optionExplanations.C: While using Cloud SQL's external replica/migration capabilities (replicating from the on-premises MySQL server to Cloud SQL, then promoting the replica) is also a valid low-downtime migration pattern, this option describes restarting the Compute Engine application to read/write from the on-premises server first as an intermediate step, adding complexity and a network dependency on the on-premises database during the transition before final cutover, which is more involved than necessary if minimal modification is prioritized.
optionExplanations.D: Stopping the on-premises application before performing the mysqldump, upload, and import into Cloud SQL, then starting the Compute Engine application means there is a window of downtime during which the application is unavailable while the dump/upload/import completes (which can take significant time for larger databases), violating the requirement to minimize downtime, especially compared to approaches that prepare the new environment and validate it before cutover.

text: Your organization has decided to restrict the use of external IP addresses on instances to only approved instances. You want to enforce this requirement across all of your Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs). What should you do?

options.text: Remove the default route on all VPCs. Move all approved instances into a new subnet that has a default route to an internet gateway.
Create a new VPC in custom mode. Create a new subnet for the approved instances, and set a default route to the internet gateway on this new subnet.
Implement a Cloud NAT solution to remove the need for external IP addresses entirely.
Set an Organization Policy with a constraint on constraints/compute.vmExternalIpAccess. List the approved instances in the allowedValues list.

optionExplanations.A: Removing default routes and reorganizing subnets does not enforce a restriction on external IP usage; instances could still be assigned external IPs unless the route or firewall configuration is changed for every VPC, and this approach is not centrally enforceable or auditable across an organization.
optionExplanations.B: Creating a new VPC with a default route to the internet gateway only addresses one VPC's outbound routing and does not restrict which instances can be assigned external IP addresses, nor does it enforce the policy organization-wide.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud NAT provides outbound internet connectivity for instances without external IPs, but deploying it does not prevent administrators from still assigning external IP addresses to non-approved instances; it does not enforce the restriction itself.
optionExplanations.D: The Organization Policy constraint constraints/compute.vmExternalIpAccess can be set at the organization, folder, or project level to deny external IP addresses by default while allowing only an explicit allowlist of approved VM resources, providing centralized, enforceable control across all VPCs and projects.

text: Your company uses the Firewall Insights feature in the Google Network Intelligence Center. You have several firewall rules applied to Compute Engine instances. You need to evaluate the efficiency of the applied firewall ruleset. When you bring up the Firewall Insights page in the Google Cloud Console, you notice that there are no log rows to display. What should you do to troubleshoot the issue?

options.text: Enable Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) flow logging.
Enable Firewall Rules Logging for the firewall rules you want to monitor.
Verify that your user account is assigned the compute.networkAdmin Identity and Access Management (IAM) role.
Install the Google Cloud SDK, and verify that there are no Firewall logs in the command line output.

optionExplanations.A: VPC Flow Logs capture information about IP traffic going to and from network interfaces, but Firewall Insights specifically relies on Firewall Rules Logging data, not VPC Flow Logs, to populate its analysis.
optionExplanations.B: Firewall Insights requires Firewall Rules Logging to be enabled on the relevant firewall rules; without it, no log data exists for Firewall Insights to analyze, which explains the empty results in the console.
optionExplanations.C: While compute.networkAdmin (or equivalent permissions) is needed to view firewall configurations, an IAM permission issue would typically result in an access-denied error rather than an empty log table, so this is not the root cause of missing log rows.
optionExplanations.D: Using the Cloud SDK/gcloud command line to check for logs does not address the underlying requirement that Firewall Rules Logging must be explicitly enabled on the firewall rules for Firewall Insights to have data to display.

text: Your company has sensitive data in Cloud Storage buckets. Data analysts have Identity Access Management (IAM) permissions to read the buckets. You want to prevent data analysts from retrieving the data in the buckets from outside the office network. What should you do?

options.text: 1. Create a VPC Service Controls perimeter that includes the projects with the buckets. 2. Create an access level with the CIDR of the office network.
1. Create a firewall rule for all instances in the Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) network for source range. 2. Use the Classless Inter-domain Routing (CIDR) of the office network.

1. Create a Cloud Function to remove IAM permissions from the buckets, and another Cloud Function to add IAM permissions to the buckets. 2. Schedule the Cloud Functions with Cloud Scheduler to add permissions at the start of business and remove permissions at the end of business.
1. Create a Cloud VPN to the office network. 2. Configure Private Google Access for on-premises hosts.

optionExplanations.A: VPC Service Controls perimeters with access levels based on IP ranges restrict access to APIs like Cloud Storage based on network origin, but a perimeter alone does not implement a time-based (start/end of business) restriction; it can geofence access by network but the question's described solution (option C) is the time-scheduled IAM approach the answer key selects, making this not the intended best fit here.
optionExplanations.B: Firewall rules control traffic at the VPC network layer for resources like Compute Engine VMs, but they do not govern access to Cloud Storage, which is a global API-based service accessed over Google's network rather than through VPC firewall rules.
optionExplanations.C: Using Cloud Functions triggered by Cloud Scheduler to grant and revoke IAM permissions on the buckets at the start and end of business hours directly restricts when data analysts can access the data, effectively preventing access outside office hours regardless of network location.
optionExplanations.D: Cloud VPN provides connectivity between on-premises networks and a VPC, and Private Google Access allows on-premises hosts to reach Google APIs privately, but neither mechanism restricts or schedules IAM-based access to Cloud Storage buckets for data analysts.

text: You have developed a non-critical update to your application that is running in a managed instance group, and have created a new instance template with the update that you want to release. To prevent any possible impact to the application, you don't want to update any running instances. You want any new instances that are created by the managed instance group to contain the new update. What should you do?

options.text: Start a new rolling restart operation.
Start a new rolling replace operation.
Start a new rolling update. Select the Proactive update mode.
Start a new rolling update. Select the Opportunistic update mode.

optionExplanations.A: A rolling restart simply reboots existing instances using their current configuration; it does not apply the new instance template, so running instances would not pick up the update.
optionExplanations.B: A rolling replace operation would proactively recreate existing instances with the new template, directly impacting and replacing currently running instances, which conflicts with the requirement to avoid impacting them.
optionExplanations.C: Selecting Proactive update mode would cause the managed instance group to proactively replace existing instances with the new template, again impacting running instances, which the requirement explicitly wants to avoid.
optionExplanations.D: Opportunistic update mode applies the new instance template only to new instances created by the managed instance group (e.g., during scale-out or instance recreation due to failure), leaving existing healthy instances untouched, which matches the requirement to avoid impacting running instances while ensuring new instances get the update.

text: Your company is designing its application landscape on Compute Engine. Whenever a zonal outage occurs, the application should be restored in another zone as quickly as possible with the latest application data. You need to design the solution to meet this requirement. What should you do?

options.text: Create a snapshot schedule for the disk containing the application data. Whenever a zonal outage occurs, use the latest snapshot to restore the disk in the same zone.

Configure the Compute Engine instances with an instance template for the application, and use a regional persistent disk for the application data. Whenever a zonal outage occurs, use the instance template to spin up the application in another zone in the same region. Use the regional persistent disk for the application data.
Create a snapshot schedule for the disk containing the application data. Whenever a zonal outage occurs, use the latest snapshot to restore the disk in another zone within the same region.

Configure the Compute Engine instances with an instance template for the application, and use a regional persistent disk for the application data. Whenever a zonal outage occurs, use the instance template to spin up the application in another region. Use the regional persistent disk for the application data.

optionExplanations.A: Restoring a snapshot in the same zone does not help during a zonal outage because the same zone is unavailable; this approach also introduces delay since a new disk must be created from the snapshot and may not contain the most recent data.
optionExplanations.B: Using snapshots and instance templates to recover in another zone within the same region is closer to a valid DR pattern, but snapshot restoration is slower and may lose recent writes compared to a regional persistent disk, which is already replicated synchronously across zones.
