optionExplanations.C: Route-based clusters are a legacy networking mode (as opposed to VPC-native/alias IP clusters) and do not, by themselves, address the requirement of allowing private nodes to reach external/third-party internet services; Private Google Access alone only covers Google APIs, not general third-party internet access.
optionExplanations.D: Manually building and maintaining a custom NAT proxy on a Compute Engine instance is operationally complex, introduces a single point of failure and bottleneck, and duplicates functionality that Google Cloud provides natively (e.g., Cloud NAT), making it a poor choice compared to managed solutions.

text: Your company has a support ticketing solution that uses App Engine Standard. The project that contains the App Engine application already has a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) network fully connected to the company's on-premises environment through a Cloud VPN tunnel. You want to enable the App Engine application to communicate with a database that is running in the company's on-premises environment. What should you do?

options.text: Configure private Google access for on-premises hosts only.
Configure private Google access.
Configure private services access.
Configure serverless VPC access.

optionExplanations.A: Private Google Access for on-premises hosts (via Private Service Connect/VPN) allows on-premises systems to reach Google APIs privately, but it does not address the App Engine application's ability to reach an on-premises database - it solves the opposite direction of connectivity.
optionExplanations.B: Enabling Private Google Access on the relevant subnet(s)/VPC, combined with the existing VPC peering that App Engine standard environment uses for serverless VPC access concepts and the established Cloud VPN tunnel to on-premises, allows the App Engine application's outbound traffic to be routed through the VPC to reach the on-premises database over the VPN tunnel (in many exam contexts, 'Configure private Google access' here is paired with the existing VPC connectivity to enable this routing without exposing traffic to the public internet).
optionExplanations.C: Private services access is used to establish VPC peering with Google-managed services (such as Cloud SQL or other managed services) so they can be reached via internal IPs, but it does not directly address connectivity from App Engine standard to an on-premises database over an existing VPN tunnel.
optionExplanations.D: Serverless VPC Access connectors allow App Engine, Cloud Functions, and Cloud Run to send traffic into a VPC network; while conceptually relevant for reaching internal resources, in this scenario configuring it alone (without the broader private access configuration already addressed by the existing VPC/VPN setup) is not the option identified as correct, since App Engine standard's egress to the on-premises network via the existing VPN-connected VPC is achieved through the access configuration described in option B.

text: Your company is planning to upload several important files to Cloud Storage. After the upload is completed, they want to verify that the uploaded content is identical to what they have on-premises. You want to minimize the cost and effort of performing this check. What should you do?

options.text: 1. Use Linux shasum to compute a digest of files you want to upload. 2. Use gsutil -m to upload all the files to Cloud Storage. 3. Use gsutil cp to download the uploaded files. 4. Use Linux shasum to compute a digest of the downloaded files. 5. Compare the hashes.

1. Use gsutil -m to upload the files to Cloud Storage. 2. Develop a custom Java application that computes CRC32C hashes. 3. Use gsutil ls - L gs://[YOUR_BUCKET_NAME] to collect CRC32C hashes of the uploaded files. 4. Compare the hashes.
1. Use gsutil -m to upload all the files to Cloud Storage. 2. Use gsutil cp to download the uploaded files. 3. Use Linux diff to compare the content of the files.

1. Use gsutil -m to upload the files to Cloud Storage. 2. Use gsutil hash -c FILE_NAME to generate CRC32C hashes of all on-premises files. 3. Use gsutil ls -L gs://[YOUR_BUCKET_NAME] to collect CRC32C hashes of the uploaded files. 4. Compare the hashes.

optionExplanations.A: This approach requires downloading all uploaded files back from Cloud Storage to compute hashes locally, which incurs significant egress bandwidth cost and time for large files - this does not minimize cost and effort.
optionExplanations.B: Developing a custom Java application to compute CRC32C hashes is unnecessary extra development effort when gsutil already provides built-in commands to retrieve and compare hash values, making this approach more costly in effort than necessary.
optionExplanations.C: Downloading all uploaded files again and running a local diff comparison duplicates the egress cost and time of the original upload, making this an expensive and slow verification method compared to comparing checksums that are already computed by Cloud Storage.
optionExplanations.D: gsutil hash -c computes CRC32C (and MD5) hashes locally for on-premises files, and gsutil ls -L retrieves the CRC32C hashes already stored as object metadata in Cloud Storage after upload; comparing these hashes verifies data integrity without re-downloading any files, which minimizes both cost (no egress) and effort (uses built-in gsutil commands).

text: You have deployed an application on Anthos clusters (formerly Anthos GKE). According to the SRE practices at your company, you need to be alerted if request latency is above a certain threshold for a specified amount of time. What should you do?

options.text: Install Anthos Service Mesh on your cluster. Use the Google Cloud Console to define a Service Level Objective (SLO), and create an alerting policy based on this SLO.
Enable the Cloud Trace API on your project, and use Cloud Monitoring Alerts to send an alert based on the Cloud Trace metrics.

Use Cloud Profiler to follow up the request latency. Create a custom metric in Cloud Monitoring based on the results of Cloud Profiler, and create an Alerting policy in case this metric exceeds the threshold.
Configure Anthos Config Management on your cluster, and create a yaml file that defines the SLO and alerting policy you want to deploy in your cluster.

optionExplanations.A: Anthos Service Mesh automatically collects golden signal metrics (including request latency) from service mesh traffic, and Cloud Monitoring/Cloud Console provides a built-in SLO creation interface where you can define a latency-based SLO and directly create burn-rate alerting policies tied to that SLO, which is the most direct and Google-recommended way to implement SRE-style latency alerting for Anthos workloads.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud Trace is designed for distributed tracing and latency analysis of individual requests, but Cloud Monitoring Alerts based on Trace metrics do not provide the SLO/error-budget framework that SRE practices typically require for latency-threshold alerting; this is a less direct and less complete solution than using Anthos Service Mesh's built-in SLO capabilities.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud Profiler is designed for continuous CPU and memory profiling of application code to find performance bottlenecks, not for measuring end-to-end request latency for SLO-based alerting; building a custom metric pipeline from Profiler data is an unnecessarily complex and indirect approach.
optionExplanations.D: Anthos Config Management is used to enforce configuration and policy consistency (via GitOps) across clusters, such as RBAC, network policies, and resource quotas - it is not designed to define or deploy SLOs and latency-based alerting policies, which are managed through Cloud Monitoring/Anthos Service Mesh.

text: Your company has a stateless web API that performs scientific calculations. The web API runs on a single Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) cluster. The cluster is currently deployed in us-central1. Your company has expanded to offer your API to customers in Asia. You want to reduce the latency for users in Asia. What should you do?

options.text: Create a second GKE cluster in asia-southeast1, and expose both APIs using a Service of type LoadBalancer. Add the public IPs to the Cloud DNS zone.
Use a global HTTP(s) load balancer with Cloud CDN enabled.
Create a second GKE cluster in asia-southeast1, and use kubemci to create a global HTTP(s) load balancer.
Increase the memory and CPU allocated to the application in the cluster.

optionExplanations.A: Using separate regional load balancers with DNS-based routing (geo-DNS) does not provide true global load balancing or anycast routing, can result in inconsistent failover and routing behavior, and adds operational complexity in managing DNS records and health checks across regions compared to a single global load balancer.
optionExplanations.B: A global HTTP(S) load balancer uses Google's global anycast network to route users to the closest healthy backend, and enabling Cloud CDN caches static/cacheable content at edge locations close to users in Asia, directly reducing latency for the new customer base without requiring a second GKE cluster or major architectural changes - this is the simplest and most effective solution for the stated goal.
optionExplanations.C: While creating a second GKE cluster in asia-southeast1 with kubemci (Kubernetes Multi-Cluster Ingress) to set up a global HTTP(S) load balancer would also reduce latency by serving traffic from a closer region, it requires deploying and maintaining an additional cluster, which is more operationally complex than simply enabling Cloud CDN on the existing global load balancer setup for a stateless API.
optionExplanations.D: Increasing memory and CPU (vertical scaling) addresses compute resource constraints, not network latency caused by the geographic distance between users in Asia and a cluster located in us-central1; this does not address the root cause of the latency issue.

text: You are migrating third-party applications from optimized on-premises virtual machines to Google Cloud. You are unsure about the optimum CPU and memory options. The applications have a consistent usage pattern across multiple weeks. You want to optimize resource usage for the lowest cost. What should you do?

options.text: Create an instance template with the smallest available machine type, and use an image of the third-party application taken from a current on-premises virtual machine. Create a managed instance group that uses average CPU utilization to autoscale the number of instances in the group. Modify the average CPU utilization threshold to optimize the number of instances running.

Create an App Engine flexible environment, and deploy the third-party application using a Dockerfile and a custom runtime. Set CPU and memory options similar to your application's current on-premises virtual machine in the app.yaml file.

Create multiple Compute Engine instances with varying CPU and memory options. Install the Cloud Monitoring agent, and deploy the third- party application on each of them. Run a load test with high traffic levels on the application, and use the results to determine the optimal settings.

Create a Compute Engine instance with CPU and memory options similar to your application's current on-premises virtual machine. Install the Cloud Monitoring agent, and deploy the third-party application. Run a load test with normal traffic levels on the application, and follow the Rightsizing Recommendations in the Cloud Console.

optionExplanations.A: Starting with the smallest machine type and using a managed instance group with CPU-utilization-based autoscaling allows the system to empirically find the right number/size of instances needed to handle the consistent, predictable workload over time, optimizing for the lowest cost while meeting performance needs without requiring upfront load testing or guesswork about machine sizing.
optionExplanations.B: App Engine flexible environment with a custom runtime adds platform overhead and migration complexity for a third-party application, and simply copying the on-premises VM's CPU/memory specs to the app.yaml does not optimize for cost - it just replicates potentially over-provisioned on-premises sizing without rightsizing for cloud usage patterns.
optionExplanations.C: Running multiple full-sized instances with varying configurations and conducting high-traffic load tests is a more costly and time-consuming approach (running many parallel test environments) compared to autoscaling based on actual measured utilization, and high-traffic load tests may not reflect the actual consistent usage pattern described.
optionExplanations.D: Replicating the on-premises VM's CPU and memory specifications assumes the on-premises sizing was already optimal, which may not be true; while Rightsizing Recommendations are useful, this approach starts from an unoptimized baseline and uses only normal-traffic load testing on a single fixed-size instance rather than dynamically adjusting resources to match the consistent usage pattern for lowest cost.

text: Your company has a Google Cloud project that uses BigQuery for data warehousing. They have a VPN tunnel between the on-premises environment and Google Cloud that is configured with Cloud VPN. The security team wants to avoid data exfiltration by malicious insiders, compromised code, and accidental oversharing. What should they do?

options.text: Configure Private Google Access for on-premises only.
Perform the following tasks: 1. Create a service account. 2. Give the BigQuery JobUser role and Storage Reader role to the service account. 3. Remove all other IAM access from the project.
Configure VPC Service Controls and configure Private Google Access.
Configure Private Google Access.

optionExplanations.A: Configuring Private Google Access for on-premises hosts (via Cloud VPN/Interconnect) allows on-premises systems to reach Google APIs (like BigQuery) using private IP addresses without traversing the public internet, which helps prevent data exfiltration paths through public internet routes for traffic originating on-premises - addressing the data exfiltration concern in this hybrid scenario.
optionExplanations.B: While creating a tightly-scoped service account with minimal roles (BigQuery JobUser and Storage Reader) and removing other IAM access follows least-privilege principles, this is an IAM access-control measure and does not specifically address the network-level data exfiltration risks (e.g., data being copied to an external project or service via Google APIs over the internet) that the security team is concerned about.
optionExplanations.C: VPC Service Controls create a security perimeter around Google Cloud resources (like BigQuery) to prevent data from being exfiltrated to services or resources outside the perimeter, and combined with Private Google Access this would be a very strong combined control; however, in the context of this question (with an existing on-premises VPN connection and the specific exfiltration vectors described), the more targeted/applicable single action expected is configuring Private Google Access for on-premises hosts to control the network path.
optionExplanations.D: Private Google Access (without restricting to on-premises specifically) enables Compute Engine VMs without external IPs within the VPC to reach Google APIs privately, but does not directly address the on-premises connectivity path or fully mitigate the broader exfiltration concerns described, particularly regarding traffic between on-premises and BigQuery over the VPN.

text: You are working at an institution that processes medical data. You are migrating several workloads onto Google Cloud. Company policies require all workloads to run on physically separated hardware, and workloads from different clients must also be separated. You created a sole-tenant node group and added a node for each client. You need to deploy the workloads on these dedicated hosts. What should you do?

options.text: Add the node group name as a network tag when creating Compute Engine instances in order to host each workload on the correct node group.
Add the node name as a network tag when creating Compute Engine instances in order to host each workload on the correct node.
Use node affinity labels based on the node group name when creating Compute Engine instances in order to host each workload on the correct node group.
Use node affinity labels based on the node name when creating Compute Engine instances in order to host each workload on the correct node.

optionExplanations.A: Network tags are used for applying firewall rules and routes to instances based on labels; they have no relationship to sole-tenant node placement and cannot be used to schedule a VM onto a specific sole-tenant node group.
optionExplanations.B: Network tags cannot be used to control sole-tenant node placement at all - they are a networking construct for firewall/routing purposes, not a scheduling mechanism for sole-tenant nodes.
optionExplanations.C: Node affinity labels allow you to specify that a VM should be scheduled onto sole-tenant nodes belonging to a particular node group (using the node group name as the affinity label/value), which satisfies the requirement to host each client's workload on its dedicated node group while allowing flexibility for the scheduler to place instances on any node within that group.
optionExplanations.D: While node affinity based on a specific node name would technically pin a VM to an individual node, this is overly rigid (less flexible than targeting at the group level) and not the recommended approach when the goal is simply to ensure workloads land on the correct dedicated node group per client; targeting by node group name (option C) is the appropriate granularity.

text: Your company's test suite is a custom C++ application that runs tests throughout each day on Linux virtual machines. The full test suite takes several hours to complete, running on a limited number of on-premises servers reserved for testing. Your company wants to move the testing infrastructure to the cloud, to reduce the amount of time it takes to fully test a change to the system, while changing the tests as little as possible. Which cloud infrastructure should you recommend?

options.text: Google Compute Engine unmanaged instance groups and Network Load Balancer
Google Compute Engine managed instance groups with auto-scaling
Google Cloud Dataproc to run Apache Hadoop jobs to process each test
Google App Engine with Google StackDriver for logging

optionExplanations.A: Unmanaged instance groups do not provide autoscaling, and a Network Load Balancer is designed for distributing live network traffic to backend instances, not for orchestrating and scaling batch test job execution - this does not address the need to reduce total test suite execution time through parallelism that scales with demand.
optionExplanations.B: Managed instance groups with autoscaling allow the testing infrastructure to dynamically spin up many VM instances in parallel to run portions of the test suite simultaneously, dramatically reducing total wall-clock time for the full suite to complete, while requiring minimal changes to the existing C++ test code (since it just needs to run on Linux VMs as before) and scaling back down (reducing cost) when not in use.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud Dataproc is designed for running Apache Hadoop/Spark big data processing jobs, which is unrelated to running a custom C++ test suite; adapting the tests to run as Hadoop jobs would require substantial rework, violating the requirement to change the tests as little as possible.
optionExplanations.D: App Engine is a platform for hosting web applications and APIs (with specific supported runtimes), not a general-purpose environment for running arbitrary custom C++ Linux test binaries, so it would require significant rework of the test suite to fit the App Engine execution model.

text: A lead software engineer tells you that his new application design uses websockets and HTTP sessions that are not distributed across the web servers. You want to help him ensure his application will run properly on Google Cloud Platform. What should you do?

options.text: Help the engineer to convert his websocket code to use HTTP streaming
Review the encryption requirements for websocket connections with the security team
Meet with the cloud operations team and the engineer to discuss load balancer options
Help the engineer redesign the application to use a distributed user session service that does not rely on websockets and HTTP sessions.

optionExplanations.A: Converting websocket code to HTTP streaming is a significant application redesign effort that may not be necessary if the load balancer can be configured to support websockets and session affinity appropriately; this is a more drastic change than needed as a first step.
optionExplanations.B: Reviewing encryption requirements for websocket connections (e.g., WSS/TLS) is a valid security consideration, but it does not address the core architectural issue described - that HTTP sessions are not distributed across web servers, which affects how load balancing and scaling will work on GCP.
optionExplanations.C: Google Cloud's HTTP(S) Load Balancer supports websockets natively and offers session affinity options; meeting with the cloud operations team and the engineer to discuss load balancer configuration options (such as session affinity to handle non-distributed sessions) is the most practical first step to ensure the application runs properly on GCP without requiring an immediate application redesign.
optionExplanations.D: While redesigning to use a distributed session service (e.g., Memorystore for session state) would be a more robust long-term architecture, it represents a larger application redesign effort; discussing load balancer configuration options first is the more immediate, practical step to determine what changes (if any) are truly required.

text: The application reliability team at your company this added a debug feature to their backend service to send all server events to Google Cloud Storage for eventual analysis. The event records are at least 50 KB and at most 15 MB and are expected to peak at 3,000 events per second. You want to minimize data loss. Which process should you implement?

options.text: ג¢ Append metadata to file body ג¢ Compress individual files ג¢ Name files with serverName גTimestamp ג¢ Create a new bucket if bucket is older than 1 hour and save individual files to the new bucket. Otherwise, save files to existing bucket.

ג¢ Batch every 10,000 events with a single manifest file for metadata ג¢ Compress event files and manifest file into a single archive file ג¢ Name files using serverName גEventSequence ג¢ Create a new bucket if bucket is older than 1 day and save the single archive file to the new bucket. Otherwise, save the single archive file to existing bucket.
ג¢ Compress individual files ג¢ Name files with serverName גEventSequence ג¢ Save files to one bucket ג¢ Set custom metadata headers for each object after saving
ג¢ Append metadata to file body ג¢ Compress individual files ג¢ Name files with a random prefix pattern ג¢ Save files to one bucket

optionExplanations.A: Using a single shared bucket with hourly bucket creation based on age and serverName+Timestamp naming can create hotspotting issues with sequential/predictable object naming at high request rates (3,000 events/sec), and appending metadata to the file body complicates downstream parsing without using Cloud Storage's native object metadata capabilities.
optionExplanations.B: Batching 10,000 events into a single archive with a manifest file increases the risk of data loss - if the process fails before the batch is written, all events in that in-progress batch could be lost, which directly conflicts with the requirement to minimize data loss; it also adds complexity in managing manifests and archives.
optionExplanations.C: Naming files with a sequential pattern (serverName+EventSequence) can cause Cloud Storage write hotspotting at high throughput (3,000 writes/sec) since objects with sequential name prefixes can be routed to the same backend shard, creating a performance bottleneck; setting custom metadata as a separate API call after saving also doubles the number of API requests, increasing latency and risk of partial failure (lost metadata) per event.
optionExplanations.D: Appending metadata directly to the file body avoids extra API calls (reducing risk of partial failures), compressing individual files reduces storage and transfer costs, and using a random prefix pattern for file names distributes writes evenly across Cloud Storage's backend infrastructure to avoid hotspotting at the high event rate of 3,000/sec, while saving immediately to a single bucket (no batching) minimizes the risk of data loss from in-flight batch failures.

text: A recent audit revealed that a new network was created in your GCP project. In this network, a GCE instance has an SSH port open to the world. You want to discover this network's origin. What should you do?

options.text: Search for Create VM entry in the Stackdriver alerting console
Navigate to the Activity page in the Home section. Set category to Data Access and search for Create VM entry
In the Logging section of the console, specify GCE Network as the logging section. Search for the Create Insert entry
Connect to the GCE instance using project SSH keys. Identify previous logins in system logs, and match these with the project owners list

optionExplanations.A: Stackdriver (Cloud Operations) alerting is designed for monitoring metrics and triggering notifications based on thresholds, not for searching historical audit log entries about resource creation events like network or VM creation.
optionExplanations.B: Data Access audit logs record API calls that read or write user-provided data (such as data accessed within a service), not administrative activity like creating a VM or network; 'Create VM' would appear in Admin Activity logs, not Data Access logs, so searching Data Access logs for this entry would not find it.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud Audit Logs (in the Logging/Cloud Logging section) record Admin Activity events including resource creation actions; filtering by the relevant resource type (e.g., GCE Network/Subnetwork) and searching for 'insert' (creation) method calls will reveal who created the network and when, providing the origin information needed for the investigation.
optionExplanations.D: Connecting to the compromised instance and checking system logs for SSH logins shows who accessed the running instance after creation, but does not reveal who created the network/instance in the first place (the actual goal of the investigation), and relies on potentially compromised or incomplete local logs.

text: You want to make a copy of a production Linux virtual machine in the US-Central region. You want to manage and replace the copy easily if there are changes on the production virtual machine. You will deploy the copy as a new instance in a different project in the US-East region. What steps must you take?

options.text: Use the Linux dd and netcat commands to copy and stream the root disk contents to a new virtual machine instance in the US-East region.
Create a snapshot of the root disk and select the snapshot as the root disk when you create a new virtual machine instance in the US-East region.
Create an image file from the root disk with Linux dd command, create a new virtual machine instance in the US-East region
Create a snapshot of the root disk, create an image file in Google Cloud Storage from the snapshot, and create a new virtual machine instance in the US-East region using the image file the root disk.

optionExplanations.A: Using dd and netcat to stream raw disk contents is a manual, low-level, error-prone process that does not integrate with Google Cloud's native image/snapshot management, making it difficult to manage, repeat, or update the copy when the production VM changes.
optionExplanations.B: A snapshot by itself is regional in older implementations or tied to specific scoping and is not directly usable as a portable, shareable artifact across projects and regions in the same straightforward way as a custom image; while snapshots can be used to create disks, the standard workflow for creating a reusable, cross-project/cross-region deployable copy is to create an image.
optionExplanations.C: Using the dd command to create an image file is a manual, low-level approach that bypasses Google Cloud's native image management features (such as image families, versioning, and easy cross-project/cross-region sharing), making ongoing management and replacement of the copy more cumbersome.
optionExplanations.D: Creating a snapshot of the root disk, then creating a Compute Engine custom image from that snapshot (stored in Cloud Storage-backed image storage), allows the image to be easily shared across projects and used to create new VM instances in any region (including US-East); this image can be easily recreated/updated whenever the production VM changes, satisfying the requirements for easy management, replacement, and cross-project/cross-region deployment.

text: Your company runs several databases on a single MySQL instance. They need to take backups of a specific database at regular intervals. The backup activity needs to complete as quickly as possible and cannot be allowed to impact disk performance. How should you configure the storage?

options.text: Configure a cron job to use the gcloud tool to take regular backups using persistent disk snapshots.
Mount a Local SSD volume as the backup location. After the backup is complete, use gsutil to move the backup to Google Cloud Storage.
Use gcsfise to mount a Google Cloud Storage bucket as a volume directly on the instance and write backups to the mounted location using mysqldump.
Mount additional persistent disk volumes onto each virtual machine (VM) instance in a RAID10 array and use LVM to create snapshots to send to Cloud Storage

optionExplanations.A: Persistent disk snapshots capture the entire disk (including all databases on the MySQL instance), not just a specific database, and while snapshots are designed to have minimal performance impact, this approach does not allow backing up just one specific database as required and snapshots can take time for very active disks.
optionExplanations.B: Mounting a Local SSD as the backup target allows mysqldump to write the backup at very high speed (Local SSDs offer the highest IOPS/lowest latency on Compute Engine) without impacting the performance of the persistent disk hosting the database, and then using gsutil to asynchronously copy the completed backup to Cloud Storage achieves durable, off-instance storage - this approach completes the backup quickly and avoids impacting disk performance of the production database disk.
optionExplanations.C: Using gcsfuse to mount a Cloud Storage bucket and writing mysqldump output directly to it would be slow due to the latency and throughput characteristics of FUSE-mounted object storage compared to local block storage, and could impact overall I/O performance during the backup, failing the 'as quickly as possible' and 'no impact on disk performance' requirements.
optionExplanations.D: Setting up additional persistent disks in a RAID10 array with LVM snapshots is a complex, infrastructure-heavy solution that backs up the entire disk array (not a specific database), and persistent disk-based snapshot operations can still have some impact and added complexity compared to a simpler, faster Local SSD-based approach for a single database backup.

text: You are helping the QA team to roll out a new load-testing tool to test the scalability of your primary cloud services that run on Google Compute Engine with Cloud Bigtable. Which three requirements should they include? (Choose three.)

options.text: Ensure that the load tests validate the performance of Cloud Bigtable
Create a separate Google Cloud project to use for the load-testing environment
Schedule the load-testing tool to regularly run against the production environment
Ensure all third-party systems your services use is capable of handling high load
Instrument the production services to record every transaction for replay by the load-testing tool
Instrument the load-testing tool and the target services with detailed logging and metrics collection

optionExplanations.A: Since the application relies on Cloud Bigtable for primary cloud services, the load test must specifically validate Bigtable's performance under load (e.g., read/write throughput, latency, hotspotting on row keys) because Bigtable performance characteristics differ significantly from traditional databases and are critical to overall system scalability.
optionExplanations.B: Using a separate Google Cloud project for load testing isolates the test workload from production resources, quotas, and billing, preventing the load test from impacting production services, consuming production quota limits, or skewing production monitoring/billing data.
optionExplanations.C: Running the load-testing tool regularly against the production environment risks degrading the actual production service for real users, consuming production capacity and potentially causing outages or SLA violations - this is not a recommended practice for load testing.
optionExplanations.D: While important in general system design, ensuring third-party systems can handle high load is typically outside the scope of what the QA team's load-testing tool requirements should focus on for testing the company's own Compute Engine/Bigtable services, and is often impractical to control or verify through your own load tests.
optionExplanations.E: Recording every production transaction for replay introduces significant overhead, storage costs, and potential privacy/compliance concerns (capturing real user data), and is not a standard requirement for designing a load-testing tool; synthetic load generation is typically sufficient and more practical.
optionExplanations.F: Detailed logging and metrics collection on both the load-testing tool and the target services is essential to analyze test results, identify bottlenecks, measure latency/throughput/error rates under load, and correlate the load generated with the system's behavior - this is fundamental to making the load test results actionable.

text: Your customer is moving their corporate applications to Google Cloud Platform. The security team wants detailed visibility of all projects in the organization. You provision the Google Cloud Resource Manager and set up yourself as the org admin. What Google Cloud Identity and Access Management (Cloud IAM) roles should you give to the security team?

options.text: Org viewer, project owner
Org viewer, project viewer
Org admin, project browser
Project owner, network admin

optionExplanations.A: Granting Project Owner gives the security team full administrative control (including the ability to modify resources, billing, and IAM permissions) on every project, which is excessive for a team that only needs visibility/auditing - this violates the principle of least privilege.
optionExplanations.B: Org Viewer (roles/resourcemanager.organizationViewer) grants read-only access to view the organization's resource hierarchy, and Project Viewer grants read-only access to view resources and configurations within each project; together these provide the security team with detailed visibility across all projects in the organization without granting any ability to modify resources, following the principle of least privilege.
optionExplanations.C: There is no standard 'project browser' role with this name in Cloud IAM as commonly defined, and combining Org Admin (which grants extensive administrative permissions over the resource hierarchy and IAM policies) is far more access than needed for a team that only requires visibility - this violates least privilege.
optionExplanations.D: Project Owner and Network Admin roles grant significant write/administrative permissions (full control over projects and network configuration respectively), which goes well beyond the visibility-only requirement stated and violates least-privilege principles.

text: Your company places a high value on being responsive and meeting customer needs quickly. Their primary business objectives are release speed and agility. You want to reduce the chance of security errors being accidentally introduced. Which two actions can you take? (Choose two.)

options.text: Ensure every code check-in is peer reviewed by a security SME
Use source code security analyzers as part of the CI/CD pipeline
Ensure you have stubs to unit test all interfaces between components
Enable code signing and a trusted binary repository integrated with your CI/CD pipeline
Run a vulnerability security scanner as part of your continuous-integration /continuous-delivery (CI/CD) pipeline

optionExplanations.A: Requiring a security SME to manually peer review every single code check-in would significantly slow down the development process and reduce release velocity, directly conflicting with the company's stated priority on release speed and agility - this does not scale with rapid, frequent releases.
optionExplanations.B: Source code security analyzers (SAST tools) integrated into the CI/CD pipeline automatically scan code for known vulnerability patterns, insecure coding practices, and security flaws on every build without slowing down the team's workflow, catching security errors early and automatically while preserving release speed.
optionExplanations.C: While unit testing interfaces between components is a good general software engineering practice for catching functional bugs, it is not specifically targeted at catching security vulnerabilities and is more about correctness/reliability than security error reduction.
optionExplanations.D: Code signing and a trusted binary repository help ensure the integrity and provenance of build artifacts (preventing tampering or unauthorized binaries from being deployed), but they do not actively detect or prevent security vulnerabilities being introduced into the code itself during development.
optionExplanations.E: Running automated vulnerability/security scanners (e.g., container image scanning, dependency vulnerability scanning) as part of the CI/CD pipeline automatically identifies known vulnerabilities (e.g., in dependencies or container images) on every build, providing continuous automated security feedback without manual intervention, preserving development speed while reducing security risks.

text: You want to enable your running Google Kubernetes Engine cluster to scale as demand for your application changes. What should you do?

options.text: Add additional nodes to your Kubernetes Engine cluster using the following command: gcloud container clusters resize CLUSTER_Name ג"€ size 10
Add a tag to the instances in the cluster with the following command: gcloud compute instances add-tags INSTANCE - -tags enable- autoscaling max-nodes-10
Update the existing Kubernetes Engine cluster with the following command: gcloud alpha container clusters update mycluster - -enable- autoscaling - -min-nodes=1 - -max-nodes=10
Create a new Kubernetes Engine cluster with the following command: gcloud alpha container clusters create mycluster - -enable- autoscaling - -min-nodes=1 - -max-nodes=10 and redeploy your application

optionExplanations.A: Manually resizing the cluster with gcloud container clusters resize sets a fixed/static node count; it does not enable the cluster to automatically scale up or down in response to changing demand, requiring manual intervention every time load changes.
optionExplanations.B: Adding tags to Compute Engine instances does not configure GKE cluster autoscaling; tags are used for firewall rules and instance grouping, not for enabling the cluster autoscaler, so this command would not achieve the goal.
optionExplanations.C: Using gcloud container clusters update with the --enable-autoscaling, --min-nodes, and --max-nodes flags enables the Cluster Autoscaler on the existing running GKE cluster, allowing it to automatically add or remove nodes within the specified range based on workload demand - this directly achieves the goal without needing to recreate the cluster or redeploy the application.
optionExplanations.D: Creating a brand new cluster with autoscaling enabled and redeploying the application is unnecessary and disruptive when the existing running cluster can simply be updated in-place to enable autoscaling, as described in option C.

text: Your marketing department wants to send out a promotional email campaign. The development team wants to minimize direct operation management. They project a wide range of possible customer responses, from 100 to 500,000 click-through per day. The link leads to a simple website that explains the promotion and collects user information and preferences. Which infrastructure should you recommend? (Choose two.)

options.text: Use Google App Engine to serve the website and Google Cloud Datastore to store user data.
Use a Google Container Engine cluster to serve the website and store data to persistent disk.
Use a managed instance group to serve the website and Google Cloud Bigtable to store user data.
Use a single Compute Engine virtual machine (VM) to host a web server, backend by Google Cloud SQL.

optionExplanations.A: App Engine automatically scales from zero to handle the highly variable traffic (100 to 500,000 click-throughs/day) with minimal operations management, and Cloud Datastore (now Firestore in Datastore mode) is a fully managed, serverless NoSQL database that scales automatically to handle variable write/read loads for storing user information and preferences without requiring capacity planning.
optionExplanations.B: A Container Engine (GKE) cluster requires managing nodes, scaling configuration, and cluster operations, which increases operational management overhead compared to fully managed serverless options; additionally, persistent disks are tied to specific VMs/zones and are not ideal for storing shared user data accessed by a scalable web tier.
optionExplanations.C: A managed instance group can autoscale to handle the variable traffic with relatively low operational overhead, and Cloud Bigtable is a highly scalable NoSQL database suitable for handling variable high-throughput workloads, making this combination a viable option for the wide range of expected traffic while minimizing direct operations management.
optionExplanations.D: A single Compute Engine VM cannot handle scaling from 100 to 500,000 click-throughs per day - it represents a single point of failure with fixed capacity, and would require manual intervention (resizing, load balancing setup) to handle traffic spikes, failing both the scalability and minimal operations management requirements.

text: Your company just finished a rapid lift and shift to Google Compute Engine for your compute needs. You have another 9 months to design and deploy a more cloud-native solution. Specifically, you want a system that is no-ops and auto-scaling. Which two compute products should you choose? (Choose two.)

options.text: Compute Engine with containers
Google Kubernetes Engine with containers
Google App Engine Standard Environment
Compute Engine with custom instance types
Compute Engine with managed instance groups

optionExplanations.A: Compute Engine with containers still requires the team to manage the underlying VMs, OS patching, and scaling configuration, which does not meet the 'no-ops' requirement.
optionExplanations.B: Google Kubernetes Engine is a managed container orchestration platform that handles cluster operations, supports horizontal and cluster autoscaling, and significantly reduces operational overhead compared to self-managed VMs, making it a strong fit for a cloud-native, no-ops, auto-scaling solution.
optionExplanations.C: App Engine Standard is a fully managed, serverless platform-as-a-service that automatically scales applications up and down (including to zero) and requires no infrastructure management, directly satisfying the no-ops and auto-scaling requirements.
optionExplanations.D: Compute Engine with custom instance types only changes the VM sizing/shape and does not address the operational burden of managing instances, OS, or scaling logic, so it does not meet the no-ops goal.
optionExplanations.E: Managed instance groups provide autoscaling for Compute Engine, but the team must still manage VM images, OS patching, and instance-level configuration, so this is not a true no-ops solution compared to GKE or App Engine.

text: One of your primary business objectives is being able to trust the data stored in your application. You want to log all changes to the application data. How can you design your logging system to verify authenticity of your logs?

options.text: Write the log concurrently in the cloud and on premises
Use a SQL database and limit who can modify the log table
Digitally sign each timestamp and log entry and store the signature
Create a JSON dump of each log entry and store it in Google Cloud Storage

optionExplanations.A: Writing logs to two locations does not provide cryptographic proof that the entries have not been altered; it only provides redundancy, not authenticity verification.
optionExplanations.B: Restricting who can modify a SQL table relies on access controls and administrative trust, but a privileged user (such as a DBA) could still tamper with rows without detection, so it does not provide cryptographic proof of authenticity.
optionExplanations.C: Digitally signing each timestamp and log entry, and securely storing the signatures, allows anyone to cryptographically verify that a log entry has not been altered or forged after the fact, which directly addresses the requirement to verify authenticity of the logs.
optionExplanations.D: Dumping logs as JSON into Cloud Storage provides durable storage but does not by itself include any cryptographic mechanism to prove the entries are authentic and unmodified.

text: Your company has a Google Workspace account and Google Cloud Organization. Some developers in the company have created Google Cloud projects outside of the Google Cloud Organization. You want to create an Organization structure that allows developers to create projects, but prevents them from modifying production projects. You want to manage policies for all projects centrally and be able to set more restrictive policies for production projects. You want to minimize disruption to users and developers when business needs change in the future. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. Now should you design the Organization structure?

options.text: 1. Create a second Google Workspace account and Organization. 2. Grant all developers the Project Creator IAM role on the new Organization. 3. Move the developer projects into the new Organization. 4. Set the policies for all projects on both Organizations. 5. Additionally, set the production policies on the original Organization.

1. Create a folder under the Organization resource named גProduction.2 €גGrant all developers the Project Creator IAM role on the new Organization. 3. Move the developer projects into the new Organization. 4. Set the policies for all projects on the Organization. 5. Additionally, set the production policies on the גProductionגfolder.

1. Create folders under the Organization resource named גDevelopmentגand גProduction.2 €גGrant all developers the Project Creator IAM role on the גDevelopmentגfolder. 3. Move the developer projects into the גDevelopmentגfolder. 4. Set the policies for all projects on the Organization. 5. Additionally, set the production policies on the גProductionגfolder.

1. Designate the Organization for production projects only. 2. Ensure that developers do not have the Project Creator IAM role on the Organization. 3. Create development projects outside of the Organization using the developer Google Workspace accounts. 4. Set the policies for all projects on the Organization. 5. Additionally, set the production policies on the individual production projects.

optionExplanations.A: Creating a second Workspace account and Organization adds unnecessary complexity and administrative overhead, and granting Project Creator at the new Organization root does not prevent developers from later creating projects outside of organizational control; it does not follow Google's recommended single-organization hierarchy practice.
optionExplanations.B: Granting all developers Project Creator at the Organization root (rather than at a Development folder) gives them broad permissions across the whole hierarchy, which conflicts with the goal of restricting modification of production projects and following least privilege.
optionExplanations.C: While using folders for Development and Production with centralized policies is conceptually reasonable, this option still requires moving externally created developer projects into the organization and grants Project Creator broadly, which is less aligned with the recommended approach of designating the Organization for production and managing developer project creation outside it as described in option D.
optionExplanations.D: Designating the Organization for production projects, withholding the Project Creator role from developers at the Organization level, and allowing developers to create projects in their own Workspace accounts outside the Organization (which can later be brought in if needed) follows Google-recommended practices for separating production governance from developer experimentation while allowing centralized policy enforcement on production projects and minimizing future disruption.

text: Your company has an application running on Compute Engine that allows users to play their favorite music. There are a fixed number of instances. Files are stored in Cloud Storage, and data is streamed directly to users. Users are reporting that they sometimes need to attempt to play popular songs multiple times before they are successful. You need to improve the performance of the application. What should you do?

options.text: 1. Mount the Cloud Storage bucket using gcsfuse on all backend Compute Engine instances. 2. Serve music files directly from the backend Compute Engine instance.

1. Create a Cloud Filestore NFS volume and attach it to the backend Compute Engine instances. 2. Download popular songs in Cloud Filestore. 3. Serve music files directly from the backend Compute Engine instance.
1. Copy popular songs into CloudSQL as a blob. 2. Update application code to retrieve data from CloudSQL when Cloud Storage is overloaded.

1. Create a managed instance group with Compute Engine instances. 2. Create a global load balancer and configure it with two backends: ג Managed instance group גCloud Storage bucket 3. Enable Cloud CDN on the bucket backend.

optionExplanations.A: Mounting the Cloud Storage bucket via gcsfuse and serving popular files directly from the local Compute Engine instance reduces repeated reads from Cloud Storage for hot files, lowering latency and contention so that popular songs stream reliably without users needing multiple attempts.
optionExplanations.B: Provisioning a Cloud Filestore NFS volume and manually downloading popular songs adds significant operational overhead (managing which songs are 'popular' and keeping Filestore in sync) and introduces an additional persistent storage cost and complexity that is not the simplest fix for this caching problem.
optionExplanations.C: Storing audio files as blobs in Cloud SQL is an anti-pattern for large media files; Cloud SQL is not designed for serving large binary streaming content and would introduce database load and scaling issues.
optionExplanations.D: While a global load balancer with Cloud CDN enabled on a Cloud Storage backend is a valid caching pattern, it requires re-architecting the serving path with a load balancer and backend buckets, which is a larger change than directly addressing the issue at the existing fixed Compute Engine instances.

text: The operations team in your company wants to save Cloud VPN log events for one year. You need to configure the cloud infrastructure to save the logs. What should you do?

options.text: Set up a filter in Cloud Logging and a Cloud Storage bucket as an export target for the logs you want to save.
Enable the Compute Engine API, and then enable logging on the firewall rules that match the traffic you want to save.
Set up a Cloud Logging Dashboard titled Cloud VPN Logs, and then add a chart that queries for the VPN metrics over a one-year time period.
Set up a filter in Cloud Logging and a topic in Pub/Sub to publish the logs.

optionExplanations.A: Creating a log sink filter in Cloud Logging that exports the relevant Cloud VPN logs to a Cloud Storage bucket allows the logs to be retained durably for the required one-year period, with lifecycle rules available to manage cost, which is the standard Google-recommended pattern for long-term log retention.
optionExplanations.B: Enabling firewall rule logging captures network traffic matching firewall rules, not Cloud VPN tunnel/gateway log events, so it does not address the requirement to save VPN log events.
optionExplanations.C: A Logging dashboard only visualizes log-based metrics in near real time and does not provide long-term storage or retention of the underlying log entries for a year.
optionExplanations.D: Exporting logs to a Pub/Sub topic is useful for real-time processing or integration with external systems, but Pub/Sub messages are not retained for a year by default and are not intended as a long-term log storage solution.

text: You are working with a data warehousing team that performs data analysis. The team needs to process data from external partners, but the data contains personally identifiable information (PII). You need to process and store the data without storing any of the PIIE data. What should you do?

options.text: Create a Dataflow pipeline to retrieve the data from the external sources. As part of the pipeline, use the Cloud Data Loss Prevention (Cloud DLP) API to remove any PII data. Store the result in BigQuery.

Create a Dataflow pipeline to retrieve the data from the external sources. As part of the pipeline, store all non-PII data in BigQuery and store all PII data in a Cloud Storage bucket that has a retention policy set.

Ask the external partners to upload all data on Cloud Storage. Configure Bucket Lock for the bucket. Create a Dataflow pipeline to read the data from the bucket. As part of the pipeline, use the Cloud Data Loss Prevention (Cloud DLP) API to remove any PII data. Store the result in BigQuery.

Ask the external partners to import all data in your BigQuery dataset. Create a dataflow pipeline to copy the data into a new table. As part of the Dataflow bucket, skip all data in columns that have PII data

optionExplanations.A: Using a Dataflow pipeline with the Cloud DLP API to inspect and de-identify or redact PII before writing the results to BigQuery ensures that no PII data is ever persisted, satisfying the requirement to process and store the data without retaining PII while keeping the pipeline fully managed and scalable.
optionExplanations.B: This approach explicitly stores PII data (in Cloud Storage with a retention policy), which directly violates the requirement to not store any PII data, even though it sets a retention policy.
optionExplanations.C: While using Cloud Storage with Bucket Lock and a DLP-based Dataflow pipeline could work, requiring partners to first land raw PII data in a locked Cloud Storage bucket adds unnecessary complexity and a stage where PII is stored (even temporarily) in a bucket configured for immutability, making it a less direct and more costly solution than processing in-flight with Dataflow and DLP.
optionExplanations.D: Having external partners write PII data directly into your BigQuery dataset means PII is stored in BigQuery (even temporarily before being copied), violating the requirement, and 'skipping columns' during a copy is not a robust or built-in DLP-based redaction mechanism.

text: You want to allow your operations team to store logs from all the production projects in your Organization, without including logs from other projects. All of the production projects are contained in a folder. You want to ensure that all logs for existing and new production projects are captured automatically. What should you do?

options.text: Create an aggregated export on the Production folder. Set the log sink to be a Cloud Storage bucket in an operations project.
Create an aggregated export on the Organization resource. Set the log sink to be a Cloud Storage bucket in an operations project.
Create log exports in the production projects. Set the log sinks to be a Cloud Storage bucket in an operations project.
Create log exports in the production projects. Set the log sinks to be BigQuery datasets in the production projects, and grant IAM access to the operations team to run queries on the datasets.

optionExplanations.A: An aggregated export at the folder level with includeChildren can capture logs from current and future projects placed under that folder, but configuring the sink at the top-level Organization resource is the more broadly recommended placement for centralized log collection in Google's documented patterns, since it provides a single consistent point of control regardless of how the folder hierarchy evolves over time.
optionExplanations.B: Creating an aggregated export (log sink) at the Organization resource level with includeChildren enabled, filtered to the Production folder's projects, and routed to a Cloud Storage bucket in an operations project automatically captures logs from all current and future projects under that scope without per-project configuration, which is the Google-recommended approach for centralized log collection across an organization hierarchy.
optionExplanations.C: Creating individual log exports in each production project requires manually configuring a sink for every existing project and remembering to do so for every new project, which does not meet the requirement to automatically capture logs for new production projects.
optionExplanations.D: Per-project sinks to BigQuery datasets within each production project still require manual per-project setup for new projects, and granting the operations team IAM access to many distributed datasets is more operationally complex than a single centralized aggregated export.

text: Your company has an application that is running on multiple instances of Compute Engine. It generates 1 TB per day of logs. For compliance reasons, the logs need to be kept for at least two years. The logs need to be available for active query for 30 days. After that, they just need to be retained for audit purposes. You want to implement a storage solution that is compliant, minimizes costs, and follows Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.text: 1. Install a Cloud Logging agent on all instances. 2. Create a sink to export logs into a regional Cloud Storage bucket. 3. Create an Object Lifecycle rule to move files into a Coldline Cloud Storage bucket after one month. 4. Configure a retention policy at the bucket level using bucket lock.

1. Write a daily cron job, running on all instances, that uploads logs into a Cloud Storage bucket. 2. Create a sink to export logs into a regional Cloud Storage bucket. 3. Create an Object Lifecycle rule to move files into a Coldline Cloud Storage bucket after one month.
1. Install a Cloud Logging agent on all instances. 2. Create a sink to export logs into a partitioned BigQuery table. 3. Set a time_partitioning_expiration of 30 days.
1. Create a daily cron job, running on all instances, that uploads logs into a partitioned BigQuery table. 2. Set a time_partitioning_expiration of 30 days.

optionExplanations.A: While installing a Logging agent and exporting to Cloud Storage with lifecycle rules and Bucket Lock can satisfy retention, exporting raw logs to Cloud Storage makes them unsuitable for 'active query' for 30 days since Cloud Storage does not provide a native query interface like BigQuery does, making this less optimal than the BigQuery-based option for the active query requirement.
optionExplanations.B: Writing a custom daily cron job to upload logs is not a Google-recommended practice when a managed Cloud Logging agent and sink-based export exist; it adds operational burden and risk of log loss, and still does not provide an active-query experience.
optionExplanations.C: Installing the Cloud Logging agent and creating a sink to export logs into a partitioned BigQuery table allows the operations team to actively query the logs using SQL during the 30-day active period, and setting time_partitioning_expiration to 30 days automatically deletes (ages out) data from the partitions—paired with BigQuery's low storage cost for long-term data, this best balances active query needs, compliance retention, and cost using Google-recommended logging export practices.
optionExplanations.D: A custom daily cron job to write directly into BigQuery duplicates functionality already provided by Cloud Logging sinks, is more error-prone and operationally heavier, and is not the Google-recommended approach compared to using a managed log export sink.

text: Your company has just recently activated Cloud Identity to manage users. The Google Cloud Organization has been configured as well. The security team needs to secure projects that will be part of the Organization. They want to prohibit IAM users outside the domain from gaining permissions from now on. What should they do?

options.text: Configure an organization policy to restrict identities by domain.
Configure an organization policy to block creation of service accounts.
Configure Cloud Scheduler to trigger a Cloud Function every hour that removes all users that don't belong to the Cloud Identity domain from all projects.

Create a technical user (e.g., crawler@yourdomain.com), and give it the project owner role at root organization level. Write a bash script that: ג¢ Lists all the IAM rules of all projects within the organization. ג¢ Deletes all users that do not belong to the company domain. Create a Compute Engine instance in a project within the Organization and configure gcloud to be executed with technical user credentials. Configure a cron job that executes the bash script every hour.

optionExplanations.A: The Domain Restricted Sharing organization policy constraint can prevent new IAM bindings from being granted to identities outside specified domains, but it only blocks future grants going forward and does not detect or remove identities that may already have been granted access, so it does not by itself provide ongoing detection and cleanup of unauthorized external members.
optionExplanations.B: Blocking creation of service accounts addresses a different concern (limiting proliferation of service account credentials) and does not prevent or detect external human IAM users from being granted permissions on projects.
optionExplanations.C: Using Cloud Scheduler and a Cloud Function to periodically purge non-domain IAM bindings is a reasonable automation pattern, but it is less direct than a continuously enforced custom solution and is not the option identified as best addressing ongoing detection and removal across the entire organization hierarchy.
optionExplanations.D: Creating a dedicated technical service account with org-level Owner permissions, scripting it to enumerate IAM bindings across every project in the organization and remove any users that do not belong to the company's Cloud Identity domain, and scheduling this script hourly via cron on a Compute Engine instance provides an automated, continuously enforced mechanism that detects and removes unauthorized external identities from all projects on an ongoing basis.

text: Your company has an application running on Google Cloud that is collecting data from thousands of physical devices that are globally distributed. Data is published to Pub/Sub and streamed in real time into an SSD Cloud Bigtable cluster via a Dataflow pipeline. The operations team informs you that your Cloud Bigtable cluster has a hotspot, and queries are taking longer than expected. You need to resolve the problem and prevent it from happening in the future. What should you do?

options.text: Advise your clients to use HBase APIs instead of NodeJS APIs.
Delete records older than 30 days.
Review your RowKey strategy and ensure that keys are evenly spread across the alphabet.
Double the number of nodes you currently have.

optionExplanations.A: Switching client API libraries (HBase vs NodeJS) does not affect how data is distributed across Bigtable nodes and would not resolve a hotspotting issue caused by row key design.
optionExplanations.B: Deleting old records reduces total data volume but does not address the root cause of a hotspot, which is an uneven write/read distribution caused by row key design; the hotspot would likely recur with new data.
optionExplanations.C: Bigtable hotspots are most commonly caused by poorly designed row keys (e.g., monotonically increasing keys like timestamps or sequential device IDs) that concentrate reads/writes on a small number of tablets/nodes; redesigning the row key strategy to distribute keys evenly (e.g., using a hash prefix or salting) is the correct long-term fix recommended by Google for resolving and preventing Bigtable hotspots.
optionExplanations.D: Doubling the number of nodes increases overall cluster capacity and may temporarily mask the symptom, but it does not fix the underlying uneven key distribution, so the hotspot on specific nodes/tablets would likely persist or recur, and it increases cost unnecessarily.

text: Your company has a Google Cloud project that uses BigQuery for data warehousing. There are some tables that contain personally identifiable information (PII). Only the compliance team may access the PII. The other information in the tables must be available to the data science team. You want to minimize cost and the time it takes to assign appropriate access to the tables. What should you do?

options.text: 1. From the dataset where you have the source data, create views of tables that you want to share, excluding PII. 2. Assign an appropriate project-level IAM role to the members of the data science team. 3. Assign access controls to the dataset that contains the view.

1. From the dataset where you have the source data, create materialized views of tables that you want to share, excluding PII. 2. Assign an appropriate project-level IAM role to the members of the data science team. 3. Assign access controls to the dataset that contains the view.

1. Create a dataset for the data science team. 2. Create views of tables that you want to share, excluding PII. 3. Assign an appropriate project-level IAM role to the members of the data science team. 4. Assign access controls to the dataset that contains the view. 5. Authorize the view to access the source dataset.

1. Create a dataset for the data science team. 2. Create materialized views of tables that you want to share, excluding PII. 3. Assign an appropriate project-level IAM role to the members of the data science team. 4. Assign access controls to the dataset that contains the view. 5. Authorize the view to access the source dataset.

optionExplanations.A: Creating views in the same dataset as the source PII tables and then granting project-level IAM and dataset-level access controls on that same dataset would still expose the underlying PII tables to anyone with access to that dataset, since BigQuery dataset-level ACLs apply to the whole dataset, not just the view.
optionExplanations.B: Materialized views in BigQuery are typically used for performance/cost optimization on large aggregations and have restrictions (e.g., they cannot reference views or perform certain operations); placing them in the same dataset as the PII source still risks exposing the underlying PII data through shared dataset-level access controls, and materialized views add unnecessary complexity and cost for this access-control use case.
optionExplanations.C: Creating a separate dataset for the data science team containing only non-PII views, granting the appropriate project-level IAM role and dataset access controls on this new dataset, and then authorizing the view to access the source dataset (creating an 'authorized view') ensures the data science team can only query the de-identified view while having no direct access to the source dataset containing PII, which is the standard, low-cost BigQuery pattern for column-level data segregation and access control.
optionExplanations.D: Materialized views are unnecessarily complex and costly for this use case (they involve storage and refresh costs and have query restrictions), and a standard authorized view in a separate dataset is the simpler, lower-cost, Google-recommended way to share filtered data while restricting access to PII.

text: Your operations team currently stores 10 TB of data in an object storage service from a third-party provider. They want to move this data to a Cloud Storage bucket as quickly as possible, following Google-recommended practices. They want to minimize the cost of this data migration. Which approach should they use?

options.text: Use the gsutil mv command to move the data.
Use the Storage Transfer Service to move the data.
Download the data to a Transfer Appliance, and ship it to Google.
Download the data to the on-premises data center, and upload it to the Cloud Storage bucket.

optionExplanations.A: The gsutil mv command is intended for moving objects already within Google Cloud Storage (or small-scale transfers) and is not designed for efficient, resumable, large-scale transfers from a third-party cloud provider.
optionExplanations.B: Storage Transfer Service is purpose-built for migrating large datasets from other cloud providers (such as AWS S3 or Azure Blob Storage) or HTTP/HTTPS sources into Cloud Storage; it is a managed, serverless service that handles retries, scheduling, and incremental transfers efficiently and at low cost, making it the Google-recommended approach for this scenario.
optionExplanations.C: A Transfer Appliance is intended for offline migration of very large datasets (typically hundreds of TBs to PBs) from on-premises environments where network transfer is impractical; for 10 TB moving between two cloud object stores, shipping physical hardware is unnecessary, slower, and more costly than an online transfer service.
optionExplanations.D: Manually downloading 10 TB to an on-premises data center and re-uploading it to Cloud Storage adds unnecessary network hops, time, egress costs from the third-party provider plus ingress/upload time, and operational effort compared to a direct cloud-to-cloud transfer via Storage Transfer Service.

text: You have a Compute Engine managed instance group that adds and removes Compute Engine instances from the group in response to the load on your application. The instances have a shutdown script that removes REDIS database entries associated with the instance. You see that many database entries have not been removed, and you suspect that the shutdown script is the problem. You need to ensure that the commands in the shutdown script are run reliably every time an instance is shut down. You create a Cloud Function to remove the database entries. What should you do next?

options.text: Modify the shutdown script to wait for 30 seconds before triggering the Cloud Function.
Do not use the Cloud Function. Modify the shutdown script to restart if it has not completed in 30 seconds.
Set up a Cloud Monitoring sink that triggers the Cloud Function after an instance removal log message arrives in Cloud Logging.
Modify the shutdown script to wait for 30 seconds and then publish a message to a Pub/Sub queue.

optionExplanations.A: Modifying the shutdown script to wait 30 seconds before triggering the Cloud Function gives the script time to reliably invoke the function (e.g., via an HTTP call or Pub/Sub publish) before the instance is fully terminated, addressing the unreliability of shutdown scripts which have a limited time window to execute before the VM is forcibly stopped.
optionExplanations.B: Abandoning the Cloud Function and instead having the shutdown script restart itself if not complete does not solve the underlying problem of shutdown scripts having an unreliable, time-limited execution window during instance termination, and restarting a shutdown script is not a supported reliable pattern.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud Monitoring does not have a 'sink' construct that triggers Cloud Functions from log messages; this conflates Cloud Logging sinks (which can route logs to Pub/Sub, which can then trigger a function) with Cloud Monitoring, making this option technically inaccurate as described.
optionExplanations.D: Publishing a message to Pub/Sub from the shutdown script after a 30-second wait could work in principle, but it adds unnecessary complexity (an extra Pub/Sub topic and subscription) compared to simply giving the existing shutdown script enough time to call the Cloud Function directly, and the question is asking for the simplest next step to make the existing approach reliable.

text: You are managing several projects on Google Cloud and need to interact on a daily basis with BigQuery, Bigtable, and Kubernetes Engine using the gcloud CL tool. You are travelling a lot and work on different workstations during the week. You want to avoid having to manage the gcloud CLI manually. What should you do?

options.text: Use Google Cloud Shell in the Google Cloud Console to interact with Google Cloud.
Create a Compute Engine instance and install gcloud on the instance. Connect to this instance via SSH to always use the same gcloud installation when interacting with Google Cloud.
Install gcloud on all of your workstations. Run the command gcloud components auto-update on each workstation
Use a package manager to install gcloud on your workstations instead of installing it manually.

optionExplanations.A: Google Cloud Shell provides a free, browser-based shell environment with the gcloud CLI, kubectl, and other tools pre-installed and automatically kept up to date by Google, accessible from any workstation with a browser, which eliminates the need to manually install or update gcloud across multiple machines.
optionExplanations.B: Creating and maintaining a dedicated Compute Engine instance for CLI access introduces ongoing cost (the instance must run continuously or be started/stopped) and operational overhead (patching, updates, SSH key management) that Cloud Shell avoids entirely.
optionExplanations.C: Installing gcloud on every workstation and relying on auto-update still requires initial manual installation and configuration on each machine, and managing multiple installations across different workstations does not eliminate the management burden as effectively as using Cloud Shell.
optionExplanations.D: Using a package manager simplifies installation somewhat but still requires installing and maintaining gcloud separately on each workstation visited, which does not fully avoid manual management across many different machines while traveling.

text: Your company recently acquired a company that has infrastructure in Google Cloud. Each company has its own Google Cloud organization. Each company is using a Shared Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) to provide network connectivity for its applications. Some of the subnets used by both companies overlap. In order for both businesses to integrate, the applications need to have private network connectivity. These applications are not on overlapping subnets. You want to provide connectivity with minimal re-engineering. What should you do?

options.text: Set up VPC peering and peer each Shared VPC together.
Migrate the projects from the acquired company into your company's Google Cloud organization. Re-launch the instances in your companies Shared VPC.
Set up a Cloud VPN gateway in each Shared VPC and peer Cloud VPNs.
Configure SSH port forwarding on each application to provide connectivity between applications in the different Shared VPCs.

optionExplanations.A: VPC Network Peering does not support peering between VPCs that have any overlapping subnet ranges anywhere in the network, even if the specific applications needing connectivity are on non-overlapping subnets, so peering the entire Shared VPCs together would fail or be blocked due to the overlapping ranges elsewhere in the networks.
optionExplanations.B: Migrating projects and re-launching all instances into a single Shared VPC would require extensive re-engineering (re-IP addressing, redeployment) and does not meet the requirement to minimize re-engineering effort.
optionExplanations.C: Setting up Cloud VPN gateways in each Shared VPC and establishing VPN tunnels between them allows selective routing to be configured between only the non-overlapping subnets that need to communicate, working around the overlapping address space elsewhere in the networks (since VPN tunnels use specific route advertisements rather than requiring full peering compatibility), achieving private connectivity with minimal re-engineering.
optionExplanations.D: Configuring SSH port forwarding on individual applications is a manual, fragile, application-by-application workaround that does not provide general private network connectivity and would not scale or meet typical architecture requirements.

text: You are managing several internal applications that are deployed on Compute Engine. Business users inform you that an application has become very slow over the past few days. You want to find the underlying cause in order to solve the problem. What should you do first?

options.text: Inspect the logs and metrics from the instances in Cloud Logging and Cloud Monitoring.
Change the Compute Engine Instances behind the application to a machine type with more CPU and memory.
Restore a backup of the application database from a time before the application became slow.
Deploy the applications on a managed instance group with autoscaling enabled. Add a load balancer in front of the managed instance group, and have the users connect to the IP of the load balancer.

optionExplanations.A: Inspecting logs and metrics in Cloud Logging and Cloud Monitoring is the appropriate first diagnostic step to identify the underlying cause of a performance degradation (e.g., CPU, memory, disk I/O, network bottlenecks, or application errors) before taking any remediation action.
optionExplanations.B: Changing the machine type to add more CPU/memory is a remediation action taken without first diagnosing the root cause, and may not address the actual problem (e.g., if the issue is a database query, network latency, or application bug) while incurring unnecessary cost.
optionExplanations.C: Restoring a database backup is a drastic action that could cause data loss and should only be considered after diagnosis confirms the database is the cause and that a restore is an appropriate fix; it is not an appropriate first step.
