optionExplanations.D: Re-architecting the application onto a managed instance group with autoscaling and a load balancer is a significant infrastructure change that should be informed by root-cause analysis first; jumping to this without understanding the actual bottleneck may not solve the problem and is a large undertaking for a 'first' step.

text: Your company has an application running as a Deployment in a Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) cluster. When releasing new versions of the application via a rolling deployment, the team has been causing outages. The root cause of the outages is misconfigurations with parameters that are only used in production. You want to put preventive measures for this in the platform to prevent outages. What should you do?

options.text: Configure liveness and readiness probes in the Pod specification.
Configure health checks on the managed instance group.
Create a Scheduled Task to check whether the application is available.
Configure an uptime alert in Cloud Monitoring.

optionExplanations.A: Liveness and readiness probes help Kubernetes determine if a container is healthy and ready to receive traffic, which can mitigate symptoms of a bad rollout, but they do not specifically catch misconfigured production-only parameters before a release; the question asks about preventing the misconfiguration itself, which is better addressed by validating configuration before deployment.
optionExplanations.B: Configuring health checks on a managed instance group is not applicable to a GKE Deployment workload, which uses Kubernetes-native probes and services rather than Compute Engine MIG health checks, making this an irrelevant/incorrect mechanism for this GKE-based scenario.
optionExplanations.C: A scheduled task that periodically checks application availability is a reactive monitoring measure that detects an outage after it has already occurred, rather than preventing a misconfiguration from being deployed in the first place.
optionExplanations.D: An uptime alert in Cloud Monitoring notifies the team after the application becomes unavailable, which is a reactive detection mechanism rather than a preventive measure that stops a misconfigured rollout from causing an outage in the first place.

text: Your company uses Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) as a platform for all workloads. Your company has a single large GKE cluster that contains batch, stateful, and stateless workloads. The GKE cluster is configured with a single node pool with 200 nodes. Your company needs to reduce the cost of this cluster but does not want to compromise availability. What should you do?

options.text: Create a second GKE cluster for the batch workloads only. Allocate the 200 original nodes across both clusters.
Configure CPU and memory limits on the namespaces in the cluster. Configure all Pods to have a CPU and memory limits.
Configure a HorizontalPodAutoscaler for all stateless workloads and for all compatible stateful workloads. Configure the cluster to use node auto scaling.
Change the node pool to use preemptible VMs.

optionExplanations.A: Splitting into a second cluster for batch workloads and dividing the existing 200 nodes across two clusters adds operational overhead (managing two clusters) and does not directly reduce the total node count or cost; it just redistributes the same capacity.
optionExplanations.B: Setting CPU and memory requests/limits on namespaces and Pods enables the cluster (and components like the cluster autoscaler and scheduler) to pack workloads more efficiently and right-size resource consumption, which is a foundational step that allows the cluster to be scaled down appropriately without compromising availability, directly reducing wasted capacity and cost.
optionExplanations.C: Configuring HorizontalPodAutoscaler and node auto scaling is a good practice for dynamically matching capacity to demand, but without first defining proper resource requests/limits on Pods, the autoscalers cannot make accurate scaling decisions, making this less effective as the primary first step compared to setting resource limits.
optionExplanations.D: Switching the entire single node pool (including stateful workloads) to preemptible VMs reduces cost but compromises availability, since preemptible VMs can be terminated at any time with only short notice, which is unsuitable for stateful workloads and violates the requirement to not compromise availability.

text: Your company has a Google Cloud project that uses BigQuery for data warehousing on a pay-per-use basis. You want to monitor queries in real time to discover the most costly queries and which users spend the most. What should you do?

options.text: 1. In the BigQuery dataset that contains all the tables to be queried, add a label for each user that can launch a query. 2. Open the Billing page of the project. 3. Select Reports. 4. Select BigQuery as the product and filter by the user you want to check.
1. Create a Cloud Logging sink to export BigQuery data access logs to BigQuery. 2. Perform a BigQuery query on the generated table to extract the information you need.
1. Create a Cloud Logging sink to export BigQuery data access logs to Cloud Storage. 2. Develop a Dataflow pipeline to compute the cost of queries split by users.
1. Activate billing export into BigQuery. 2. Perform a BigQuery query on the billing table to extract the information you need.

optionExplanations.A: Adding labels to BigQuery datasets/tables for each user and then using the Billing Reports page filtered by the BigQuery product and label allows cost to be broken down by user/label in near real time through the Cloud Billing reports console, directly addressing the need to see which users and queries are costing the most without building custom pipelines.
optionExplanations.B: While exporting BigQuery data access audit logs to a BigQuery table and querying it can provide query-level details, this requires building and maintaining custom queries to compute costs per user, which is more complex and less 'real-time' than using labels with the built-in Billing Reports.
optionExplanations.C: Exporting audit logs to Cloud Storage and building a custom Dataflow pipeline to compute per-user query costs is a heavyweight, custom-engineered solution that requires significant development and maintenance effort compared to using built-in labeling and billing reports.
optionExplanations.D: Billing export to BigQuery provides aggregated cost data at the SKU/service level but does not break down costs by individual query or by which BigQuery user ran a query unless combined with labels, making it insufficient on its own to identify the most costly queries and top-spending users at a granular level.

text: Your company and one of its partners each have a Google Cloud project in separate organizations. Your company's project (prj-a) runs in Virtual Private Cloud (vpc-a). The partner's project (prj-b) runs in vpc-b. There are two instances running on vpc-a and one instance running on vpc-b. Subnets defined in both VPCs are not overlapping. You need to ensure that all instances communicate with each other via internal IPs, minimizing latency and maximizing throughput. What should you do?

options.text: Set up a network peering between vpc-a and vpc-b.
Set up a VPN between vpc-a and vpc-b using Cloud VPN.

Configure IAP TCP forwarding on the instance in vpc-b, and then launch the following gcloud command from one of the instances in vpc-a gcloud: gcloud compute start-iap-tunnel INSTANCE_NAME_IN_VPC_8 22 \ --local-host-port=localhost:22

1. Create an additional instance in vpc-a. 2. Create an additional instance in vpc-b. 3. Install OpenVPN in newly created instances. 4. Configure a VPN tunnel between vpc-a and vpc-b with the help of OpenVPN.

optionExplanations.A: VPC Network Peering between vpc-a and vpc-b allows instances in both non-overlapping VPCs (across different organizations) to communicate using internal/private IP addresses with low latency and high throughput, as traffic stays on Google's internal network without traversing the public internet, which is the recommended approach when subnets do not overlap.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud VPN establishes encrypted tunnels typically over the internet (or via Interconnect) and introduces additional encryption/encapsulation overhead and potential bandwidth caps per tunnel, making it less optimal for minimizing latency and maximizing throughput compared to direct VPC Peering when peering is possible.
optionExplanations.C: IAP TCP forwarding is designed for secure remote access (e.g., SSH/RDP) to individual instances without external IPs, not for general-purpose, high-throughput inter-VPC application communication between multiple instances.
optionExplanations.D: Deploying and managing OpenVPN on additional instances introduces unnecessary operational overhead, additional cost, encryption overhead, and a throughput bottleneck through the VPN instances, which is inferior to native VPC Peering for this non-overlapping subnet scenario.

text: You want to store critical business information in Cloud Storage buckets. The information is regularly changed, but previous versions need to be referenced on a regular basis. You want to ensure that there is a record of all changes to any information in these buckets. You want to ensure that accidental edits or deletions can be easily rolled back. Which feature should you enable?

options.text: Bucket Lock
Object Versioning
Object change notification
Object Lifecycle Management

optionExplanations.A: Bucket Lock enforces a retention policy that prevents objects from being deleted or overwritten until a specified retention period has passed; it is designed for compliance/WORM use cases, not for maintaining a history of changes that can be referenced or rolled back.
optionExplanations.B: Object Versioning maintains a history of every version of an object whenever it is overwritten or deleted, allowing previous versions to be referenced, restored, or rolled back, which directly satisfies the requirement to track all changes and easily roll back accidental edits or deletions.
optionExplanations.C: Object change notification (Pub/Sub notifications for Cloud Storage) alerts external systems when objects change but does not itself retain previous versions of the data or provide rollback capability.
optionExplanations.D: Object Lifecycle Management automates transitioning or deleting objects based on rules (e.g., age, storage class) but does not preserve historical versions of objects or provide a mechanism to roll back changes.

text: You have a Compute Engine application that you want to autoscale when total memory usage exceeds 80%. You have installed the Cloud Monitoring agent and configured the autoscaling policy as follows: ✑ Metric identifier: agent.googleapis.com/memory/percent_used ✑ Filter: metric.label.state = 'used' ✑ Target utilization level: 80 ✑ Target type: GAUGE You observe that the application does not scale under high load. You want to resolve this. What should you do?

options.text: Change the Target type to DELTA_PER_MINUTE.
Change the Metric identifier to agent.googleapis.com/memory/bytes_used.
Change the filter to metric.label.state = 'used' AND metric.label.state = 'buffered' AND metric.label.state = 'cached' AND metric.label.state = 'slab'.
Change the filter to metric.label.state = 'free' and the Target utilization to 20.

optionExplanations.A: Custom Cloud Monitoring metrics used for autoscaling must be configured with a target type that matches how the autoscaler is meant to interpret the metric's behavior over time; changing the Target type to DELTA_PER_MINUTE corrects a mismatch in how the agent.googleapis.com/memory/percent_used metric is being evaluated by the autoscaling policy so that the autoscaler correctly reacts to sustained high memory usage and triggers scale-out actions.
optionExplanations.B: Switching to memory/bytes_used would change the metric from a percentage to an absolute byte count, but the autoscaling policy's target utilization of 80 is expressed as a percentage (80%), so using a bytes-based metric with a percentage target would be a unit mismatch and would not fix the scaling issue.
optionExplanations.C: Adding multiple state filters joined with AND in a single filter expression is logically invalid (a single metric data point cannot simultaneously have label.state equal to multiple different values), so this filter would match no data at all and the autoscaler would have no metric data to act on.
optionExplanations.D: Changing the filter to track 'free' memory and inverting the target to 20% changes the semantic meaning of the policy but does not address the actual configuration issue causing the autoscaler to ignore the metric, and introduces an inverted/confusing policy that is harder to maintain.

text: You are deploying an application to Google Cloud. The application is part of a system. The application in Google Cloud must communicate over a private network with applications in a non-Google Cloud environment. The expected average throughput is 200 kbps. The business requires: ✑ as close to 100% system availability as possible ✑ cost optimization You need to design the connectivity between the locations to meet the business requirements. What should you provision?

options.text: An HA Cloud VPN gateway connected with two tunnels to an on-premises VPN gateway
Two Classic Cloud VPN gateways connected to two on-premises VPN gateways Configure each Classic Cloud VPN gateway to have two tunnels, each connected to different on-premises VPN gateways
Two HA Cloud VPN gateways connected to two on-premises VPN gateways Configure each HA Cloud VPN gateway to have two tunnels, each connected to different on-premises VPN gateways
A single Cloud VPN gateway connected to an on-premises VPN gateway

optionExplanations.A: An HA (Highly Available) Cloud VPN gateway with two tunnels to a single on-premises VPN gateway provides a 99.99% SLA from Google's side at low cost, and for a low-throughput requirement (200 kbps) with a goal of 'as close to 100% as possible' while optimizing cost, a single HA VPN gateway with redundant tunnels offers a strong balance of availability and cost without the expense of dedicated/partner interconnects or multiple gateways.
optionExplanations.B: Classic Cloud VPN gateways only offer a 99.9% SLA (lower than HA VPN's 99.99%), and using two separate Classic VPN gateways each with two tunnels to different on-premises gateways adds significant cost and complexity beyond what is needed for a 200 kbps workload, making it not the most cost-optimized choice.
optionExplanations.C: Provisioning two separate HA VPN gateways, each connected to two different on-premises VPN gateways with redundant tunnels, provides extremely high availability but at a much higher cost (multiple gateways and tunnels) than necessary for a 200 kbps connection, failing the cost optimization requirement.
optionExplanations.D: A single Cloud VPN gateway with only one tunnel (and not specified as HA) represents a single point of failure and does not meet the 'as close to 100% availability as possible' requirement, since Classic VPN with a single tunnel offers lower availability guarantees.

text: Your company has an application running on App Engine that allows users to upload music files and share them with other people. You want to allow users to upload files directly into Cloud Storage from their browser session. The payload should not be passed through the backend. What should you do?

options.text: 1. Set a CORS configuration in the target Cloud Storage bucket where the base URL of the App Engine application is an allowed origin. 2. Use the Cloud Storage Signed URL feature to generate a POST URL.

1. Set a CORS configuration in the target Cloud Storage bucket where the base URL of the App Engine application is an allowed origin. 2. Assign the Cloud Storage WRITER role to users who upload files.
1. Use the Cloud Storage Signed URL feature to generate a POST URL. 2. Use App Engine default credentials to sign requests against Cloud Storage.
1. Assign the Cloud Storage WRITER role to users who upload files. 2. Use App Engine default credentials to sign requests against Cloud Storage.

optionExplanations.A: Configuring CORS on the Cloud Storage bucket to allow the App Engine app's origin, combined with using Cloud Storage Signed URLs to generate a pre-signed POST URL, allows the browser to upload files directly to Cloud Storage without the payload passing through the App Engine backend, while the backend retains control over authorization by generating the signed URL.
optionExplanations.B: Configuring CORS alone allows cross-origin browser requests, but assigning users the Cloud Storage WRITER role would require giving end users direct IAM permissions on the bucket (and managing GCP credentials for them), which is impractical and insecure for typical end-user upload scenarios compared to using signed URLs.
optionExplanations.C: Generating a Signed URL and signing it with the App Engine default service account credentials describes part of the backend's role in the process, but without also configuring a CORS policy on the bucket to allow the App Engine origin, the browser's direct cross-origin upload request to Cloud Storage would be blocked, so this option is incomplete on its own.
optionExplanations.D: Granting end users the Cloud Storage WRITER IAM role and having them use App Engine's default credentials to sign requests is not a viable or secure pattern—end users should not be issued GCP IAM roles or have access to the application's service account credentials for signing.

text: You are configuring the cloud network architecture for a newly created project in Google Cloud that will host applications in Compute Engine. Compute Engine virtual machine instances will be created in two different subnets (sub-a and sub-b) within a single region: • Instances in sub-a will have public IP addresses. • Instances in sub-b will have only private IP addresses. To download updated packages, instances must connect to a public repository outside the boundaries of Google Cloud. You need to allow sub-b to access the external repository. What should you do?

options.text: Enable Private Google Access on sub-b.
Configure Cloud NAT and select sub-b in the NAT mapping section.
Configure a bastion host instance in sub-a to connect to instances in sub-b.
Enable Identity-Aware Proxy for TCP forwarding for instances in sub-b.

optionExplanations.A: Private Google Access allows instances without external IP addresses to reach Google APIs and services (like Cloud Storage or BigQuery) using internal IPs, but it does not provide access to external/public repositories outside of Google Cloud, so it does not solve this requirement.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud NAT provides managed Network Address Translation that allows instances without external IP addresses (such as those in sub-b) to initiate outbound connections to the internet (e.g., to download packages from public repositories) while remaining without public IPs themselves, which directly satisfies the requirement.
optionExplanations.C: Configuring a bastion host in sub-a to connect to instances in sub-b addresses inbound administrative access to private instances, not outbound internet access for package downloads from sub-b instances.
optionExplanations.D: Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) TCP forwarding is used to provide secure inbound access (e.g., SSH/RDP) to instances without public IPs; it does not provide a mechanism for those instances to make outbound connections to external public repositories.

text: Your company is planning to migrate their Windows Server 2022 from their on-premises data center to Google Cloud. You need to bring the licenses that are currently in use in on-premises virtual machines into the target cloud environment. What should you do?

options.text: 1. Create an image of the on-premises virtual machines and upload into Cloud Storage. 2. Import the image as a virtual disk on Compute Engine.
1. Create standard instances on Compute Engine. 2. Select as the OS the same Microsoft Windows version that is currently in use in the on-premises environment.

1. Create an image of the on-premises virtual machine. 2. Import the image as a virtual disk on Compute Engine. 3. Create a standard instance on Compute Engine, selecting as the OS the same Microsoft Windows version that is currently in use in the on- premises environment. 4. Attach a data disk that includes data that matches the created image.

1. Create an image of the on-premises virtual machines. 2. Import the image as a virtual disk on Compute Engine using --os=windows-2022-dc-v . 3. Create a sole-tenancy instance on Compute Engine that uses the imported disk as a boot disk.

optionExplanations.A: Simply creating an image of the on-premises VMs and importing it as a virtual disk does not address Windows Server licensing; without using the correct import flags or sole-tenancy, Google may apply its own per-second Windows licensing on top, resulting in the customer paying for licenses twice (their existing license plus Google's).
optionExplanations.B: Creating standard Compute Engine instances with a Google-provided Windows Server image means the customer would be using Google's licensed Windows images (paying Google's per-second OS licensing fees) rather than bringing their own existing licenses, which does not fulfill the 'bring your own license' (BYOL) requirement.
optionExplanations.C: This approach imports the OS disk image but then creates a separate standard instance with a Google-provided Windows OS and merely attaches a data disk—this still results in Google's standard Windows licensing being applied to the new instance rather than leveraging the customer's existing licenses via BYOL.
optionExplanations.D: Importing the on-premises VM image as a virtual disk using the appropriate BYOL OS flag (e.g., --os=windows-2022-dc-byol or similar identifier indicating a license-free/BYOL import) and deploying it on a sole-tenancy node allows the organization to use its existing Windows Server licenses under Microsoft's licensing terms (which require dedicated hardware for BYOL), satisfying both the licensing and compliance requirements for bringing existing licenses to Google Cloud.

text: You are deploying an application to Google Cloud. The application is part of a system. The application in Google Cloud must communicate over a private network with applications in a non-Google Cloud environment. The expected average throughput is 200 kbps. The business requires: • 99.99% system availability • cost optimization You need to design the connectivity between the locations to meet the business requirements. What should you provision?

options.text: An HA Cloud VPN gateway connected with two tunnels to an on-premises VPN gateway.
A Classic Cloud VPN gateway connected with two tunnels to an on-premises VPN gateway.
Two HA Cloud VPN gateways connected to two on-premises VPN gateways. Configure each HA Cloud VPN gateway to have two tunnels, each connected to different on-premises VPN gateways.
A Classic Cloud VPN gateway connected with one tunnel to an on-premises VPN gateway.

optionExplanations.A: An HA Cloud VPN gateway configured with two tunnels to a single on-premises VPN gateway provides Google's 99.99% SLA for the Cloud VPN side, which directly meets the stated 99.99% availability requirement at a relatively low cost appropriate for a 200 kbps workload, satisfying both availability and cost optimization goals.
optionExplanations.B: A Classic Cloud VPN gateway only offers a 99.9% SLA, even with two tunnels, which does not meet the required 99.99% availability target, since Classic VPN's redundancy model does not provide the same SLA guarantee as HA VPN.
optionExplanations.C: Deploying two separate HA VPN gateways, each connected to different on-premises gateways with redundant tunnels, would provide availability beyond 99.99% but at a significantly higher cost than necessary for a low-throughput (200 kbps) workload, failing the cost optimization requirement.
optionExplanations.D: A Classic Cloud VPN gateway with only a single tunnel provides the lowest availability (no redundancy) and does not come close to meeting the 99.99% availability requirement.

text: Your company wants to migrate their 10-TB on-premises database export into Cloud Storage. You want to minimize the time it takes to complete this activity and the overall cost. The bandwidth between the on-premises environment and Google Cloud is 1 Gbps. You want to follow Google- recommended practices. What should you do?

options.text: Develop a Dataflow job to read data directly from the database and write it into Cloud Storage.
Use the Data Transfer appliance to perform an offline migration.
Use a commercial partner ETL solution to extract the data from the on-premises database and upload it into Cloud Storage.
Upload the data with gcloud storage cp.

optionExplanations.A: Building a custom Dataflow job to read directly from the on-premises database and stream it to Cloud Storage adds development complexity and would still be constrained by the 1 Gbps link, making it more time-consuming and costly to build/maintain than necessary for a one-time 10 TB export migration.
optionExplanations.B: The Transfer Appliance is a Google-provided physical storage device that is shipped to the customer's data center, loaded with data locally at high local-disk speeds, and shipped back to Google for ingestion into Cloud Storage; for a 10 TB dataset, this offline approach avoids consuming the limited 1 Gbps link for an extended period and is the Google-recommended option for minimizing both migration time and cost when online transfer would tie up production bandwidth for too long.
optionExplanations.C: Using a third-party commercial ETL tool to extract and upload the data introduces additional licensing costs and complexity that are unnecessary for a straightforward bulk data export migration to Cloud Storage, and does not necessarily minimize time or cost compared to Google's purpose-built transfer options.
optionExplanations.D: Using gcloud storage cp (or gsutil cp) to upload 10 TB over a 1 Gbps link would take a very long time, consume significant production bandwidth, lacks the resumability/parallelism optimizations of Storage Transfer Service, and is not the Google-recommended approach for large bulk migrations of this size.

text: You are working at a financial institution that stores mortgage loan approval documents on Cloud Storage. Any change to these approval documents must be uploaded as a separate approval file. You need to ensure that these documents cannot be deleted or overwritten for the next 5 years. What should you do?

options.text: Create a retention policy on the bucket for the duration of 5 years. Create a lock on the retention policy.
Create a retention policy organizational constraint constraints/storage.retentionPolicySeconds at the organization level. Set the duration to 5 years.
Use a customer-managed key for the encryption of the bucket. Rotate the key after 5 years.
Create a retention policy organizational constraint constraints/storage.retentionPolicySeconds at the project level. Set the duration to 5 years.

optionExplanations.A: A Cloud Storage retention policy set on the bucket for 5 years enforces that objects cannot be deleted or overwritten until that retention period elapses, and locking the policy makes it permanently immutable (it can never be removed or shortened, only the bucket can eventually be deleted once empty after retention expires), which fully satisfies the WORM (write-once-read-many) compliance requirement for financial documents.
optionExplanations.B: There is no organization-level constraint named constraints/storage.retentionPolicySeconds; retention policies are configured directly on individual Cloud Storage buckets, not via Organization Policy constraints, so this option describes a feature that does not exist.
optionExplanations.C: Customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) and key rotation control how data is encrypted and re-wrapped, but they have no effect on whether an object can be deleted or overwritten, so this does not meet the immutability/retention requirement.
optionExplanations.D: Like option B, there is no project-level organizational policy constraint constraints/storage.retentionPolicySeconds; retention enforcement is done through bucket-level retention policies with optional locks, not through Org Policy constraints at the project level.

text: Your company has decided to make a major revision of their API in order to create better experiences for their developers. They need to keep the old version of the API available and deployable, while allowing new customers and testers to try out the new API. They want to keep the same SSL and DNS records in place to serve both APIs. What should they do?

options.text: Configure a new load balancer for the new version of the API
Reconfigure old clients to use a new endpoint for the new API
Have the old API forward traffic to the new API based on the path
Use separate backend pools for each API path behind the load balancer

optionExplanations.A: Deploying a brand-new load balancer for the new API version would require new IP addresses, SSL certificates, and DNS records, violating the requirement to keep the same SSL and DNS endpoints for both API versions.
optionExplanations.B: Reconfiguring old clients to point to a new endpoint would break the requirement that existing clients continue using the same SSL/DNS endpoint, and forces unnecessary client-side changes for a server-side versioning concern.
optionExplanations.C: Using the existing load balancer's URL map to forward requests to the new API backend based on URL path (e.g., /v2/*) lets both API versions coexist behind the same SSL certificate and DNS record, allowing testers to reach the new API while old clients continue unaffected on the existing paths.
optionExplanations.D: While separate backend pools per path is part of how this is implemented under the hood, simply stating 'use separate backend pools' without routing logic doesn't directly describe the mechanism (path-based forwarding/routing) that achieves the goal as precisely as option C, which explicitly addresses how traffic is directed to the new version.

text: You have a Compute Engine application that you want to autoscale when total memory usage exceeds 80%. You have installed the Cloud Monitoring agent and configured the autoscaling policy as follows: You observe that the application does not scale under high load. You want to resolve this. What should you do?

options.text: Change the Target type to DELTA_PER_MINUTE.
Change the Metric identifier to agent.googleapis.com/memory/bytes_used.
Change the filter to metric.label.state = ‘used’.
Change the filter to metric.label.state = ‘free’ and the Target utilization to 20.

optionExplanations.A: DELTA_PER_MINUTE is a target type used for rate-based metrics (like requests per second), not for a gauge metric such as memory utilization percentage, so changing this would not fix an autoscaling policy based on memory usage threshold.
optionExplanations.B: agent.googleapis.com/memory/bytes_used reports an absolute byte count rather than a percentage, and using raw bytes_used without correctly filtering by state would not align with an 80% utilization target, making this an incomplete fix.
optionExplanations.C: The Cloud Monitoring agent's memory metric (agent.googleapis.com/memory/percent_used) requires a label filter on metric.label.state to specify which memory state (e.g., 'used') to measure; without this filter set correctly to 'used', the autoscaler may be evaluating the wrong state and never crossing the 80% threshold, so correcting the filter to state = 'used' resolves the issue.
optionExplanations.D: Filtering on state = 'free' and setting target utilization to 20 inverts the logic in a confusing way that doesn't match the stated goal of scaling when memory usage exceeds 80%; this introduces unnecessary complexity and is not the straightforward fix compared to filtering directly on 'used' memory.

text: Your company has a Google Cloud project that uses BigOuery for data warehousing. The VPN tunnel between the on-premises environment and Google Cloud is configured with Cloud VPN. Your security team wants to avoid data exfiltration by malicious insiders, compromised code, and accidental oversharing. What should you do?

options.text: Configure Private Service Connect.
Configure VPC Service Controls and configure Private Google Access for on-promises hosts.
Create a service account, grant the BigQuery JobUser role and Storage Object Viewer role to the service account, and remove all other Identity and Access Management (IAM) access from the project.
Configure Private Google Access.

optionExplanations.A: Private Service Connect enables private connectivity to Google APIs or published services via private IP addresses, but on its own it does not establish a security perimeter that prevents data exfiltration of BigQuery data to unauthorized external destinations.
optionExplanations.B: VPC Service Controls create a security perimeter around BigQuery and other GCP services, preventing data from being copied or exfiltrated to resources outside the perimeter (including by compromised credentials or insiders), while configuring Private Google Access allows on-premises hosts connected via Cloud VPN to reach Google APIs (like BigQuery) over private connectivity without traversing the public internet, together addressing both the exfiltration risk and secure on-premises access.
optionExplanations.C: While restricting IAM roles to the minimum required (BigQuery JobUser and Storage Object Viewer) follows least-privilege principles, it does not prevent an authorized but malicious or compromised identity from exporting/copying data outside the organization's boundary, which is the core exfiltration concern that VPC Service Controls is designed to address.
optionExplanations.D: Private Google Access alone only allows VMs without external IP addresses (or on-premises hosts via VPN) to reach Google APIs over private connectivity; it does not create any perimeter or control to prevent data exfiltration from BigQuery to unauthorized destinations.

text: The JencoMart security team requires that all Google Cloud Platform infrastructure is deployed using a least privilege model with separation of duties for administration between production and development resources. What Google domain and project structure should you recommend?

options.text: Create two G Suite accounts to manage users: one for development/test/staging and one for production. Each account should contain one project for every application
Create two G Suite accounts to manage users: one with a single project for all development applications and one with a single project for all production applications
Create a single G Suite account to manage users with each stage of each application in its own project
Create a single G Suite account to manage users with one project for the development/test/staging environment and one project for the production environment

optionExplanations.A: Creating two separate G Suite (Google Workspace/Cloud Identity) accounts/domains for development and production introduces unnecessary administrative overhead and complexity for identity management, and is not the recommended approach—organizations should use a single identity domain with project-level separation for proper governance.
optionExplanations.B: Using two separate domains with a single project each for all dev and all prod applications combines unrelated applications into shared projects, which violates the principle of separating resources for least-privilege IAM control per application/environment, and maintaining two domains adds unnecessary identity management complexity.
optionExplanations.C: While a single domain is correct, putting every stage of every application into its own project (without grouping by dev vs. prod boundary) can create excessive project sprawl and makes it harder to apply consistent separation-of-duties policies between development and production at a higher level.
optionExplanations.D: A single Cloud Identity/G Suite domain centralizes user and group management while a project-level split between development/test/staging and production enables IAM policies to be scoped per project, achieving least privilege and separation of duties (e.g., developers have access to non-prod projects but not the production project) without the overhead of multiple domains.

text: A few days after JencoMart migrates the user credentials database to Google Cloud Platform and shuts down the old server, the new database server stops responding to SSH connections. It is still serving database requests to the application servers correctly. What three steps should you take to diagnose the problem? (Choose three.)

options.text: Delete the virtual machine (VM) and disks and create a new one
Delete the instance, attach the disk to a new VM, and investigate
Take a snapshot of the disk and connect to a new machine to investigate
Check inbound firewall rules for the network the machine is connected to
Connect the machine to another network with very simple firewall rules and investigate
Print the Serial Console output for the instance for troubleshooting, activate the interactive console, and investigate

optionExplanations.A: Deleting the VM and its disks would destroy any forensic evidence needed to diagnose why SSH stopped responding, and is a destructive first step that should be avoided during troubleshooting—data loss risk is too high before root cause is understood.
optionExplanations.B: Deleting the running instance (even if the disk is preserved) is unnecessarily destructive while the database is actively serving production traffic correctly; a non-destructive approach (like taking a snapshot) should be used first to investigate without disrupting the live service.
optionExplanations.C: Taking a snapshot of the boot disk and attaching it to a new diagnostic VM allows you to inspect the filesystem, SSH configuration, and logs (e.g., /var/log/auth.log, sshd_config) without touching the live, still-functioning database server, making it a safe and effective diagnostic step.
optionExplanations.D: Checking the VPC firewall rules for the network is a quick, non-disruptive first check to confirm whether an inbound rule for TCP port 22 (SSH) was inadvertently removed or modified, which is a common cause of SSH connectivity loss after a migration.
optionExplanations.E: Moving the live, currently-functioning production database to a different network with different firewall rules is risky and unnecessary—it could disrupt the application servers' connectivity to the database, and doesn't directly diagnose the SSH issue without that disruption.
optionExplanations.F: The Serial Console provides out-of-band access to the VM's console output and an interactive shell independent of SSH/network connectivity, allowing you to check sshd status, logs, and configuration directly even when SSH itself is broken, making it an essential non-destructive diagnostic tool.

text: JencoMart has decided to migrate user profile storage to Google Cloud Datastore and the application servers to Google Compute Engine (GCE). During the migration, the existing infrastructure will need access to Datastore to upload the data. What service account key-management strategy should you recommend?

options.text: Provision service account keys for the on-premises infrastructure and for the GCE virtual machines (VMs)
Authenticate the on-premises infrastructure with a user account and provision service account keys for the VMs
Provision service account keys for the on-premises infrastructure and use Google Cloud Platform (GCP) managed keys for the VMs
Deploy a custom authentication service on GCE/Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) for the on-premises infrastructure and use GCP managed keys for the VMs

optionExplanations.A: Provisioning and distributing long-lived service account keys to both on-premises infrastructure and GCE VMs increases the attack surface and operational burden of key management/rotation; GCE VMs should instead use the built-in metadata-server-based service account credentials rather than downloaded keys.
optionExplanations.B: Authenticating on-premises infrastructure with a user account is not a recommended practice for automated server-to-server access (user accounts are for humans and tied to individual identity, with session/token limitations), and provisioning keys for VMs is also unnecessary given GCP's attached service account mechanism.
optionExplanations.C: On-premises infrastructure, lacking access to Google's metadata server, must use downloaded service account JSON keys to authenticate to Datastore, while GCE VMs can leverage GCP-managed service account credentials automatically supplied via the instance metadata server—this avoids unnecessary key distribution/rotation overhead for the VMs while still enabling the on-prem systems to authenticate during migration.
optionExplanations.D: Building a custom authentication service for on-premises infrastructure is an over-engineered, costly solution when a simple service account key would suffice for the temporary migration period; this introduces unnecessary development and maintenance overhead.

text: JencoMart has built a version of their application on Google Cloud Platform that serves traffic to Asia. You want to measure success against their business and technical goals. Which metrics should you track?

options.text: Error rates for requests from Asia
Latency difference between US and Asia
Total visits, error rates, and latency from Asia
Total visits and average latency for users from Asia
The number of character sets present in the database

optionExplanations.A: Error rates alone measure reliability/correctness but don't address the business goal of understanding usage/adoption (visits) or the technical performance goal (latency), so this is an incomplete metric set.
optionExplanations.B: Comparing latency between US and Asia provides a relative performance comparison but doesn't capture the absolute business metric of how much traffic/usage the new Asia deployment is generating, which is needed to measure business success.
optionExplanations.C: While total visits, error rates, and latency together would be a thorough set, error rates were not specifically called out as a stated goal in this scenario, making this option broader than what's needed—the more targeted answer focuses on the core business (visits/adoption) and technical (latency) goals.
optionExplanations.D: Total visits measures the business goal (adoption/usage of the service serving Asia) and average latency from Asia measures the technical goal (whether the new regional deployment is delivering improved performance to that audience), directly aligning with both stated business and technical objectives.
optionExplanations.E: The number of character sets in the database is an internationalization/localization detail unrelated to measuring the success of serving traffic to a new region from a business or technical performance standpoint.

text: The migration of JencoMart's application to Google Cloud Platform (GCP) is progressing too slowly. The infrastructure is shown in the diagram. You want to maximize throughput. What are three potential bottlenecks? (Choose three.)

options.text: A single VPN tunnel, which limits throughput
A tier of Google Cloud Storage that is not suited for this task
A copy command that is not suited to operate over long distances
Fewer virtual machines (VMs) in GCP than on-premises machines
A separate storage layer outside the VMs, which is not suited for this task
Complicated internet connectivity between the on-premises infrastructure and GCP

optionExplanations.A: A single VPN tunnel has a throughput cap (typically around 1.5–3 Gbps per tunnel), so if all data migration traffic is funneled through one Cloud VPN tunnel, it becomes a hard bandwidth bottleneck limiting overall migration throughput.
optionExplanations.B: Choosing an inappropriate Cloud Storage class (e.g., a class with a higher latency that requires retrieval delays before write acceptance) is a configuration choice but is less commonly cited as a primary throughput bottleneck compared to networking, transfer tooling, and storage architecture issues described in the other selected options.
optionExplanations.C: Tools such as a basic 'cp' or 'rsync' command are designed for local or short-distance copies and are not optimized for high-latency, long-distance WAN transfers (they don't parallelize, use multiple streams, or handle retries efficiently), making them a significant bottleneck; tools like gsutil with parallel composite uploads or Storage Transfer Service are better suited.
optionExplanations.D: Having fewer VMs in GCP than on-premises is a capacity/scaling consideration for the running application post-migration, but it's not directly a throughput bottleneck for the data migration/transfer process itself.
optionExplanations.E: If the on-premises architecture relies on a separate, slower storage layer (e.g., NFS or SAN with limited IOPS/throughput) external to the compute that's reading data for transfer, this storage layer can throttle how fast data can be read and sent, becoming a bottleneck during migration.
optionExplanations.F: While internet connectivity issues could be a factor, the scenario emphasizes a VPN-based connection (not general internet connectivity); a single VPN tunnel (option A) more precisely captures the connectivity constraint, making this option redundant/less precise compared to the selected answers.

text: JencoMart wants to move their User Profiles database to Google Cloud Platform. Which Google Database should they use?

options.text: Cloud Spanner
Google BigQuery
Google Cloud SQL
Google Cloud Datastore

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Spanner is a globally distributed, strongly consistent relational database designed for very large-scale transactional workloads requiring horizontal scalability across regions; it is more complex and costly than necessary for a typical user profile store and is overkill unless extreme global scale and strong consistency across regions is required.
optionExplanations.B: BigQuery is a fully-managed analytical data warehouse optimized for large-scale OLAP queries and analytics, not for low-latency transactional reads/writes of individual user profile records, making it unsuitable as an operational user profile database.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud SQL is a managed relational database (MySQL/PostgreSQL/SQL Server) suitable for structured, relational data with fixed schemas; while viable for some profile stores, it is less naturally suited to the flexible, schema-less, key-value style access patterns typical of user profile data compared to a NoSQL document store.
optionExplanations.D: Cloud Datastore (now Firestore in Datastore mode) is a managed, horizontally-scalable NoSQL document database ideal for storing semi-structured user profile data with high read/write throughput at global scale and low operational overhead, making it the natural fit for a user profiles database migration.

text: For this question, refer to the Helicopter Racing League (HRL) case study. Your team is in charge of creating a payment card data vault for card numbers used to bill tens of thousands of viewers, merchandise consumers, and season ticket holders. You need to implement a custom card tokenization service that meets the following requirements: * It must provide low latency at minimal cost. * It must be able to identify duplicate credit cards and must not store plaintext card numbers. * It should support annual key rotation. Which storage approach should you adopt for your tokenization service?

options.text: Store the card data in Secret Manager after running a query to identify duplicates.
Encrypt the card data with a deterministic algorithm stored in Firestore using Datastore mode.
Encrypt the card data with a deterministic algorithm and shard it across multiple Memorystore instances.
Use column-level encryption to store the data in Cloud SQL.

optionExplanations.A: Secret Manager is designed for storing a small number of sensitive configuration secrets (like API keys and passwords) with versioning, not for storing and querying large volumes of tokenized card data records or running duplicate-detection queries against them—it lacks the query capabilities needed for this use case.
optionExplanations.B: Firestore in Datastore mode is a NoSQL document database; while it could technically store encrypted card data, it does not provide the same low-latency, cost-effective relational query and indexing capabilities for deduplication and key rotation as a properly designed Cloud SQL schema with column-level encryption, and storing encrypted PAN data in a document database is less aligned with PCI-DSS tokenization patterns.
optionExplanations.C: Sharding encrypted card data across multiple Memorystore (Redis) instances would provide low latency, but Memorystore is an in-memory cache that is not designed as a durable system of record for sensitive payment data—data could be lost on instance failure/restart unless persistence is carefully configured, making it a poor fit for a payment card vault that must reliably store tokenized data long-term.
optionExplanations.D: Cloud SQL with column-level encryption allows the tokenization service to store encrypted card numbers in a relational schema, use deterministic encryption to detect duplicate card numbers via indexed lookups, support periodic re-encryption for annual key rotation, and provide low-latency reads/writes at minimal cost compared to specialized or in-memory alternatives, satisfying all stated requirements.

text: For this question, refer to the Helicopter Racing League (HRL) case study. The HRL development team releases a new version of their predictive capability application every Tuesday evening at 3 a.m. UTC to a repository. The security team at HRL has developed an in-house penetration test Cloud Function called Airwolf. The security team wants to run Airwolf against the predictive capability application as soon as it is released every Tuesday. You need to set up Airwolf to run at the recurring weekly cadence. What should you do?

options.text: Set up Cloud Tasks and a Cloud Storage bucket that triggers a Cloud Function.
Set up a Cloud Logging sink and a Cloud Storage bucket that triggers a Cloud Function.
Configure the deployment job to notify a Pub/Sub queue that triggers a Cloud Function.
Set up Identity and Access Management (IAM) and Confidential Computing to trigger a Cloud Function.

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Tasks can be used to schedule a task that, upon completion of the deployment (e.g., a new artifact landing in a Cloud Storage bucket), triggers a Cloud Storage event notification that invokes the Airwolf Cloud Function, providing the recurring, event-driven trigger tied to the actual release event rather than a fixed schedule, which matches the requirement to run immediately after each weekly release.
optionExplanations.B: While Cloud Logging sinks can route log entries to various destinations, configuring a sink plus a Cloud Storage bucket to trigger a function is a more convoluted and indirect path compared to directly using the artifact's arrival in Cloud Storage as the trigger event.
optionExplanations.C: While a Pub/Sub-triggered Cloud Function is a valid pattern in general, the scenario specifically centers on a release artifact landing in a repository (Cloud Storage), making the Cloud Storage object-creation trigger (paired with Cloud Tasks/notification) the more directly applicable mechanism described in the correct answer.
optionExplanations.D: IAM and Confidential Computing relate to access control and protecting data/code in use during processing; neither is a triggering mechanism for invoking a Cloud Function based on a deployment event, so this does not address the requirement to run Airwolf automatically after each release.

text: For this question, refer to the Helicopter Racing League (HRL) case study. HRL wants better prediction accuracy from their ML prediction models. They want you to use Google's AI Platform so HRL can understand and interpret the predictions. What should you do?

options.text: Use Explainable AI.
Use Vision AI.
Use Google Cloud's operations suite.
Use Jupyter Notebooks.

optionExplanations.A: Explainable AI is the Vertex AI / AI Platform feature specifically designed to provide feature attributions and interpretability for ML model predictions, helping users understand why a model made a particular prediction, directly satisfying HRL's requirement to interpret prediction results and improve trust in model outputs.
optionExplanations.B: Vision AI is a pre-trained API for image analysis (object detection, OCR, label detection) and has nothing to do with interpreting the predictions of HRL's custom ML models for race performance.
optionExplanations.C: Google Cloud's operations suite (formerly Stackdriver) provides monitoring, logging, and alerting for infrastructure and application performance, not interpretability or explanations of ML model predictions.
optionExplanations.D: Jupyter Notebooks (e.g., via Vertex AI Workbench) are a development environment for writing and exploring code/data interactively, but they are a tool for building/exploring models, not a built-in capability for generating prediction explanations—Explainable AI is the specific feature for that purpose.

text: For this question, refer to the Helicopter Racing League (HRL) case study. HRL is looking for a cost-effective approach for storing their race data such as telemetry. They want to keep all historical records, train models using only the previous season's data, and plan for data growth in terms of volume and information collected. You need to propose a data solution. Considering HRL business requirements and the goals expressed by CEO S. Hawke, what should you do?

options.text: Use Firestore for its scalable and flexible document-based database. Use collections to aggregate race data by season and event.
Use Cloud Spanner for its scalability and ability to version schemas with zero downtime. Split race data using season as a primary key.
Use BigQuery for its scalability and ability to add columns to a schema. Partition race data based on season.
Use Cloud SQL for its ability to automatically manage storage increases and compatibility with MySQL. Use separate database instances for each season.

optionExplanations.A: Firestore is a NoSQL document database optimized for transactional, low-latency application data access patterns (like user profiles or game state), not for cost-effective long-term storage and large-scale analytical querying of growing telemetry datasets used for training models on historical season data.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud Spanner is a globally distributed relational database built for high-throughput transactional (OLTP) workloads with strong consistency; it is significantly more expensive than necessary for storing large volumes of historical telemetry data primarily used for analytics and model training.
optionExplanations.C: BigQuery is a cost-effective, serverless data warehouse that scales seamlessly to petabytes, supports schema evolution (adding columns as new telemetry data types are collected), and its partitioning feature (e.g., by season/date) allows queries to be restricted to only the relevant season's data for model training, reducing both cost and query time while retaining all historical records indefinitely.
optionExplanations.D: Cloud SQL has storage and scalability limits that make it impractical for ever-growing telemetry data at scale, and using separate database instances per season fragments the data, increases operational overhead, and makes cross-season historical analysis cumbersome and costly.

text: For this question, refer to the Helicopter Racing League (HRL) case study. A recent finance audit of cloud infrastructure noted an exceptionally high number of Compute Engine instances are allocated to do video encoding and transcoding. You suspect that these Virtual Machines are zombie machines that were not deleted after their workloads completed. You need to quickly get a list of which VM instances are idle. What should you do?

options.text: Log into each Compute Engine instance and collect disk, CPU, memory, and network usage statistics for analysis.
Use the gcloud compute instances list to list the virtual machine instances that have the idle: true label set.
Use the gcloud recommender command to list the idle virtual machine instances.
From the Google Console, identify which Compute Engine instances in the managed instance groups are no longer responding to health check probes.

optionExplanations.A: Manually logging into each Compute Engine instance to gather metrics is extremely time-consuming and does not scale to find idle VMs 'quickly' across a large fleet, making it impractical for this use case.
optionExplanations.B: There is no built-in 'idle: true' label automatically set by GCP on idle VM instances; this would require a custom labeling process that doesn't currently exist, so this command would not return meaningful results out of the box.
optionExplanations.C: The Active Assist Recommender (gcloud recommender) provides a built-in 'Idle VM recommender' that analyzes historical CPU, memory, and network utilization to automatically identify VM instances that appear to be idle and recommends stopping or deleting them, providing a quick, built-in way to get this list without manual analysis.
optionExplanations.D: Checking managed instance group health check status identifies unhealthy instances being recreated, not idle/zombie VMs that are healthy but unused; many zombie VMs for batch encoding/transcoding workloads may not even be part of a managed instance group, so this approach would miss the target VMs.

text: For this question, refer to the EHR Healthcare case study. You are responsible for ensuring that EHR's use of Google Cloud will pass an upcoming privacy compliance audit. What should you do? (Choose two.)

options.text: Verify EHR's product usage against the list of compliant products on the Google Cloud compliance page.
Advise EHR to execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with Google Cloud.
Use Firebase Authentication for EHR's user facing applications.
Implement Prometheus to detect and prevent security breaches on EHR's web-based applications.
Use GKE private clusters for all Kubernetes workloads.

optionExplanations.A: Checking product usage against Google Cloud's published compliance offerings (e.g., HIPAA-eligible services list) is a useful step to confirm that only compliant services are used for regulated healthcare data, helping prepare for the privacy compliance audit.
optionExplanations.B: For a healthcare organization handling protected health information (PHI), executing a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with Google Cloud is a mandatory contractual requirement under HIPAA before storing or processing PHI in Google Cloud services, making this essential for passing a privacy compliance audit.
optionExplanations.C: Firebase Authentication is an identity/authentication service for end-user sign-in; while useful for application security, it is not directly related to preparing for or passing a privacy compliance audit regarding data handling and regulatory agreements.
optionExplanations.D: Implementing security breach detection (intrusion detection/prevention) addresses security monitoring, which is valuable but is more of a general security control rather than a step specific to demonstrating privacy regulatory compliance (like HIPAA) in an audit; Google Cloud's native tools (e.g., Security Command Center) are typically recommended over deploying and managing Prometheus for this purpose.
optionExplanations.E: Using GKE private clusters improves network security posture by removing public IPs from nodes, but it is a general infrastructure security best practice rather than something that directly addresses the privacy compliance audit requirements (BAA, compliant product usage).

text: For this question, refer to the EHR Healthcare case study. You need to define the technical architecture for securely deploying workloads to Google Cloud. You also need to ensure that only verified containers are deployed using Google Cloud services. What should you do? (Choose two.)

options.text: Enable Binary Authorization on GKE, and sign containers as part of a CI/CD pipeline.
Configure Jenkins to utilize Kritis to cryptographically sign a container as part of a CI/CD pipeline.
Configure Container Registry to only allow trusted service accounts to create and deploy containers from the registry.
Configure Container Registry to use vulnerability scanning to confirm that there are no vulnerabilities before deploying the workload.

optionExplanations.A: Binary Authorization is a GKE/Google Cloud deploy-time security control that enforces that only container images signed by trusted authorities (attestors) as part of the CI/CD pipeline can be deployed to the cluster, directly fulfilling the requirement that only verified containers are deployed.
optionExplanations.B: Kritis (or its successor functionality now largely integrated into Binary Authorization) allows a CI/CD pipeline (e.g., run from Jenkins) to cryptographically sign container images after they pass required checks (build, test, vulnerability scan), creating attestations that Binary Authorization can later verify before allowing deployment, completing the signing half of the verified-deployment workflow.
optionExplanations.C: Restricting which service accounts can push/pull from Container Registry controls who can interact with the registry, but it does not verify the integrity or provenance of the container images themselves at deploy time—it doesn't prevent an authorized account from deploying an unsigned or non-compliant image.
optionExplanations.D: Vulnerability scanning identifies known CVEs in container images, which is a valuable security practice, but it does not by itself enforce that only specifically verified/signed images can be deployed—that enforcement requires Binary Authorization with signed attestations.

text: You need to upgrade the EHR connection to comply with their requirements. The new connection design must support business-critical needs and meet the same network and security policy requirements. What should you do?

options.text: Add a new Dedicated Interconnect connection.
Upgrade the bandwidth on the Dedicated Interconnect connection to 100 G.
Add three new Cloud VPN connections.
Add a new Carrier Peering connection.

optionExplanations.A: Adding another Dedicated Interconnect connection would still require EHR to manage physical colocation facility connections and cross-connects, which may not align with the desire for a less operationally intensive, business-critical redundant connection compared to a carrier peering option in this scenario.
optionExplanations.B: Simply upgrading bandwidth on the existing Dedicated Interconnect to 100G increases capacity on a single connection but does not address redundancy/diversity requirements for a business-critical connection (a single connection remains a single point of failure regardless of its bandwidth).
optionExplanations.C: Adding three new Cloud VPN connections would route traffic over the public internet through encrypted tunnels, which typically has lower throughput, higher latency, and less predictable performance than dedicated connectivity options, making it unsuitable for business-critical, high-bandwidth needs.
optionExplanations.D: A Carrier Peering connection provides a direct, enterprise-grade connection to Google's network through a supported service provider without requiring EHR to establish a physical Dedicated Interconnect, offering improved reliability and performance over the public internet while meeting business-critical needs and matching existing security/network policy requirements with less infrastructure overhead than adding interconnects.

text: For this question, refer to the EHR Healthcare case study. You need to define the technical architecture for hybrid connectivity between EHR's on- premises systems and Google Cloud. You want to follow Google's recommended practices for production-level applications. Considering the EHR Healthcare business and technical requirements, what should you do?

options.text: Configure two Partner Interconnect connections in one metro (City), and make sure the Interconnect connections are placed in different metro zones.
Configure two VPN connections from on-premises to Google Cloud, and make sure the VPN devices on-premises are in separate racks.
Configure Direct Peering between EHR Healthcare and Google Cloud, and make sure you are peering at least two Google locations.
Configure two Dedicated Interconnect connections in one metro (City) and two connections in another metro, and make sure the Interconnect connections are placed in different metro zones.

optionExplanations.A: Partner Interconnect is appropriate when EHR's premises are not located in a facility supporting Dedicated Interconnect colocation, but for the highest level of throughput and reliability appropriate for production-critical applications at a large healthcare organization, Dedicated Interconnect is Google's recommended choice over Partner Interconnect when colocation access is feasible.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud VPN over the public internet provides lower throughput (capped per tunnel) and less predictable latency/performance compared to Interconnect options, making it insufficient as the primary connectivity method for production-level, business-critical hybrid workloads, even with redundant VPN devices.
optionExplanations.C: Direct Peering connects directly to Google's edge network for accessing Google public APIs/services but does not provide an SLA and is not intended for accessing private VPC resources, making it unsuitable for secure, reliable hybrid connectivity to Google Cloud VPCs for production workloads.
optionExplanations.D: Google's recommended best practice for high-availability, production-grade hybrid connectivity is to deploy a minimum of four Dedicated Interconnect connections—two in one metro across different edge availability domains/zones and two in a second metro—providing 99.99% availability SLA through full physical and metro-level redundancy, which matches this configuration exactly.

text: For this question, refer to the EHR Healthcare case study. You are a developer on the EHR customer portal team. Your team recently migrated the customer portal application to Google Cloud. The load has increased on the application servers, and now the application is logging many timeout errors. You recently incorporated Pub/Sub into the application architecture, and the application is not logging any Pub/Sub publishing errors. You want to improve publishing latency. What should you do?

options.text: Increase the Pub/Sub Total Timeout retry value.
Move from a Pub/Sub subscriber pull model to a push model.
Turn off Pub/Sub message batching.
Create a backup Pub/Sub message queue.

optionExplanations.A: Increasing the Pub/Sub client library's Total Timeout retry setting allows publish requests more time to succeed before failing, which under high load with transient delays can reduce publish-side timeout errors and improve effective publishing reliability/latency as observed by the application, directly addressing the symptom described.
optionExplanations.B: Switching between push and pull delivery models affects how subscribers receive messages, not how quickly the application can publish messages to a topic; since the issue is about publishing latency/timeouts (not subscription delivery), this change would not address the root cause.
optionExplanations.C: Turning off message batching would cause each message to be sent as an individual network request immediately, which generally increases the number of API calls and can increase—not decrease—publishing overhead and latency under high load, the opposite of the desired improvement.
optionExplanations.D: Creating a backup Pub/Sub topic/queue does not address publishing latency or timeout errors on the primary publish path; it would add architectural complexity without resolving the underlying timeout configuration issue.

text: For this question, refer to the EHR Healthcare case study. In the past, configuration errors put public IP addresses on backend servers that should not have been accessible from the Internet. You need to ensure that no one can put external IP addresses on backend Compute Engine instances and that external IP addresses can only be configured on frontend Compute Engine instances. What should you do?

options.text: Create an Organizational Policy with a constraint to allow external IP addresses only on the frontend Compute Engine instances.
Revoke the compute.networkAdmin role from all users in the project with front end instances.
Create an Identity and Access Management (IAM) policy that maps the IT staff to the compute.networkAdmin role for the organization.
Create a custom Identity and Access Management (IAM) role named GCE_FRONTEND with the compute.addresses.create permission.

optionExplanations.A: While an Organizational Policy constraint exists to disable external IP addresses on VM instances entirely (constraints/compute.vmExternalIpAccess), it is typically applied at a project, folder, or org level as an allow/deny list of instances and doesn't easily provide the fine-grained role-based separation needed to let frontend admins assign external IPs while blocking backend admins—achieving the precise frontend-only carve-out is better handled via custom IAM roles/permissions scoping as in option D.
optionExplanations.B: Revoking the compute.networkAdmin role from all users on the frontend project would prevent legitimate frontend administrators from managing necessary network configurations (including assigning external IPs to frontend instances), which conflicts with the requirement that frontend instances should still be able to get external IPs.
optionExplanations.C: Granting the broad compute.networkAdmin role to IT staff at the organization level gives them permission to add external IP addresses to any Compute Engine instance in any project, including backend instances, which is the opposite of restricting external IP assignment to only frontend instances.
optionExplanations.D: Creating a custom IAM role that includes only the compute.addresses.create permission (and related minimal permissions) and granting it only to staff/service accounts managing frontend resources ensures that only those with this narrowly-scoped role can assign external IP addresses, and it can be granted only on frontend-related resources/projects, preventing anyone without this role (including on backend projects) from configuring external IPs on backend instances.

text: For this question, refer to the EHR Healthcare case study. You are responsible for designing the Google Cloud network architecture for Google Kubernetes Engine. You want to follow Google best practices. Considering the EHR Healthcare business and technical requirements, what should you do to reduce the attack surface?

options.text: Use a private cluster with a private endpoint with master authorized networks configured.
Use a public cluster with firewall rules and Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) routes.
Use a private cluster with a public endpoint with master authorized networks configured.
Use a public cluster with master authorized networks enabled and firewall rules.

optionExplanations.A: A private cluster with a private endpoint means the GKE control plane has no public IP at all, and master authorized networks restricts which internal IP ranges can reach it; however, with no public endpoint at all, this can complicate access for tools/CI systems that need to reach the API server from outside the authorized internal networks, making it less aligned with the 'reduce attack surface while remaining manageable' balance sought—though private endpoints are the most restrictive, the case study's hybrid (on-prem + cloud) requirements often necessitate the public-endpoint-with-authorized-networks approach to allow controlled external administrative access (option C) as the recommended balance in this scenario.
optionExplanations.B: A public cluster (with a publicly accessible control plane endpoint and nodes potentially having external IPs) relying solely on firewall rules and VPC routes leaves the Kubernetes API server's control plane exposed to the internet, significantly increasing the attack surface compared to a private cluster.
optionExplanations.C: A private cluster ensures GKE worker nodes have no external IP addresses (reducing attack surface for the data plane), while a public endpoint for the control plane combined with master authorized networks restricts which IP ranges (e.g., corporate office and on-premises ranges via the existing hybrid connection) can access the Kubernetes API server, providing a strong balance of security and manageability appropriate for EHR's hybrid environment.
optionExplanations.D: A public cluster means worker nodes can have external IP addresses, which significantly increases the attack surface regardless of master authorized networks and firewall rules being configured for the control plane access.

text: Mountkirk Games wants you to design their new testing strategy. How should the test coverage differ from their existing backends on the other platforms?

options.text: Tests should scale well beyond the prior approaches
Unit tests are no longer required, only end-to-end tests
Tests should be applied after the release is in the production environment
Tests should include directly testing the Google Cloud Platform (GCP) infrastructure

optionExplanations.A: Moving to a cloud-native, elastically scalable backend on GCP means the testing strategy itself must scale far beyond what was feasible with the prior fixed-capacity, on-premises/other-platform infrastructure—tests need to validate behavior under massive concurrent load and autoscaling scenarios that weren't previously testable at the same scale.
optionExplanations.B: Eliminating unit tests in favor of only end-to-end tests would reduce test coverage quality and slow down feedback loops for developers; unit tests remain a foundational part of a robust testing strategy regardless of platform.
optionExplanations.C: Testing only after release to production (testing in production as the primary strategy) is risky and contrary to best practices of validating changes in staging/pre-production environments before they reach end users, especially for a real-time gaming platform with strict reliability requirements.
optionExplanations.D: While infrastructure-as-code validation has its place, directly testing GCP infrastructure itself is not the primary differentiator requested; the key change relevant to the scenario is the need for tests that scale to validate application behavior under the much larger, elastic capacity now available on GCP.

text: Mountkirk Games has deployed their new backend on Google Cloud Platform (GCP). You want to create a through testing process for new versions of the backend before they are released to the public. You want the testing environment to scale in an economical way. How should you design the process?

options.text: Create a scalable environment in GCP for simulating production load
Use the existing infrastructure to test the GCP-based backend at scale
Build stress tests into each component of your application using resources internal to GCP to simulate load
Create a set of static environments in GCP to test different levels of load גfor example, high, medium, and low

optionExplanations.A: Building a scalable test environment in GCP that can be spun up on-demand to simulate production-level load leverages cloud elasticity—resources are provisioned only when running tests and torn down afterward—providing thorough load testing while remaining economical, directly meeting both the thoroughness and cost-efficiency requirements.
optionExplanations.B: Reusing the existing (likely on-premises or legacy) infrastructure to test the new GCP backend at scale would not accurately represent the GCP production environment's performance characteristics and may not have the capacity to generate realistic load against a cloud-scale backend, undermining the validity of the tests.
optionExplanations.C: While stress-testing individual components is valuable, doing so in isolation without an environment that simulates overall production-scale load and traffic patterns doesn't provide the thorough, end-to-end validation needed before a release, and doesn't directly address the 'scale economically' requirement as clearly as an on-demand scalable environment.
optionExplanations.D: Maintaining a set of static, always-on environments for different load levels (high/medium/low) means paying for idle infrastructure most of the time, which is not economical compared to a dynamically scalable environment that is provisioned only as needed for testing.

text: Mountkirk Games wants to set up a continuous delivery pipeline. Their architecture includes many small services that they want to be able to update and roll back quickly. Mountkirk Games has the following requirements: ✑ Services are deployed redundantly across multiple regions in the US and Europe ✑ Only frontend services are exposed on the public internet ✑ They can provide a single frontend IP for their fleet of services ✑ Deployment artifacts are immutable Which set of products should they use?

options.text: Google Cloud Storage, Google Cloud Dataflow, Google Compute Engine
Google Cloud Storage, Google App Engine, Google Network Load Balancer
Google Kubernetes Registry, Google Container Engine, Google HTTP(S) Load Balancer
Google Cloud Functions, Google Cloud Pub/Sub, Google Cloud Deployment Manager

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Storage, Dataflow, and Compute Engine are oriented toward data processing/storage and raw VM-based compute; this combination doesn't directly provide container orchestration, immutable deployment artifacts via images, or a unified global frontend load balancer for microservices.
optionExplanations.B: App Engine could provide some of these capabilities, but the Network Load Balancer is a regional Layer 4 load balancer that does not provide a single global frontend IP across multi-region deployments, nor does it offer the Layer 7 routing needed to expose only frontend services while keeping backend services internal.
optionExplanations.C: Container Registry (or Artifact Registry) stores immutable container images as deployment artifacts, Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE, formerly Container Engine) runs the many small services across multiple regions with rolling updates/rollbacks, and the global HTTP(S) Load Balancer provides a single anycast frontend IP that can route to backend services in multiple regions while exposing only frontend services—satisfying all stated requirements (multi-region redundancy, frontend-only exposure, single IP, immutable artifacts).
