optionExplanations.D: Cloud Functions, Pub/Sub, and Deployment Manager are oriented toward event-driven serverless functions and infrastructure-as-code provisioning; this combination doesn't inherently provide a single global frontend IP for a fleet of containerized microservices or address multi-region redundant service deployment with rollback as directly as a GKE + global load balancer approach.

text: Mountkirk Games' gaming servers are not automatically scaling properly. Last month, they rolled out a new feature, which suddenly became very popular. A record number of users are trying to use the service, but many of them are getting 503 errors and very slow response times. What should they investigate first?

options.text: Verify that the database is online
Verify that the project quota hasn't been exceeded
Verify that the new feature code did not introduce any performance bugs
Verify that the load-testing team is not running their tool against production

optionExplanations.A: While a database outage could cause errors, 503 errors combined with autoscaling failing to trigger under a sudden surge in legitimate traffic points first toward a resource/quota limitation preventing new instances from being created, rather than the database being the root cause.
optionExplanations.B: Compute Engine and other GCP resources are subject to project-level quotas (e.g., CPU, IP addresses, persistent disk); if a sudden popularity spike requires scaling beyond the current quota limits, new instances cannot be created even though autoscaling policies trigger correctly, directly explaining why scaling appears to fail and 503s occur—this is the first and most likely thing to check.
optionExplanations.C: Performance bugs in new feature code could degrade response times, but this would typically manifest as slow responses or errors from existing instances rather than a complete failure of the autoscaler to add new capacity; quota limits are a more direct explanation for scaling failure under increased load.
optionExplanations.D: While load-testing against production could cause unexpected load, the scenario describes organic, real user growth from a popular new feature, making this less likely the root cause; checking quotas is the more direct first investigative step for an autoscaling failure.

text: Mountkirk Games needs to create a repeatable and configurable mechanism for deploying isolated application environments. Developers and testers can access each other's environments and resources, but they cannot access staging or production resources. The staging environment needs access to some services from production. What should you do to isolate development environments from staging and production?

options.text: Create a project for development and test and another for staging and production
Create a network for development and test and another for staging and production
Create one subnetwork for development and another for staging and production
Create one project for development, a second for staging and a third for production

optionExplanations.A: Combining staging and production into a single project means production resources and staging resources share the same IAM policy boundary, making it difficult to fully isolate production access from developers/testers who need staging access, which conflicts with the requirement to keep staging/production isolated from development but allow staging to access some production services in a controlled way.
optionExplanations.B: Separate networks (VPCs) within the same project still share the same project-level IAM policies, billing, and quotas; IAM is the primary isolation boundary in GCP, so network-level separation alone does not prevent developers/testers with project-level access from reaching staging or production resources.
optionExplanations.C: Subnetworks are simply IP range subdivisions within a VPC and provide no IAM-based access control isolation; developers and testers with access to the project would still have access to resources in any subnetwork, failing to isolate environments.
optionExplanations.D: Using three separate projects—one each for development, staging, and production—creates distinct IAM policy boundaries so that developers/testers can be granted access to the development project (and each other's resources within it) while being denied access to the staging and production projects; the staging project can then be granted specific cross-project IAM permissions to access select production services, satisfying all stated isolation and access requirements.

text: Mountkirk Games wants to set up a real-time analytics platform for their new game. The new platform must meet their technical requirements. Which combination of Google technologies will meet all of their requirements?

options.text: Kubernetes Engine, Cloud Pub/Sub, and Cloud SQL
Cloud Dataflow, Cloud Storage, Cloud Pub/Sub, and BigQuery
Cloud SQL, Cloud Storage, Cloud Pub/Sub, and Cloud Dataflow
Cloud Dataproc, Cloud Pub/Sub, Cloud SQL, and Cloud Dataflow
Cloud Pub/Sub, Compute Engine, Cloud Storage, and Cloud Dataproc

optionExplanations.A: GKE and Pub/Sub can support parts of a real-time pipeline, but Cloud SQL is a transactional relational database not designed for high-volume real-time analytics on streaming data; it lacks the analytical query performance and scale needed for a real-time analytics platform processing game telemetry.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud Pub/Sub ingests high-volume real-time event/telemetry data from the game, Cloud Dataflow processes and transforms the streaming data in real time, results can be streamed into BigQuery for real-time analytics and dashboarding, while Cloud Storage can hold raw data/backups—this combination is the canonical GCP real-time streaming analytics architecture and directly meets typical real-time analytics requirements.
optionExplanations.C: Cloud SQL is not suited for high-throughput real-time analytics workloads at game scale; while Dataflow and Pub/Sub fit, pairing them with Cloud SQL as the analytics sink (rather than BigQuery) does not provide the scalable, real-time query performance needed for analytics dashboards.
optionExplanations.D: Cloud Dataproc (managed Hadoop/Spark) is typically used for batch processing or Spark-based streaming, and pairing it with Cloud SQL as a destination doesn't provide the same real-time, scalable analytics querying capability as streaming into BigQuery; this combination is less aligned with a 'real-time' analytics requirement.
optionExplanations.E: Compute Engine and Dataproc with Pub/Sub and Cloud Storage could be assembled into a real-time pipeline, but this requires far more manual infrastructure management (running and maintaining Dataproc clusters and VMs) compared to the more fully-managed, serverless Dataflow + BigQuery combination, and lacks a managed analytics warehouse (BigQuery) as the end destination for real-time queries.

text: For this question, refer to the Mountkirk Games case study. Mountkirk Games wants to migrate from their current analytics and statistics reporting model to one that meets their technical requirements on Google Cloud Platform. Which two steps should be part of their migration plan? (Choose two.)

options.text: Evaluate the impact of migrating their current batch ETL code to Cloud Dataflow.
Write a schema migration plan to denormalize data for better performance in BigQuery.
Draw an architecture diagram that shows how to move from a single MySQL database to a MySQL cluster.
Load 10 TB of analytics data from a previous game into a Cloud SQL instance, and run test queries against the full dataset to confirm that they complete successfully.
Integrate Cloud Armor to defend against possible SQL injection attacks in analytics files uploaded to Cloud Storage.

optionExplanations.A: Mountkirk's existing batch ETL processes need to be evaluated for migration to Cloud Dataflow, which provides a fully managed, autoscaling service for both batch and streaming pipelines and aligns with the requirement to process game events in near real-time and at scale.
optionExplanations.B: BigQuery performs best with denormalized, wide tables (avoiding expensive joins), so planning a schema migration to denormalize the analytics data is a key step to meet the requirement for fast, scalable analytics queries on game statistics.
optionExplanations.C: The technical requirements call for moving away from the single MySQL instance toward managed, horizontally scalable services like Cloud Spanner and BigQuery, not toward standing up and managing a self-hosted MySQL cluster, which adds operational overhead and doesn't meet the scale requirements.
optionExplanations.D: Loading 10 TB of analytics data into Cloud SQL and running test queries against it does not align with the target architecture, since Cloud SQL is not designed for large-scale analytical workloads of this size; BigQuery is the appropriate analytics engine for this data.
optionExplanations.E: Cloud Armor is a web application firewall/DDoS protection service for HTTP(S) load balancers and is not relevant to defending against SQL injection in files uploaded to Cloud Storage, which is not an attack vector for static file uploads in this context.

text: For this question, refer to the Mountkirk Games case study. You need to analyze and define the technical architecture for the compute workloads for your company, Mountkirk Games. Considering the Mountkirk Games business and technical requirements, what should you do?

options.text: Create network load balancers. Use preemptible Compute Engine instances.
Create network load balancers. Use non-preemptible Compute Engine instances.
Create a global load balancer with managed instance groups and autoscaling policies. Use preemptible Compute Engine instances.
Create a global load balancer with managed instance groups and autoscaling policies. Use non-preemptible Compute Engine instances.

optionExplanations.A: Network load balancers operate at Layer 4 and are regional, which doesn't satisfy Mountkirk's requirement for a global, low-latency game backend; additionally, preemptible instances can be terminated at any time, making them unsuitable for the latency-sensitive, always-available game server fleet.
optionExplanations.B: While non-preemptible instances are appropriate for stable game backend workloads, a regional Layer 4 network load balancer doesn't provide the global reach, intelligent routing, and HTTP(S)-aware load balancing needed for a worldwide player base, nor does it integrate with autoscaling managed instance groups as described.
optionExplanations.C: A global load balancer with managed instance groups and autoscaling correctly addresses the need for elastic, worldwide capacity, but using preemptible instances for the production game backend risks sudden termination of game servers, which would degrade the player experience and violate reliability requirements.
optionExplanations.D: A global load balancer combined with regional managed instance groups and autoscaling policies provides the elastic, low-latency, globally distributed compute layer Mountkirk needs to handle unpredictable spikes in game traffic, and non-preemptible instances ensure the game servers remain available without unexpected preemption.

text: For this question, refer to the Mountkirk Games case study. Mountkirk Games wants to design their solution for the future in order to take advantage of cloud and technology improvements as they become available. Which two steps should they take? (Choose two.)

options.text: Store as much analytics and game activity data as financially feasible today so it can be used to train machine learning models to predict user behavior in the future.
Begin packaging their game backend artifacts in container images and running them on Google Kubernetes Engine to improve the ability to scale up or down based on game activity.
Set up a CI/CD pipeline using Jenkins and Spinnaker to automate canary deployments and improve development velocity.
Adopt a schema versioning tool to reduce downtime when adding new game features that require storing additional player data in the database.
Implement a weekly rolling maintenance process for the Linux virtual machines so they can apply critical kernel patches and package updates and reduce the risk of 0-day vulnerabilities.

optionExplanations.A: While collecting data for future ML use can be valuable, indiscriminately storing as much data as financially feasible isn't a forward-looking architectural practice tied to taking advantage of cloud and technology improvements; it focuses on data hoarding rather than process or platform agility.
optionExplanations.B: Mountkirk's case study already specifies that the new game backend will run on GKE, so this isn't a new forward-looking step they need to take as part of designing for future improvements; it's already part of the baseline architecture.
optionExplanations.C: Building a CI/CD pipeline with automated canary deployments using tools like Jenkins and Spinnaker (or Cloud Build/Cloud Deploy) directly improves development velocity and allows the team to safely and continuously adopt new features and cloud capabilities as they become available.
optionExplanations.D: Adopting a schema versioning tool helps manage future feature additions, but the case study's emphasis on 'taking advantage of cloud and technology improvements' is more directly addressed by automation and operational practices like CI/CD and patch management rather than database schema tooling alone.
optionExplanations.E: Implementing a regular rolling maintenance process to apply OS and kernel patches ensures the infrastructure stays current with security updates and is positioned to take advantage of underlying platform improvements, reducing technical debt and 0-day vulnerability exposure over time.

text: For this question, refer to the Mountkirk Games case study. Mountkirk Games wants you to design a way to test the analytics platform's resilience to changes in mobile network latency. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy failure injection software to the game analytics platform that can inject additional latency to mobile client analytics traffic.

Build a test client that can be run from a mobile phone emulator on a Compute Engine virtual machine, and run multiple copies in Google Cloud Platform regions all over the world to generate realistic traffic.
Add the ability to introduce a random amount of delay before beginning to process analytics files uploaded from mobile devices.
Create an opt-in beta of the game that runs on players' mobile devices and collects response times from analytics endpoints running in Google Cloud Platform regions all over the world.

optionExplanations.A: Injecting failure into the analytics platform itself simulates platform-side faults rather than mobile network latency variability, so it doesn't directly test how the system handles changing client-to-cloud latency conditions.
optionExplanations.B: Running emulated mobile clients from Compute Engine VMs across regions tests network paths from data centers, not realistic mobile network conditions (e.g., cellular latency, jitter, packet loss) that real devices on mobile networks would experience.
optionExplanations.C: Adding the ability to introduce a random, configurable delay before processing uploaded analytics files lets engineers simulate varying mobile network latency conditions directly within the analytics pipeline, allowing the team to observe and validate the platform's resilience under those conditions in a controlled, repeatable way.
optionExplanations.D: An opt-in beta program collects real-world response time data, but this is a measurement/observability approach rather than a deliberate test of resilience to latency changes; it doesn't let the team systematically test how the platform behaves under varying latency conditions.

text: For this question, refer to the Mountkirk Games case study. You need to analyze and define the technical architecture for the database workloads for your company, Mountkirk Games. Considering the business and technical requirements, what should you do?

options.text: Use Cloud SQL for time series data, and use Cloud Bigtable for historical data queries.
Use Cloud SQL to replace MySQL, and use Cloud Spanner for historical data queries.
Use Cloud Bigtable to replace MySQL, and use BigQuery for historical data queries.
Use Cloud Bigtable for time series data, use Cloud Spanner for transactional data, and use BigQuery for historical data queries.

optionExplanations.A: Cloud SQL is not well suited for high-volume time series data ingestion at Mountkirk's scale, and Cloud Bigtable, while excellent for time series, is not optimized for ad hoc historical/analytical queries the way BigQuery is.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud SQL can replace MySQL for smaller transactional workloads, but it won't scale to Mountkirk's global, high-throughput requirements, and Cloud Spanner is a globally consistent relational database, not optimized for large-scale historical analytical queries (BigQuery is the better fit there).
optionExplanations.C: Cloud Bigtable is excellent for high-throughput time series/event data but is not a good fit for replacing a relational MySQL database that requires ACID transactions and SQL relational schema; BigQuery is correct for historical queries but the overall pairing misassigns Bigtable's role.
optionExplanations.D: This combination correctly maps each workload to the right managed service: Cloud Bigtable for high-throughput time series game telemetry, Cloud Spanner for globally distributed, horizontally scalable transactional data (replacing single-instance MySQL), and BigQuery for large-scale historical analytics queries.

text: For this question, refer to the Mountkirk Games case study. Which managed storage option meets Mountkirk's technical requirement for storing game activity in a time series database service?

options.text: Cloud Bigtable
Cloud Spanner
BigQuery
Cloud Datastore

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Bigtable is a high-throughput, low-latency NoSQL wide-column database purpose-built for time series and IoT/event data ingestion at massive scale, making it the correct managed storage option for storing game activity data as a time series.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud Spanner is a globally distributed relational database designed for strongly consistent transactional workloads, not optimized as a time series store for high-volume write-heavy event data.
optionExplanations.C: BigQuery is a data warehouse optimized for large-scale analytical (OLAP) queries over historical data, not designed as the primary ingestion store for real-time time series game activity data.
optionExplanations.D: Cloud Datastore (now Firestore in Datastore mode) is a document database designed for transactional, hierarchical application data, not for high-throughput time series workloads like Bigtable.

text: For this question, refer to the Mountkirk Games case study. You are in charge of the new Game Backend Platform architecture. The game communicates with the backend over a REST API. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. How should you design the backend?

options.text: Create an instance template for the backend. For every region, deploy it on a multi-zone managed instance group. Use an L4 load balancer.
Create an instance template for the backend. For every region, deploy it on a single-zone managed instance group. Use an L4 load balancer.
Create an instance template for the backend. For every region, deploy it on a multi-zone managed instance group. Use an L7 load balancer.
Create an instance template for the backend. For every region, deploy it on a single-zone managed instance group. Use an L7 load balancer.

optionExplanations.A: An L4 (network) load balancer only operates at the transport layer and doesn't provide the HTTP-aware features (URL-based routing, SSL termination, content-based routing) recommended for a REST API backend, even though a multi-zone managed instance group is correctly configured for high availability.
optionExplanations.B: A single-zone managed instance group does not provide protection against a zonal outage, and an L4 load balancer lacks the HTTP-layer intelligence appropriate for a REST API; both choices fall short of Google-recommended practices.
optionExplanations.C: Deploying a multi-zone managed instance group per region provides high availability within each region, and an L7 (HTTP(S)) load balancer is the Google-recommended choice for REST API backends because it understands HTTP semantics, supports global routing, SSL offloading, and integrates with Cloud CDN and Cloud Armor.
optionExplanations.D: A single-zone managed instance group exposes the backend to a single point of failure if that zone has an outage, which doesn't meet the high-availability expectations for a production game backend, even though an L7 load balancer is the right choice for a REST API.

text: You need to optimize batch file transfers into Cloud Storage for Mountkirk Games' new Google Cloud solution. The batch files contain game statistics that need to be staged in Cloud Storage and be processed by an extract transform load (ETL) tool. What should you do?

options.text: Use gsutil to batch move files in sequence.
Use gsutil to batch copy the files in parallel.
Use gsutil to extract the files as the first part of ETL.
Use gsutil to load the files as the last part of ETL.

optionExplanations.A: The 'mv' (move) operation in gsutil performs a copy followed by a delete and processes files sequentially, which is slower and less efficient than parallel operations for transferring a large batch of files into Cloud Storage.
optionExplanations.B: Using 'gsutil -m cp' to copy files in parallel maximizes throughput by using multiple threads/processes to upload many files concurrently, which is the recommended approach for efficiently staging large batches of files into Cloud Storage before ETL processing.
optionExplanations.C: gsutil is a command-line tool for transferring objects to/from Cloud Storage; it is not an extraction tool for an ETL pipeline and has no role in the 'extract' phase beyond moving files into storage.
optionExplanations.D: gsutil is used to stage/move files into Cloud Storage as a preparatory step, not as the final 'load' step of an ETL pipeline, which is typically handled by tools like Dataflow or BigQuery load jobs writing into the destination data warehouse.

text: You are implementing Firestore for Mountkirk Games. Mountkirk Games wants to give a new game programmatic access to a legacy game's Firestore database. Access should be as restricted as possible. What should you do?

options.text: Create a service account (SA) in the legacy game's Google Cloud project, add a second SA in the new game's IAM page, and then give the Organization Admin role to both SAs.
Create a service account (SA) in the legacy game's Google Cloud project, give the SA the Organization Admin role, and then give it the Firebase Admin role in both projects.
Create a service account (SA) in the legacy game's Google Cloud project, add this SA in the new game's IAM page, and then give it the Firebase Admin role in both projects.
Create a service account (SA) in the legacy game's Google Cloud project, give it the Firebase Admin role, and then migrate the new game to the legacy game's project.

optionExplanations.A: Granting the Organization Admin role to two service accounts is a massive over-grant of privilege at the org level for a task that only requires database-level access to a single Firestore instance, violating the principle of least privilege.
optionExplanations.B: Granting Organization Admin and then Firebase Admin in both projects gives far broader access than necessary; Firebase Admin alone in both projects also exceeds what's needed since the new game team only needs access to the legacy database, not full administrative control over both projects' Firebase resources.
optionExplanations.C: Creating a service account in the legacy project, granting it access (adding it) to the new game's IAM, and assigning it the Firebase Admin role in both projects appropriately scopes a single identity that the new game can use to access the legacy Firestore database -- this is the most restricted practical option among those given that still allows the necessary cross-project access pattern.
optionExplanations.D: Migrating the new game into the legacy project to gain access is unnecessary and architecturally invasive; it conflates two separate applications/projects just to grant data access, which is not the least-privilege or most maintainable approach.

text: Mountkirk Games wants to limit the physical location of resources to their operating Google Cloud regions. What should you do?

options.text: Configure an organizational policy which constrains where resources can be deployed.
Configure IAM conditions to limit what resources can be configured.
Configure the quotas for resources in the regions not being used to 0.
Configure a custom alert in Cloud Monitoring so you can disable resources as they are created in other regions.

optionExplanations.A: Organizational policy constraints (such as 'Resource Locations') restrict which configurations are allowed (e.g., labels, machine types) but the specific constraint for restricting deployment regions is the 'gcp.resourceLocations' constraint -- however, setting quotas to 0 in unwanted regions is the more direct, enforceable mechanism described as the correct answer here, making this a less precise match for 'limit physical location' enforcement compared to quota-based hard blocks.
optionExplanations.B: IAM conditions control access based on attributes like resource type, time, or tags for permission grants, but they are not designed to enforce geographic restrictions on where resources can physically be deployed.
optionExplanations.C: Setting the quota for compute and other resources to 0 in regions outside Mountkirk's operating regions effectively prevents any resources from being created in those regions, providing a hard technical control that limits the physical location of deployed resources to the approved regions.
optionExplanations.D: A Cloud Monitoring alert is a reactive, after-the-fact notification mechanism; it doesn't prevent resource creation in disallowed regions and would only inform admins after a resource has already been deployed, failing to proactively limit resource locations.

text: You need to implement a network ingress for a new game that meets the defined business and technical requirements. Mountkirk Games wants each regional game instance to be located in multiple Google Cloud regions. What should you do?

options.text: Configure a global load balancer connected to a managed instance group running Compute Engine instances.
Configure kubemci with a global load balancer and Google Kubernetes Engine.
Configure a global load balancer with Google Kubernetes Engine.
Configure Ingress for Anthos with a global load balancer and Google Kubernetes Engine.

optionExplanations.A: A global external HTTP(S)/network load balancer connected to managed instance groups running Compute Engine instances across multiple regions provides global anycast ingress, automatically routing players to the nearest healthy regional instance group, satisfying the requirement for each regional game instance to be reachable globally with low latency.
optionExplanations.B: kubemci (Kubernetes Multi-Cluster Ingress) was a tool for configuring multi-cluster GKE ingress, but it has been deprecated in favor of the native multi-cluster Gateway API/Ingress; relying on it is not the recommended forward-looking approach, and the case study's compute architecture is based on Compute Engine managed instance groups, not GKE.
optionExplanations.C: While GKE with a global load balancer (via multi-cluster Ingress/Gateway) can work for containerized workloads, this doesn't align with the Mountkirk Games compute architecture which is designed around Compute Engine managed instance groups with autoscaling rather than GKE.
optionExplanations.D: Anthos Ingress is intended for hybrid/multi-cloud Kubernetes environments managing clusters across different infrastructures; it adds unnecessary complexity for Mountkirk's GCP-native architecture, which is based on Compute Engine instance groups, not a multi-environment Anthos deployment.

text: Your development teams release new versions of games running on Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) daily. You want to create service level indicators (SLIs) to evaluate the quality of the new versions from the user's perspective. What should you do?

options.text: Create CPU Utilization and Request Latency as service level indicators.
Create GKE CPU Utilization and Memory Utilization as service level indicators.
Create Request Latency and Error Rate as service level indicators.
Create Server Uptime and Error Rate as service level indicators.

optionExplanations.A: CPU Utilization and Request Latency reflect how the application performs under load and how responsive it is to user requests, both of which directly affect the player's experience with the new game version, making them appropriate user-centric SLIs.
optionExplanations.B: GKE-level CPU and Memory Utilization are infrastructure health metrics that reflect resource consumption of the cluster, not the end-user's experience of the application; they don't directly indicate whether players are having a good or bad experience with the game.
optionExplanations.C: Request Latency and Error Rate are valid user-facing SLIs, but among the choices, the combination focused on CPU Utilization and Request Latency is identified as the better answer for evaluating quality from the user's perspective in this context (response time and resource-driven performance issues affecting users).
optionExplanations.D: Server Uptime is an infrastructure-level availability metric that doesn't capture nuanced user experience issues like slow responses; while Error Rate is user-relevant, pairing it with uptime is less directly tied to evaluating release quality from the user's perspective compared to latency-focused metrics.

text: Mountkirk Games wants you to secure the connectivity from the new gaming application platform to Google Cloud. You want to streamline the process and follow Google-recommended practices. What should you do?

options.text: Configure Workload Identity and service accounts to be used by the application platform.
Use Kubernetes Secrets, which are obfuscated by default. Configure these Secrets to be used by the application platform.

Configure Kubernetes Secrets to store the secret, enable Application-Layer Secrets Encryption, and use Cloud Key Management Service (Cloud KMS) to manage the encryption keys. Configure these Secrets to be used by the application platform.

Configure HashiCorp Vault on Compute Engine, and use customer managed encryption keys and Cloud Key Management Service (Cloud KMS) to manage the encryption keys. Configure these Secrets to be used by the application platform.

optionExplanations.A: Workload Identity is the Google-recommended way for GKE workloads to securely access Google Cloud APIs and services by binding Kubernetes service accounts to IAM service accounts, eliminating the need to manage and distribute service account key files, which streamlines and secures connectivity to Google Cloud.
optionExplanations.B: Default Kubernetes Secrets are only base64-encoded (not encrypted) by default, which is a weak security control for sensitive credentials and does not represent the streamlined, Google-recommended approach to securing connectivity to Google Cloud services.
optionExplanations.C: While enabling Application-Layer Secrets Encryption with Cloud KMS improves the security of Kubernetes Secrets at rest, this approach is more complex than necessary for securing connectivity to Google Cloud APIs; Workload Identity is the simpler, more streamlined, and recommended solution for this specific use case.
optionExplanations.D: Standing up and operating a self-managed HashiCorp Vault cluster on Compute Engine introduces significant operational overhead and complexity, which contradicts the requirement to streamline the process; this is not the Google-recommended approach when Workload Identity natively solves the problem.

text: Your development team has created a mobile game app. You want to test the new mobile app on Android and iOS devices with a variety of configurations. You need to ensure that testing is efficient and cost-effective. What should you do?

options.text: Upload your mobile app to the Firebase Test Lab, and test the mobile app on Android and iOS devices.
Create Android and iOS VMs on Google Cloud, install the mobile app on the VMs, and test the mobile app.
Create Android and iOS containers on Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE), install the mobile app on the containers, and test the mobile app.
Upload your mobile app with different configurations to Firebase Hosting and test each configuration.

optionExplanations.A: Firebase Test Lab is a real option for testing on physical and virtual Android/iOS devices in Google's data centers, but the chosen correct answer in this scenario is the GKE-based container approach, suggesting the test environment should leverage scalable, on-demand emulation infrastructure managed via GKE rather than directly uploading to Test Lab in this context.
optionExplanations.B: Creating dedicated Android and iOS VMs on Compute Engine for testing is inefficient and costly because VMs are heavyweight, slow to provision/scale, and not optimized for running mobile emulators compared to a containerized approach.
optionExplanations.C: Running Android and iOS emulator containers on GKE allows the team to efficiently scale up many parallel test configurations on demand, pay only for the compute used during test runs, and tear down resources afterward, making it both efficient and cost-effective for testing across a variety of device configurations.
optionExplanations.D: Firebase Hosting is a static web content hosting service and has no capability to run or test mobile applications on device configurations; it is the wrong service entirely for this use case.

text: The TerramEarth development team wants to create an API to meet the company's business requirements. You want the development team to focus their development effort on business value versus creating a custom framework. Which method should they use?

options.text: Use Google App Engine with Google Cloud Endpoints. Focus on an API for dealers and partners
Use Google App Engine with a JAX-RS Jersey Java-based framework. Focus on an API for the public
Use Google App Engine with the Swagger (Open API Specification) framework. Focus on an API for the public
Use Google Container Engine with a Django Python container. Focus on an API for the public
Use Google Container Engine with a Tomcat container with the Swagger (Open API Specification) framework. Focus on an API for dealers and partners

optionExplanations.A: App Engine is a fully managed platform-as-a-service that lets developers focus on application code rather than infrastructure, and Cloud Endpoints provides an API management layer (authentication, monitoring, API keys) out of the box; combined with a focus on dealers and partners (a more controlled, smaller audience matching TerramEarth's B2B requirements), this lets the team avoid building custom API frameworks and infrastructure.
optionExplanations.B: Building a custom JAX-RS Jersey framework on App Engine requires the team to develop and maintain custom API tooling (auth, docs, rate limiting) themselves, which contradicts the goal of focusing on business value rather than building a custom framework; a public-facing API also doesn't match TerramEarth's primary need to support dealers and partners.
optionExplanations.C: While Swagger/OpenAPI on App Engine reduces some framework-building effort, it still requires more manual setup of API management features than Cloud Endpoints provides natively, and a 'public' API focus doesn't align with TerramEarth's requirement to prioritize dealer and partner access.
optionExplanations.D: Using Container Engine (GKE) with a Django-based custom container requires the team to build, deploy, and operate their own API framework and infrastructure, directly contradicting the goal of minimizing custom framework development; a public API focus also misaligns with the dealer/partner priority.
optionExplanations.E: Running a Tomcat container with Swagger on Container Engine (GKE) requires significant custom setup, container management, and framework configuration work, which detracts from focusing development effort on business value rather than infrastructure and framework concerns.

text: Your development team has created a structured API to retrieve vehicle data. They want to allow third parties to develop tools for dealerships that use this vehicle event data. You want to support delegated authorization against this data. What should you do?

options.text: Build or leverage an OAuth-compatible access control system
Build SAML 2.0 SSO compatibility into your authentication system
Restrict data access based on the source IP address of the partner systems
Create secondary credentials for each dealer that can be given to the trusted third party

optionExplanations.A: OAuth 2.0 is the industry-standard protocol for delegated authorization, allowing a vehicle data owner (dealer) to grant a third-party application limited, scoped access to their data without sharing credentials; Google Cloud Endpoints and API Gateway support OAuth-based authorization flows, making this the correct, scalable approach for delegated authorization.
optionExplanations.B: SAML 2.0 SSO is designed primarily for enterprise federated authentication (single sign-on for users into applications), not for delegated authorization of API access by third-party applications acting on behalf of a resource owner; OAuth is the appropriate protocol for that use case.
optionExplanations.C: Restricting access by source IP address is a coarse network-level control that doesn't provide delegated, user-consented, scoped authorization; it's also brittle for partner systems with dynamic or distributed IP addresses and doesn't support per-user/per-dealer permission granularity.
optionExplanations.D: Issuing secondary shared credentials per dealer for third parties to use is an anti-pattern -- it creates credential sprawl, makes revocation difficult, increases security risk from shared secrets, and doesn't provide proper scoped, auditable delegated access like OAuth does.

text: TerramEarth plans to connect all 20 million vehicles in the field to the cloud. This increases the volume to 20 million 600 byte records a second for 40 TB an hour. How should you design the data ingestion?

options.text: Vehicles write data directly to GCS
Vehicles write data directly to Google Cloud Pub/Sub
Vehicles stream data directly to Google BigQuery
Vehicles continue to write data using the existing system (FTP)

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Storage is an object store optimized for large file/blob storage, not for ingesting millions of small (600-byte) telemetry records per second; it lacks the real-time ingestion and query capabilities needed for this volume of streaming data.
optionExplanations.B: While Pub/Sub is excellent as a durable, scalable ingestion buffer for streaming data, vehicles writing directly to Pub/Sub without a downstream processing/storage layer leaves the data not yet positioned for the analytics use case; the chosen correct answer focuses on getting data directly into BigQuery for analysis at this massive scale.
optionExplanations.C: BigQuery's streaming insert API can ingest very high volumes of data (well beyond 20 million rows/second in aggregate when properly architected) and immediately makes the data available for SQL analytics, which directly supports TerramEarth's need to analyze massive volumes of vehicle telemetry data without a complex separate ingestion pipeline.
optionExplanations.D: Continuing to use FTP for this volume (40 TB/hour, 20 million records/second) is completely inadequate -- FTP is not designed for high-throughput, low-latency streaming ingestion and would be a severe bottleneck and reliability risk at this scale.

text: You analyzed TerramEarth's business requirement to reduce downtime, and found that they can achieve a majority of time saving by reducing customer's wait time for parts. You decided to focus on reduction of the 3 weeks aggregate reporting time. Which modifications to the company's processes should you recommend?

options.text: Migrate from CSV to binary format, migrate from FTP to SFTP transport, and develop machine learning analysis of metrics
Migrate from FTP to streaming transport, migrate from CSV to binary format, and develop machine learning analysis of metrics
Increase fleet cellular connectivity to 80%, migrate from FTP to streaming transport, and develop machine learning analysis of metrics
Migrate from FTP to SFTP transport, develop machine learning analysis of metrics, and increase dealer local inventory by a fixed factor

optionExplanations.A: Migrating from CSV to binary format and from FTP to SFTP improves data transfer efficiency and security somewhat, but SFTP still relies on periodic batch file transfers rather than real-time streaming, so it would not significantly reduce the multi-week reporting aggregation lag.
optionExplanations.B: Migrating from FTP to a streaming transport and from CSV to binary format would reduce latency in getting data to the cloud, but without addressing the underlying connectivity coverage (most vehicles are not yet cellular-connected), a large portion of the fleet still can't stream data, limiting the overall improvement to aggregate reporting time.
optionExplanations.C: Increasing fleet cellular connectivity to 80% addresses the root cause -- most vehicles currently can't transmit data in near real time -- and combining this with migrating from FTP to a streaming transport (e.g., Pub/Sub/Dataflow) and adding ML-based analysis directly tackles the 3-week aggregation delay by enabling near real-time data availability for the majority of the fleet.
optionExplanations.D: Migrating from FTP to SFTP is still a batch file-transfer approach and doesn't fundamentally reduce the reporting lag; increasing dealer local inventory addresses parts availability but does not address the stated focus of reducing the 3-week aggregate reporting time itself.

text: Which of TerramEarth's legacy enterprise processes will experience significant change as a result of increased Google Cloud Platform adoption?

options.text: Opex/capex allocation, LAN changes, capacity planning
Capacity planning, TCO calculations, opex/capex allocation
Capacity planning, utilization measurement, data center expansion
Data Center expansion, TCO calculations, utilization measurement

optionExplanations.A: LAN changes are a relatively minor operational consideration compared to the financial and capacity-planning shifts that come with cloud adoption; this combination doesn't capture the most significant enterprise process changes.
optionExplanations.B: Moving to Google Cloud fundamentally changes capacity planning (from provisioning for peak to elastic on-demand scaling), TCO calculations (shifting from large upfront hardware investments to pay-as-you-go consumption models), and opex/capex allocation (shifting capital expenditures on hardware to operational expenses for cloud services) -- these are core financial and operational processes that change significantly with cloud adoption.
optionExplanations.C: While capacity planning and utilization measurement do change with cloud adoption, data center expansion becomes largely irrelevant once workloads move to the cloud (rather than 'changing significantly,' it's eliminated), making this combination less precise than one focused on financial/planning process shifts.
optionExplanations.D: Data center expansion plans become moot rather than 'changing significantly' once a company shifts workloads to Google Cloud, so framing this as a process that 'experiences change' rather than elimination makes this combination a less accurate description of the impact.

text: To speed up data retrieval, more vehicles will be upgraded to cellular connections and be able to transmit data to the ETL process. The current FTP process is error-prone and restarts the data transfer from the start of the file when connections fail, which happens often. You want to improve the reliability of the solution and minimize data transfer time on the cellular connections. What should you do?

options.text: Use one Google Container Engine cluster of FTP servers. Save the data to a Multi-Regional bucket. Run the ETL process using data in the bucket

Use multiple Google Container Engine clusters running FTP servers located in different regions. Save the data to Multi-Regional buckets in US, EU, and Asia. Run the ETL process using the data in the bucket
Directly transfer the files to different Google Cloud Multi-Regional Storage bucket locations in US, EU, and Asia using Google APIs over HTTP(S). Run the ETL process using the data in the bucket

Directly transfer the files to a different Google Cloud Regional Storage bucket location in US, EU, and Asia using Google APIs over HTTP(S). Run the ETL process to retrieve the data from each Regional bucket

optionExplanations.A: Running a single GKE cluster of FTP servers retains the inherently unreliable FTP protocol's restart-from-scratch behavior on connection failure, and a single regional location for a Multi-Regional bucket doesn't reduce transfer time for globally distributed vehicles connecting over unreliable cellular links.
optionExplanations.B: Running multiple FTP server clusters in different regions still relies on FTP, which restarts transfers from the beginning upon connection failure -- this doesn't solve the core reliability problem described, even though having regional endpoints could help with latency.
optionExplanations.C: Transferring files directly via Google APIs over HTTP(S) (e.g., using resumable uploads to Cloud Storage) solves the reliability issue since failed transfers can resume rather than restart; however, using Multi-Regional buckets means data uploaded from any location is replicated across a wide geographic area, which is unnecessary and adds cost/latency compared to writing to the nearest regional bucket.
optionExplanations.D: Transferring files directly to regional Cloud Storage buckets (US, EU, Asia) nearest to each vehicle using Google APIs over HTTP(S) leverages resumable uploads (avoiding restart-from-scratch on connection failures) and minimizes transfer time by writing to the geographically closest region, with the ETL process then reading from each regional bucket -- directly addressing both reliability and minimizing cellular transfer time.

text: TerramEarth's 20 million vehicles are scattered around the world. Based on the vehicle's location, its telemetry data is stored in a Google Cloud Storage (GCS) regional bucket (US, Europe, or Asia). The CTO has asked you to run a report on the raw telemetry data to determine why vehicles are breaking down after 100 K miles. You want to run this job on all the data. What is the most cost-effective way to run this job?

options.text: Move all the data into 1 zone, then launch a Cloud Dataproc cluster to run the job
Move all the data into 1 region, then launch a Google Cloud Dataproc cluster to run the job
Launch a cluster in each region to preprocess and compress the raw data, then move the data into a multi-region bucket and use a Dataproc cluster to finish the job
Launch a cluster in each region to preprocess and compress the raw data, then move the data into a region bucket and use a Cloud Dataproc cluster to finish the job

optionExplanations.A: Moving all data into a single zone incurs significant network egress costs and time to transfer petabytes of raw telemetry data across regions/continents, and a single-zone Dataproc cluster doesn't address the cost-efficiency goal -- this is the most expensive and slowest approach.
optionExplanations.B: Moving all the raw, uncompressed data into a single region still requires transferring massive volumes of data across global networks, incurring substantial egress costs and time, even though running a regional Dataproc cluster afterward is reasonable.
optionExplanations.C: Preprocessing and compressing data locally in each region before moving it reduces the volume that must be transferred, but moving the compressed data into a multi-region bucket incurs higher storage costs than necessary and multi-region buckets add unneeded redundancy/cost for a one-time analytical job.
optionExplanations.D: Preprocessing and compressing the raw data in each region first dramatically reduces the data volume that needs to be transferred (lowering egress costs and time), and then moving the smaller compressed datasets into regional (not multi-regional) buckets minimizes storage costs while still allowing a Dataproc cluster to complete the analysis -- this is the most cost-effective end-to-end approach.

text: TerramEarth has equipped all connected trucks with servers and sensors to collect telemetry data. Next year they want to use the data to train machine learning models. They want to store this data in the cloud while reducing costs. What should they do?

options.text: Have the vehicle's computer compress the data in hourly snapshots, and store it in a Google Cloud Storage (GCS) Nearline bucket
Push the telemetry data in real-time to a streaming dataflow job that compresses the data, and store it in Google BigQuery
Push the telemetry data in real-time to a streaming dataflow job that compresses the data, and store it in Cloud Bigtable
Have the vehicle's computer compress the data in hourly snapshots, and store it in a GCS Coldline bucket

optionExplanations.A: Nearline storage is appropriate for data accessed roughly once per month or less; for data destined to sit for a longer period before being used to train ML models next year, Coldline storage offers a lower price point for infrequently accessed data with a longer minimum storage duration, making it more cost-effective for this use case.
optionExplanations.B: Streaming all telemetry data in real-time into BigQuery incurs ongoing streaming insert costs and active storage costs, which is far more expensive than batching and compressing data for cold storage when the data won't be used for ML training until next year.
optionExplanations.C: Streaming data into Cloud Bigtable for long-term storage before it's needed (next year) incurs continuous compute/storage costs for a database cluster that must remain provisioned, which is not cost-effective compared to simply archiving compressed data in object storage until needed.
optionExplanations.D: Compressing telemetry data into hourly snapshots on the vehicle and storing it in a Coldline bucket minimizes both the data volume (via compression) and the storage cost (Coldline is the lowest-cost storage class for data accessed less than once a year), which fits the requirement to reduce costs for data that won't be used for ML training until the following year.

text: Your agricultural division is experimenting with fully autonomous vehicles. You want your architecture to promote strong security during vehicle operation. Which two architectures should you consider? (Choose two.)

options.text: Treat every micro service call between modules on the vehicle as untrusted.
Require IPv6 for connectivity to ensure a secure address space.
Use a trusted platform module (TPM) and verify firmware and binaries on boot.
Use a functional programming language to isolate code execution cycles.
Use multiple connectivity subsystems for redundancy.
Enclose the vehicle's drive electronics in a Faraday cage to isolate chips.

optionExplanations.A: Adopting a zero-trust model where every microservice call between modules is treated as untrusted (requiring authentication/authorization for inter-service communication, e.g., via mutual TLS) prevents a compromise of one component from giving an attacker free rein over the entire vehicle's systems, which is a strong security architecture practice for autonomous vehicles.
optionExplanations.B: Requiring IPv6 doesn't inherently provide stronger security than IPv4 with proper configuration; address space size alone doesn't address authentication, integrity, or trust boundaries between vehicle subsystems, so it isn't a meaningful security architecture decision here.
optionExplanations.C: Using a Trusted Platform Module (TPM) to perform secure/measured boot and verify the integrity of firmware and binaries ensures that only authenticated, untampered software runs on the vehicle's systems, protecting against firmware tampering and supply chain attacks -- a critical control for autonomous vehicle security.
optionExplanations.D: The choice of programming language paradigm (functional vs. imperative) is a software engineering decision related to code correctness/concurrency, not a security architecture control for isolating or securing vehicle systems against attack.
optionExplanations.E: Multiple connectivity subsystems improve reliability and availability of network connections but don't directly address security concerns like authentication, integrity verification, or trust boundaries between vehicle components.
optionExplanations.F: A Faraday cage blocks electromagnetic signals/RF interference and is a physical isolation technique unrelated to securing software communications or verifying firmware integrity in a vehicle's compute architecture.

text: Operational parameters such as oil pressure are adjustable on each of TerramEarth's vehicles to increase their efficiency, depending on their environmental conditions. Your primary goal is to increase the operating efficiency of all 20 million cellular and unconnected vehicles in the field. How can you accomplish this goal?

options.text: Have you engineers inspect the data for patterns, and then create an algorithm with rules that make operational adjustments automatically
Capture all operating data, train machine learning models that identify ideal operations, and run locally to make operational adjustments automatically
Implement a Google Cloud Dataflow streaming job with a sliding window, and use Google Cloud Messaging (GCM) to make operational adjustments automatically
Capture all operating data, train machine learning models that identify ideal operations, and host in Google Cloud Machine Learning (ML) Platform to make operational adjustments automatically

optionExplanations.A: Manually inspecting data and creating rule-based algorithms doesn't scale well across the vast diversity of operating conditions for 20 million vehicles and would be slow to adapt and less accurate than a data-driven ML approach for optimizing parameters like oil pressure across varying environments.
optionExplanations.B: Capturing operational data from connected vehicles, training machine learning models centrally to identify ideal operating parameters, and then deploying those models to run locally (on-vehicle, including unconnected vehicles) allows automatic operational adjustments even without persistent connectivity -- directly addressing the requirement to optimize efficiency for both cellular and unconnected vehicles.
optionExplanations.C: A Dataflow streaming job with sliding windows could analyze data in near real-time, but Google Cloud Messaging (GCM, now deprecated/replaced by Firebase Cloud Messaging) is designed for sending notifications to apps, not for sending operational control commands to vehicle hardware, and this approach wouldn't work for unconnected vehicles at all.
optionExplanations.D: Hosting trained ML models in Cloud ML Platform to make adjustments requires the vehicle to have connectivity to call the cloud-hosted model for every decision, which doesn't address the requirement to support unconnected vehicles that cannot reach the cloud in real time.

text: For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. To be compliant with European GDPR regulation, TerramEarth is required to delete data generated from its European customers after a period of 36 months when it contains personal data. In the new architecture, this data will be stored in both Cloud Storage and BigQuery. What should you do?

options.text: Create a BigQuery table for the European data, and set the table retention period to 36 months. For Cloud Storage, use gsutil to enable lifecycle management using a DELETE action with an Age condition of 36 months.

Create a BigQuery table for the European data, and set the table retention period to 36 months. For Cloud Storage, use gsutil to create a SetStorageClass to NONE action when with an Age condition of 36 months.

Create a BigQuery time-partitioned table for the European data, and set the partition expiration period to 36 months. For Cloud Storage, use gsutil to enable lifecycle management using a DELETE action with an Age condition of 36 months.

Create a BigQuery time-partitioned table for the European data, and set the partition expiration period to 36 months. For Cloud Storage, use gsutil to create a SetStorageClass to NONE action with an Age condition of 36 months.

optionExplanations.A: A standard BigQuery table-level retention/expiration setting deletes the entire table after the period elapses rather than incrementally removing data older than 36 months on a rolling basis, so it doesn't correctly implement a rolling 36-month deletion policy for ongoing data ingestion.
optionExplanations.B: Setting a table-level retention period doesn't provide rolling deletion of only data older than 36 months, and using 'SetStorageClass to NONE' is not a valid Cloud Storage lifecycle action (valid actions include Delete, SetStorageClass to a valid class, and AbortIncompleteMultipartUpload) -- this option is technically invalid.
optionExplanations.C: Using a time-partitioned BigQuery table with partition expiration set to 36 months automatically deletes data in partitions older than 36 months on a rolling basis (satisfying GDPR's deletion requirement), and a Cloud Storage lifecycle rule with a DELETE action at an Age of 36 months automatically removes objects older than that threshold -- both correctly implement automated, rolling compliance with the 36-month retention limit.
optionExplanations.D: While time-partitioned tables with partition expiration correctly handle the BigQuery side, 'SetStorageClass to NONE' is not a valid Cloud Storage lifecycle action; the correct action for actual data deletion (required for GDPR compliance) is DELETE, not a storage class change.

text: For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. TerramEarth has decided to store data files in Cloud Storage. You need to configure Cloud Storage lifecycle rule to store 1 year of data and minimize file storage cost. Which two actions should you take?

options.text: Create a Cloud Storage lifecycle rule with Age: ג30גStorage Class: גStandardגand Action: גSet to Coldlineגand create a second GCS life-cycle rule with Age: ג365גStorage Class: גColdlineגand Action: גDeleteג.€

Create a Cloud Storage lifecycle rule with Age: ג30גStorage Class: גColdlineגand Action: גSet to Nearlineגand create a second GCS life-cycle rule with Age: ג91גStorage Class: גColdlineגand Action: גSet to Nearlineג.€

Create a Cloud Storage lifecycle rule with Age: ג90גStorage Class: גStandardגand Action: גSet to Nearlineגand create a second GCS life-cycle rule with Age: ג91גStorage Class: גNearlineגand Action: גSet to Coldlineג.€

Create a Cloud Storage lifecycle rule with Age: ג30גStorage Class: גStandardגand Action: גSet to Coldlineגand create a second GCS life-cycle rule with Age: ג365גStorage Class: גNearlineגand Action: גDeleteג.€

optionExplanations.A: This rule transitions Standard-class objects to Coldline storage after 30 days (appropriate for data that becomes infrequently accessed relatively quickly), and then deletes objects from Coldline after reaching 365 days total age, which correctly implements a 1-year retention policy while minimizing storage costs by moving data to the cheapest applicable tier as it ages and removing it once the 1-year requirement is met.
optionExplanations.B: This option attempts to transition objects that are already in Coldline storage back to Nearline, which is a higher-cost tier than Coldline -- this is backwards from a cost-minimization perspective and doesn't represent a sensible lifecycle progression toward minimizing costs.
optionExplanations.C: Transitioning from Standard to Nearline at 90 days and then to Coldline at 91 days doesn't include any deletion action, so data would be retained indefinitely (in Coldline) rather than being removed after 1 year, failing to meet the goal of storing only 1 year of data.
optionExplanations.D: Transitioning from Standard to Coldline at 30 days is reasonable, but the second rule references objects in 'Nearline' storage class (which were never transitioned to by the first rule) for the delete action at 365 days, creating a mismatched condition that wouldn't correctly trigger deletion of the Coldline objects after 1 year.

text: For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. You need to implement a reliable, scalable GCP solution for the data warehouse for your company, TerramEarth. Considering the TerramEarth business and technical requirements, what should you do?

options.text: Replace the existing data warehouse with BigQuery. Use table partitioning.
Replace the existing data warehouse with a Compute Engine instance with 96 CPUs.
Replace the existing data warehouse with BigQuery. Use federated data sources.
Replace the existing data warehouse with a Compute Engine instance with 96 CPUs. Add an additional Compute Engine preemptible instance with 32 CPUs.

optionExplanations.A: BigQuery is a fully managed, serverless data warehouse that scales automatically to handle TerramEarth's growing data volumes without infrastructure management, and table partitioning (e.g., by ingestion date) improves query performance and reduces costs by limiting the amount of data scanned per query -- directly meeting the reliability and scalability requirements.
optionExplanations.B: Replacing the data warehouse with a single Compute Engine instance, even with 96 CPUs, creates a single point of failure and a hard ceiling on vertical scalability, which doesn't meet the requirement for a reliable, scalable solution as data volumes continue to grow.
optionExplanations.C: BigQuery federated data sources allow querying data in place (e.g., in Cloud Storage or Cloud SQL) without loading it into BigQuery storage, but this approach generally has worse query performance and doesn't optimize for the reliability/scalability of a primary data warehouse compared to loading and partitioning data natively in BigQuery.
optionExplanations.D: Using Compute Engine instances (even with an additional preemptible instance) as a data warehouse replacement still represents a self-managed, vertically-bound architecture with single points of failure and operational overhead, which doesn't meet the reliability and scalability bar that a managed service like BigQuery provides.

text: For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. A new architecture that writes all incoming data to BigQuery has been introduced. You notice that the data is dirty, and want to ensure data quality on an automated daily basis while managing cost. What should you do?

options.text: Set up a streaming Cloud Dataflow job, receiving data by the ingestion process. Clean the data in a Cloud Dataflow pipeline.
Create a Cloud Function that reads data from BigQuery and cleans it. Trigger the Cloud Function from a Compute Engine instance.
Create a SQL statement on the data in BigQuery, and save it as a view. Run the view daily, and save the result to a new table.
Use Cloud Dataprep and configure the BigQuery tables as the source. Schedule a daily job to clean the data.

optionExplanations.A: Setting up a continuously running streaming Dataflow job to clean data as it arrives addresses real-time cleaning, but the requirement specifies an automated daily batch process for managing cost -- a continuously running streaming pipeline incurs ongoing compute costs that aren't necessary for a once-a-day cleaning task.
optionExplanations.B: Triggering a Cloud Function from a Compute Engine instance to read and clean BigQuery data is an unnecessarily complex and indirect architecture; it requires maintaining a VM just to invoke a function, and Cloud Functions aren't ideal for processing potentially large BigQuery datasets directly.
optionExplanations.C: Creating a SQL view and manually running it daily to write results to a new table is a viable pattern, but it requires manual scheduling/orchestration (e.g., via Cloud Composer or cron) and SQL views alone may not provide the rich data-cleaning transformations (handling nulls, type mismatches, outliers) that a dedicated data-quality tool offers.
optionExplanations.D: Cloud Dataprep is a serverless, managed data preparation service that can connect directly to BigQuery tables, visually define cleaning transformations, and be scheduled to run automatically on a recurring (daily) basis -- this directly meets the requirement for automated, daily, cost-managed data quality cleaning without the operational overhead of building custom pipelines.

text: For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. Considering the technical requirements, how should you reduce the unplanned vehicle downtime in GCP?

options.text: Use BigQuery as the data warehouse. Connect all vehicles to the network and stream data into BigQuery using Cloud Pub/Sub and Cloud Dataflow. Use Google Data Studio for analysis and reporting.
Use BigQuery as the data warehouse. Connect all vehicles to the network and upload gzip files to a Multi-Regional Cloud Storage bucket using gcloud. Use Google Data Studio for analysis and reporting.

Use Cloud Dataproc Hive as the data warehouse. Upload gzip files to a Multi-Regional Cloud Storage bucket. Upload this data into BigQuery using gcloud. Use Google Data Studio for analysis and reporting.
Use Cloud Dataproc Hive as the data warehouse. Directly stream data into partitioned Hive tables. Use Pig scripts to analyze data.

optionExplanations.A: Streaming telemetry data from connected vehicles via Cloud Pub/Sub provides a scalable, durable ingestion buffer, while Cloud Dataflow can perform real-time transformation and anomaly detection before loading into BigQuery, enabling near-real-time analytics that can flag impending vehicle failures and reduce unplanned downtime. BigQuery as the analytical data warehouse combined with Data Studio (now Looker Studio) provides fast, scalable reporting and dashboards for fleet health monitoring, which directly supports proactive maintenance.
optionExplanations.B: Uploading gzip files manually via gcloud is a batch-oriented, high-latency approach that does not provide the near-real-time visibility needed to predict and prevent unplanned downtime; it introduces delays between data generation and analysis that are unacceptable for proactive maintenance use cases.
optionExplanations.C: Using Cloud Dataproc Hive as the warehouse adds unnecessary operational overhead (managing a Hadoop cluster) compared to a fully managed BigQuery warehouse, and the batch gzip upload process again introduces latency that prevents timely detection of vehicle issues.
optionExplanations.D: Streaming directly into Hive tables on Dataproc requires managing and scaling a Hadoop cluster yourself, which is more operationally complex and less cost-effective than a serverless streaming pipeline into BigQuery, and Pig scripts are a legacy, less efficient analysis tool compared to BigQuery SQL and Data Studio.

text: For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. You are asked to design a new architecture for the ingestion of the data of the 200,000 vehicles that are connected to a cellular network. You want to follow Google-recommended practices. Considering the technical requirements, which components should you use for the ingestion of the data?

options.text: Google Kubernetes Engine with an SSL Ingress
Cloud IoT Core with public/private key pairs
Compute Engine with project-wide SSH keys
Compute Engine with specific SSH keys

optionExplanations.A: GKE with an SSL Ingress is designed for serving containerized applications and APIs to end users, not for securely ingesting telemetry from hundreds of thousands of distributed cellular-connected devices; it lacks the device management, authentication, and protocol support (MQTT/HTTP bridge) tailored for IoT fleets.
optionExplanations.B: Cloud IoT Core (Google's managed IoT device connection and management service) is purpose-built for securely connecting, managing, and ingesting data from large fleets of devices using per-device public/private key pair authentication (JWT-based), which is the Google-recommended pattern for this exact use case of ingesting data from 200,000 connected vehicles. Note that Cloud IoT Core was deprecated by Google in 2023, but at the time this question was written it represented the recommended architecture for IoT device ingestion.
optionExplanations.C: Compute Engine with project-wide SSH keys is a poor fit for device-to-cloud telemetry ingestion and is also a security anti-pattern, since project-wide SSH keys grant broad access to all VMs in a project rather than scoping credentials per device.
optionExplanations.D: Compute Engine with specific SSH keys still does not address the core requirement of scalable, secure device ingestion for 200,000 vehicles; SSH access is meant for administrative VM access, not for telemetry data ingestion from IoT devices, and managing per-vehicle SSH keys at this scale is operationally unmanageable.

text: For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. You start to build a new application that uses a few Cloud Functions for the backend. One use case requires a Cloud Function func_display to invoke another Cloud Function func_query. You want func_query only to accept invocations from func_display. You also want to follow Google's recommended best practices. What should you do?

options.text: Create a token and pass it in as an environment variable to func_display. When invoking func_query, include the token in the request. Pass the same token to func_query and reject the invocation if the tokens are different.

Make func_query 'Require authentication.' Create a unique service account and associate it to func_display. Grant the service account invoker role for func_query. Create an id token in func_display and include the token to the request when invoking func_query.

Make func_query 'Require authentication' and only accept internal traffic. Create those two functions in the same VPC. Create an ingress firewall rule for func_query to only allow traffic from func_display.

Create those two functions in the same project and VPC. Make func_query only accept internal traffic. Create an ingress firewall for func_query to only allow traffic from func_display. Also, make sure both functions use the same service account.

optionExplanations.A: Manually managing and comparing custom tokens is a fragile, homemade authentication scheme that does not leverage Google Cloud's built-in IAM-based authentication for Cloud Functions, and is explicitly discouraged compared to using service accounts and ID tokens.
optionExplanations.B: Setting func_query to 'Require authentication,' assigning a dedicated service account to func_display, granting that service account the Cloud Functions Invoker role on func_query, and having func_display generate a Google-signed OIDC ID token to include in its request is the Google-recommended pattern for secure function-to-function (or service-to-service) authentication, ensuring only func_display can successfully invoke func_query.
optionExplanations.C: Relying on VPC-based ingress firewall rules to restrict traffic to internal sources does not provide caller identity verification; firewall rules control network paths but cannot authenticate which specific function or service account is making the call, so this does not meet the requirement that only func_display can invoke func_query.
optionExplanations.D: Using a shared service account for both functions and relying solely on network-level (firewall/VPC) restrictions does not provide proper identity-based authorization between the two functions and violates the principle of least privilege by giving both functions identical permissions.

text: For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. You have broken down a legacy monolithic application into a few containerized RESTful microservices. You want to run those microservices on Cloud Run. You also want to make sure the services are highly available with low latency to your customers. What should you do?

options.text: Deploy Cloud Run services to multiple availability zones. Create Cloud Endpoints that point to the services. Create a global HTTP(S) Load Balancing instance and attach the Cloud Endpoints to its backend.

Deploy Cloud Run services to multiple regions. Create serverless network endpoint groups pointing to the services. Add the serverless NEGs to a backend service that is used by a global HTTP(S) Load Balancing instance.
Deploy Cloud Run services to multiple regions. In Cloud DNS, create a latency-based DNS name that points to the services.
Deploy Cloud Run services to multiple availability zones. Create a TCP/IP global load balancer. Add the Cloud Run Endpoints to its backend service.

optionExplanations.A: Cloud Run is a regional serverless service, and 'availability zones' is not the correct unit of deployment for achieving multi-region high availability; Cloud Endpoints is an API management gateway, not a load-balancing mechanism for distributing traffic across regions to minimize latency.
optionExplanations.B: Deploying Cloud Run to multiple regions and fronting them with serverless Network Endpoint Groups (NEGs) behind a global external HTTP(S) Load Balancer is indeed a valid, Google-recommended pattern for highly available, low-latency Cloud Run deployments, routing users to the nearest healthy region.
optionExplanations.C: Latency-based DNS routing via Cloud DNS provides only basic geo-routing without health checking or automatic failover at the load-balancer level, and DNS-based routing has caching/TTL delays that make it less responsive to outages compared to a global HTTP(S) load balancer with serverless NEGs; this is the better-fit, simpler, and more 'cloud-native' answer for Cloud Run multi-region setups described as the recommended approach.
optionExplanations.D: A TCP/IP (Network) global load balancer does not support Cloud Run as a backend target; Cloud Run requires an HTTP(S) Load Balancer with serverless NEGs, and 'availability zones' is again the wrong unit of redundancy for a regional serverless product like Cloud Run.

text: For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. You are migrating a Linux-based application from your private data center to Google Cloud. The TerramEarth security team sent you several recent Linux vulnerabilities published by Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE). You need assistance in understanding how these vulnerabilities could impact your migration. What should you do? (Choose two.)

options.text: Open a support case regarding the CVE and chat with the support engineer.
Read the CVEs from the Google Cloud Status Dashboard to understand the impact.
Read the CVEs from the Google Cloud Platform Security Bulletins to understand the impact.
Post a question regarding the CVE in Stack Overflow to get an explanation.
Post a question regarding the CVE in a Google Cloud discussion group to get an explanation.

optionExplanations.A: Opening a support case with Google Cloud Support and discussing the CVE with a support engineer is an appropriate, official channel to get authoritative guidance on how a specific vulnerability impacts Google Cloud services and the customer's migration plans.
optionExplanations.B: The Google Cloud Status Dashboard reports the operational status (uptime/incidents) of Google Cloud services, not security vulnerability disclosures or CVE impact analyses, so it would not provide the needed information.
optionExplanations.C: The Google Cloud Platform Security Bulletins page is the official source where Google publishes information about how CVEs affect GCP infrastructure and services, making it a correct and authoritative resource for understanding CVE impact.
optionExplanations.D: Stack Overflow is a community Q&A site with no official affiliation to Google Cloud and cannot provide authoritative or guaranteed-accurate information about how a specific CVE impacts Google Cloud's infrastructure or a customer's environment.
optionExplanations.E: A Google Cloud discussion group (community forum) is not an official or authoritative channel for vulnerability impact assessments and may not provide reliable or timely guidance for security-sensitive CVE analysis.

text: For this question, refer to the TerramEarth case study. TerramEarth has a legacy web application that you cannot migrate to cloud. However, you still want to build a cloud-native way to monitor the application. If the application goes down, you want the URL to point to a "Site is unavailable" page as soon as possible. You also want your Ops team to receive a notification for the issue. You need to build a reliable solution for minimum cost. What should you do?

options.text: Create a scheduled job in Cloud Run to invoke a container every minute. The container will check the application URL. If the application is down, switch the URL to the "Site is unavailable" page, and notify the Ops team.

Create a cron job on a Compute Engine VM that runs every minute. The cron job invokes a Python program to check the application URL. If the application is down, switch the URL to the "Site is unavailable" page, and notify the Ops team.

Create a Cloud Monitoring uptime check to validate the application URL. If it fails, put a message in a Pub/Sub queue that triggers a Cloud Function to switch the URL to the "Site is unavailable" page, and notify the Ops team.
Use Cloud Error Reporting to check the application URL. If the application is down, switch the URL to the "Site is unavailable" page, and notify the Ops team.

optionExplanations.A: A scheduled (cron-style) job in Cloud Run that runs a container every minute to perform an HTTP health check, swap the DNS/URL target on failure, and send a notification is a fully managed, serverless, pay-per-use solution (using Cloud Scheduler to trigger Cloud Run), making it the lowest-cost, cloud-native option that meets the reliability and minimal-cost requirements.
optionExplanations.B: Running a cron job on a persistent Compute Engine VM incurs continuous compute costs for a VM that is idle most of the time (only doing work once per minute), which is more expensive and less cloud-native than a serverless scheduled solution.
